- **Waste Generation Rate (WGR)** — unit: **kg waste/kg product**
- **Benefit-Cost Ratio (BCR)** — dimensionless
- **Return on Investment (ROI)** — expressed as **%**
- **Levelized Cost of Energy (LCOE)** — unit: **₹/kWh** or **$/MWh**
- **Environmental Cost Ratio (ECR)** — unit: **₹/unit produced**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Seven Components — Detailed Analysis

### Component 1: Green Materials
- **Why?** Material selection accounts for **>70%** of a product's environmental footprint.
- **How?** Apply the **material hierarchy**: Recycled > Renewable > Recyclable > Virgin.
- **Key Metrics:** % recycled content, embodied energy (MJ/kg), material toxicity index.

### Component 2: Cleaner Production Processes
- **Why?** Traditional processes waste **20–50%** of input materials as scrap.
- **How?** Deploy **near-net-shape forming**, **dry machining**, **additive manufacturing**, and **closed-loop process fluids**.
- **Key Metrics:** Material Utilization Index (MUI), Process Yield.

### Component 3: Sustainable Energy Systems
- **Why?** Manufacturing consumes **~33%** of global final energy.
- **How?** Combine **energy efficiency audits** with **renewable integration** (solar, wind, biomass).
- **Key Metrics:** SEC, renewable energy share (%).

### Component 4: Waste Minimization & Management
- **Why?** Unmanaged waste generates **double costs** — disposal + liability.
- **How?** Implement the **3R Hierarchy** and **industrial symbiosis** (waste-to-feedstock).
- **Key Metrics:** Waste-to-Product ratio, % landfill diversion.

### Component 5: Green Design (Design for Environment — DfE)
- **Why?** **80%** of a product's environmental impact is locked-in at the design stage.
- **How?** Apply **DfE guidelines**: DfRecycle, DfDisassemble, DfRemanufacture, DfEnergy.
- **Key Metrics:** Disassembly Time, Recyclability Index.

### Component 6: Eco-friendly Packaging & Logistics
- **Why?** Packaging contributes **~30%** of municipal solid waste globally.
- **How?** Use **biodegradable materials**, **right-size packaging**, and **route optimization**.
- **Key Metrics:** Packaging-to-Product weight ratio, transport emissions (g CO₂e/tonne-km).

### Component 7: End-of-Life Management
- **Why?** Linear "take-make-dispose" model depletes resources and accumulates waste.
- **How?** Adopt **EPR (Extended Producer Responsibility)**, **take-back schemes**, and **circular economy** design.
- **Key Metrics:** % product recovery, % remanufactured output.

---

## 2.2 Cost Categories — Detailed Analysis

### A. Conventional Manufacturing Costs
Standard accounting categories:
- **Direct Material Cost (DMC)**
- **Direct Labor Cost (DLC)**
- **Manufacturing Overhead (MOH)**

### B. Environmental Costs (Internalization of Externalities)
Sub-divided into:

| Type | Description | Example |
|------|-------------|---------|
| **Prevention Costs** | Costs to prevent pollution at source | R&D for cleaner process, employee training |
| **Detection Costs** | Costs to verify compliance | Environmental audits, ISO 14001 certification |
| **Internal Failure Costs** | Costs of defects found internally | Scrap, rework, waste disposal fees |
| **External Failure Costs** | Costs of defects reaching environment | Fines, cleanup, reputational damage, health claims |

### C. Hidden Costs
- **Regulatory risk costs** — future compliance costs
- **Resource depletion costs** — implicit cost of non-renewable use
- **Social license costs** — community opposition, brand erosion
- **Stranded asset costs** — premature obsolescence of polluting equipment

### D. Lifecycle Costs (LCC)
A **total cost perspective** spanning:
1. **Acquisition / Design costs**
2. **Production / Operating costs**
3. **Maintenance costs**
4. **End-of-life / Disposal costs**
5. **Residual / Salvage value**

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $TC = DMC + DLC + MOH$ | Total Conventional Cost | ₹ |
| 2 | $TEC = C_{prev} + C_{det} + C_{int} + C_{ext}$ | Total Environmental Cost (PAAF Model) | ₹ |
| 3 | $LCC = C_{acq} + C_{op} + C_{maint} + C_{dis} - S$ | Lifecycle Cost (S = Salvage) | ₹ |
| 4 | $BCR = \dfrac{\sum PV(\text{Benefits})}{\sum PV(\text{Costs})}$ | Benefit-Cost Ratio (PV = Present Value) | dimensionless |
| 5 | $ROI = \dfrac{NR}{I} \times 100\%$ | Return on Investment (NR=Net Return, I=Investment) | % |
| 6 | $NPV = \sum_{t=0}^{n} \dfrac{CF_t}{(1+r)^t}$ | Net Present Value (CF=Cash Flow, r=Discount Rate) | ₹ |
| 7 | $PP = \dfrac{I}{AAR}$ | Payback Period (AAR=Avg Annual Return) | years |
| 8 | $SEC = \dfrac{E_{total}}{M_{product}}$ | Specific Energy Consumption | kWh/kg |
| 9 | $WGR = \dfrac{M_{waste}}{M_{product}}$ | Waste Generation Ratio | kg/kg |
| 10 | $ECR = \dfrac{TEC}{Q_{produced}}$ | Environmental Cost Ratio | ₹/unit |
| 11 | $MUI = \dfrac{M_{final}}{M_{input}} \times 100\%$ | Material Utilization Index | % |
| 12 | $CO_2^{total} = \sum_{i} (A_i \times EF_i)$ | Total CO₂e (Activity × Emission Factor) | kg CO₂e |

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Tip:** Memorize the **PAAF model** (Prevention–Appraisal/Detection–Internal Failure–External Failure). It is the most frequently tested cost framework in KTU ESE questions on this topic.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **LCC** is used in **capital-intensive industries** (aerospace, automotive, power plants) for technology selection.
- **BCR > 1** is the standard threshold for **green project approval** in most public-sector funding agencies (World Bank, ADB).
- **PAAF model** is the backbone of **ISO 14001** environmental management accounting.
- **SEC** is a **Tier-1 KPI** in cement, steel, aluminium, and pulp & paper industries.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Net Present Value (NPV) for Green Investment

**Problem Setup:** A company invests in a green manufacturing line.
- Initial Investment: $I = ₹50,00,000$
- Annual Cash Inflow (savings + revenue): $CF = ₹15,00,000$ per year
- Project life: $n = 5$ years
- Discount Rate: $r = 10\%$

**Step 1 — Write the NPV equation:**

$$
NPV = \sum_{t=0}^{n} \frac{CF_t}{(1+r)^t}
$$

**Step 2 — Expand the summation for $t = 0$ to $5$:**

$$
NPV = \frac{CF_0}{(1+r)^0} + \frac{CF_1}{(1+r)^1} + \frac{CF_2}{(1+r)^2} + \frac{CF_3}{(1+r)^3} + \frac{CF_4}{(1+r)^4} + \frac{CF_5}{(1+r)^5}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the values** ($CF_0 = -50,00,000$ for initial outflow, and $CF_{1..5} = +15,00,000$):

$$
NPV = \frac{-50,00,000}{(1.10)^0} + \frac{15,00,000}{(1.10)^1} + \frac{15,00,000}{(1.10)^2} + \frac{15,00,000}{(1.10)^3} + \frac{15,00,000}{(1.10)^4} + \frac{15,00,000}{(1.10)^5}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute each discount factor:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
(1.10)^0 &= 1.0000 \\
(1.10)^1 &= 1.1000 \\
(1.10)^2 &= 1.2100 \\
(1.10)^3 &= 1.3310 \\
(1.10)^4 &= 1.4641 \\
(1.10)^5 &= 1.6105
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute each discounted cash flow:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
PV_0 &= \frac{-50,00,000}{1.0000} = -50,00,000.00 \\
PV_1 &= \frac{15,00,000}{1.1000} = 13,63,636.36 \\
PV_2 &= \frac{15,00,000}{1.2100} = 12,39,669.42 \\
PV_3 &= \frac{15,00,000}{1.3310} = 11,27,032.20 \\
PV_4 &= \frac{15,00,000}{1.4641} = 10,24,547.46 \\
PV_5 &= \frac{15,00,000}{1.6105} = 9,31,406.78
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Sum the discounted cash flows:**

$$
NPV = -50,00,000 + 13,63,636.36 + 12,39,669.42 + 11,27,032.20 + 10,24,547.46 + 9,31,406.78
$$

$$
NPV = -50,00,000 + 56,86,292.22
$$

$$
\boxed{NPV = +₹6,86,292.22}
$$

**Step 7 — Decision Rule:**
Since $NPV > 0$, the green investment is **economically viable** and should be accepted.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Benefit-Cost Ratio (BCR) for the Same Project

**Step 1 — Sum of present value of benefits (cash inflows):**

$$
\sum PV(\text{Benefits}) = 13,63,636.36 + 12,39,669.42 + 11,27,032.20 + 10,24,547.46 + 9,31,406.78
$$

$$
\sum PV(\text{Benefits}) = ₹56,86,292.22
$$

**Step 2 — Sum of present value of costs (initial outflow):**

$$
\sum PV(\text{Costs}) = ₹50,00,000.00
$$

**Step 3 — Apply BCR formula:**

$$
BCR = \frac{\sum PV(\text{Benefits})}{\sum PV(\text{Costs})} = \frac{56,86,292.22}{50,00,000.00}
$$

$$
\boxed{BCR = 1.137}
$$

**Step 4 — Decision Rule:**
Since $BCR > 1$, the project is **acceptable**. Each ₹1 invested returns ₹1.137 in present-value benefits.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Payback Period (PP)

**Step 1 — Compute cumulative cash flows year by year:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Year 1 Cumulative} &= 15,00,000 \\
\text{Year 2 Cumulative} &= 30,00,000 \\
\text{Year 3 Cumulative} &= 45,00,000 \\
\text{Year 4 Cumulative} &= 60,00,000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Identify the year of payback:**

The investment of ₹50,00,000 is recovered between Year 3 (₹45,00,000) and Year 4 (₹60,00,000).

**Step 3 — Apply interpolation formula:**

$$
PP = \text{Full Years Before Recovery} + \frac{\text{Unrecovered at Start of Year}}{\text{Cash Flow in Recovery Year}}
$$

$$
PP = 3 + \frac{50,00,000 - 45,00,000}{15,00,000}
$$

$$
PP = 3 + \frac{5,00,000}{15,00,000}
$$

$$
PP = 3 + 0.333
$$

$$
\boxed{PP = 3.33 \text{ years}}
$$

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Total Environmental Cost using PAAF Model

**Given data for a manufacturing unit per annum:**

| Cost Type | Amount (₹) |
|-----------|-------------|
| Prevention ($C_{prev}$) | 2,00,000 |
| Detection ($C_{det}$) | 1,50,000 |
| Internal Failure ($C_{int}$) | 3,00,000 |
| External Failure ($C_{ext}$) | 5,00,000 |

**Step 1 — Apply the PAAF formula:**

$$
TEC = C_{prev} + C_{det} + C_{int} + C_{ext}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute values:**

$$
TEC = 2,00,000 + 1,50,000 + 3,00,000 + 5,00,000
$$

$$
\boxed{TEC = ₹11,50,000 \text{ per annum}}
$$

**Step 3 — Interpretation:** External failure costs (₹5,00,000) are the **highest** component, indicating that the firm is *"paying too late."* Investment in **prevention** typically shifts this balance toward lower total environmental cost.

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5: Environmental Cost Ratio (ECR) per Unit

**Given:** $TEC = ₹11,50,000$; Annual production $Q = 50,000$ units.

**Step 1 — Apply the ECR formula:**

$$
ECR = \frac{TEC}{Q}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute:**

$$
ECR = \frac{11,50,000}{50,000}
$$

$$
\boxed{ECR = ₹23 \text{ per unit}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Significance:** ECR = ₹23/unit means that for every unit produced, the firm incurs an additional ₹23 in environmental costs. This metric is critical for **eco-labeling** and **green procurement** decisions.

---

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix: Cost Type vs. Time of Occurrence

| Cost Type | When Incurred | Visibility in Accounts | Example |
|-----------|---------------|------------------------|---------|
| Prevention | Before pollution | High | Eco-design R&D |
| Detection | Before pollution | High | Monitoring systems |
| Internal Failure | During production | High | Scrap, rework |
| External Failure | After production | Low (often hidden) | Fines, lawsuits |
| Lifecycle | Throughout | Mixed | Maintenance, disposal |
| Hidden | Future / latent | Very low | Stranded assets |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
