<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1: Define the PAAF model of environmental costing. List its four components.**
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> The **PAAF model** (Prevention–Appraisal/Detection–Internal Failure–External Failure) is an environmental cost classification framework derived from quality cost management, adapted by the UN Division for Sustainable Development for environmental accounting.

**Four Components:**

1. **Prevention Costs** — Expenditure to *prevent* pollution at source. *Examples:* Cleaner process R&D, eco-design, employee environmental training, supplier audits.

2. **Appraisal / Detection Costs** — Expenditure to *detect and measure* environmental performance. *Examples:* Environmental audits, ISO 14001 certification, emission monitoring systems, environmental testing labs.

3. **Internal Failure Costs** — Costs incurred when pollution is *detected within the organization* before release. *Examples:* Scrap from defective process, waste disposal fees, effluent treatment, soil contamination within plant boundary.

4. **External Failure Costs** — Costs incurred when pollution reaches the *external environment* and stakeholders. *Examples:* Fines, lawsuits, community health claims, brand reputation damage, government-mandated cleanup, compensation.

> **Examiner's Note:** Full marks require *one example* for each category. Generic definitions alone are insufficient.

---

### **Question 2: Differentiate between Conventional Cost and Environmental Cost in green manufacturing.**
**[KTU University Exam — June 2024]**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Conventional Cost | Environmental Cost |
|-----------|-------------------|---------------------|
| **Definition** | Costs directly tied to production output | Costs arising from environmental impacts of production |
| **Scope** | Confined to factory floor | Spans cradle-to-grave lifecycle |
| **Examples** | Material, labor, overhead | Pollution control, fines, waste treatment, remediation |
| **Accountability** | Operations/Finance | EHS (Environment, Health, Safety) + Finance |
| **Time horizon** | Short-term (per accounting period) | Often long-term (liabilities may accrue over decades) |
| **Visibility** | Always on financial statements | Often *hidden* or off-balance sheet |
| **Decision driver** | Profit maximization | Risk minimization + sustainable value |

> **Marks Allocation (3 marks):** Definition (1) + Tabular comparison with at least 4 parameters (1.5) + Real-world example (0.5).

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Internal Choice Format (KTU 2024 Standard):** Answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — COMPONENTS FOCUS**

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the **seven major components of green manufacturing**. Discuss the interrelationship between **Green Design (DfE)** and **End-of-Life Management** with a suitable industrial example. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss in detail the role of **Cleaner Production Processes** and **Sustainable Energy Systems** as twin pillars of green manufacturing. Illustrate with **two real-world industrial case studies** (one from each pillar). **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution to Question A:**

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:**

**Block Diagram** (1.5 marks):

```
[Reference: Diagram 4.1 from Section 4]
Seven components: Green Materials, Cleaner Production,
Sustainable Energy, Waste Minimization, Green Design,
Eco Packaging, End-of-Life Management.
```

**Explanation of each component (3 marks):**
- **Green Materials:** Selection of renewable, recycled, biodegradable inputs; reduces embodied energy and toxicity.
- **Cleaner Production Processes:** Adoption of dry machining, near-net-shape forming, additive manufacturing; reduces waste and emissions.
- **Sustainable Energy Systems:** Use of solar, wind, biomass, and energy-efficient equipment; reduces carbon footprint.
- **Waste Minimization & Management:** 3R hierarchy (Reduce, Reuse, Recycle); industrial symbiosis where one firm's waste becomes another's feedstock.
- **Green Design (DfE):** Design decisions that minimize environmental impact across lifecycle; e.g., DfRecycle, DfDisassemble.
- **Eco-friendly Packaging & Logistics:** Biodegradable packaging, optimized routes; reduces transport emissions and packaging waste.
- **End-of-Life Management:** Take-back schemes, remanufacturing, safe disposal; closes the materials loop.

**Interrelationship DfE ↔ EoL (2 marks):**
Green Design **decides** how easily a product can be recovered, recycled, or remanufactured at End-of-Life. Example: **Apple's iPhone disassembly design** — the iPhone 14 was redesigned with a single-piece back glass and modular components to enable easier disassembly for recycling. The DfE choices (e.g., snap-fit joints, standardized screws, labeled plastic parts) **directly enable** the EoL process. This is a closed-loop feedback: EoL data feeds back into DfE revisions.

**Industrial Example (0.5 mark):** Xerox's "Green World Alliance" — DfRemanufacture design choices allow >70% component recovery in copiers.

---

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:**

**Cleaner Production Processes (3 marks):**
- Definition: Continuous application of preventive environmental strategies to processes, products, and services.
- Techniques: input substitution, process modification, equipment redesign, product reformulation, housekeeping.
- Case Study: **Toyota Production System (TPS)** — elimination of *muda* (waste), *mura* (unevenness), *muri* (overburden). Achieved **30% reduction** in paint solvent emissions through water-based paints and VOC recovery systems.

**Sustainable Energy Systems (3 marks):**
- Definition: Energy sources and systems that are renewable, low-carbon, and efficient.
- Techniques: cogeneration (CHP), waste heat recovery, solar PV, biomass, energy-efficient motors (IE3/IE4 class).
- Case Study: **Tata Steel's Jamshedpur plant** — installation of a 5 MW waste heat recovery system reduced specific energy consumption from **6.8 GCal/t to 5.9 GCal/t** of crude steel, saving ₹120 crore/year.

**Conclusion (1 mark):** Both pillars work in **synergy** — cleaner processes often consume less energy, and renewable energy powers cleaner processes. Together, they form the **operational backbone** of green manufacturing.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — COSTS FOCUS**

**(a)** Explain the **four categories of environmental costs** using the PAAF model. How does increasing **prevention cost** affect the total environmental cost? Justify with a graphical and mathematical argument. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A manufacturing firm is evaluating a green investment. The **initial investment** is ₹50 lakh. The project yields **annual cash inflows of ₹15 lakh for 5 years**. The **discount rate is 10%**. Compute the **NPV, BCR, and Payback Period**, and recommend whether the project should be accepted. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution to Question B:**

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:**

**PAAF Model Explanation (3 marks):** Already covered in Q1 of Part A (Prevention, Detection, Internal Failure, External Failure). Expand with one example per category.

**Graphical argument (2 marks):**

```
Cost
  |     External Failure (downward curve as prevention rises)
  |     /
  |    /
  |   /          Internal Failure (also decreasing)
  |  /         /
  | /         /
  |/_________/________________ Prevention Cost
  ↑         ↑
  Low       High
```

- As prevention cost **increases**, internal and external failure costs **decrease** (often exponentially).
- A U-shaped curve emerges for **Total Environmental Cost (TEC)** with an **optimum point**.
- At very low prevention → high failure → high TEC.
- At very high prevention → low failure but high prevention cost → high TEC.
- **Optimum** lies at moderate prevention.

**Mathematical argument (1.5 marks):**

$$
TEC = C_{prev} + C_{det} + C_{int}(C_{prev}) + C_{ext}(C_{prev})
$$

Assuming:
- $C_{int} = k_1 / C_{prev}$
- $C_{ext} = k_2 / C_{prev}^2$

$$
\frac{d(TEC)}{dC_{prev}} = 1 - \frac{k_1}{C_{prev}^2} - \frac{2k_2}{C_{prev}^3} = 0
$$

This yields the **optimal prevention cost** $C_{prev}^*$. (1.5 marks)

**Conclusion (0.5 mark):** Prevention cost is a **strategic investment** — spending more on prevention reduces far greater failure costs, but with diminishing returns.

---

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:**

**Given:**
- $I = ₹50,00,000$
- $CF = ₹15,00,000$ per annum
- $n = 5$ years
- $r = 10\%$

**Step 1: NPV Calculation (3 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
NPV &= -50,00,000 + \frac{15,00,000}{1.10} + \frac{15,00,000}{(1.10)^2} + \frac{15,00,000}{(1.10)^3} \\
&\quad + \frac{15,00,000}{(1.10)^4} + \frac{15,00,000}{(1.10)^5}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
NPV = -50,00,000 + (13,63,636 + 12,39,669 + 11,27,032 + 10,24,547 + 9,31,407)
$$

$$
NPV = -50,00,000 + 56,86,291
$$

$$
\boxed{NPV = +₹6,86,291}
$$

**Step 2: BCR Calculation (2 marks):**

$$
BCR = \frac{56,86,291}{50,00,000} = \boxed{1.137}
$$

**Step 3: Payback Period (1.5 marks):**

Cumulative cash flow reaches ₹50,00,000 between Year 3 (₹45,00,000) and Year 4 (₹60,00,000).

$$
PP = 3 + \frac{50,00,000 - 45,00,000}{15,00,000} = 3 + 0.333 = \boxed{3.33 \text{ years}}
$$

**Step 4: Recommendation (0.5 mark):**

Since $NPV > 0$, $BCR > 1$, and $PP = 3.33$ years (acceptable for most industries), the **project should be ACCEPTED**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Warning:**
> - **Do NOT** confuse **Payback Period** (which uses undiscounted cash flows) with **NPV** (which uses discounted cash flows). Many students mix them up and lose 2–3 marks.
> - **Always** show the **decision rule** explicitly (e.g., "Since NPV > 0, accept"). A correct numerical answer without a decision statement fetches only partial credit.
> - **Discount factors must be shown to 4 decimal places** in the working. Rounding errors at intermediate steps cost marks.
> - **For PAAF questions**, students frequently forget to provide an **example** for each of the four cost categories. Each missing example = 0.5 mark deduction.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Components and Costs**

### **Components (7 Pillars) — Quick Recall:**
1. **G**reen **M**aterials (renewable, recycled, non-toxic)
2. **C**leaner **P**roduction **P**rocesses (low-waste, low-emission)
3. **S**ustainable **E**nergy **S**ystems (renewables + efficiency)
4. **W**aste **M**inimization & **M**anagement (3R + industrial symbiosis)
5. **G**reen **D**esign / **D**f**E** (locked-in impact at design stage)
6. **E**co-friendly **P**ackaging & **L**ogistics (route + material optimization)
7. **E**nd-of-**L**ife **M**anagement (EPR, take-back, remanufacturing)

### **Cost Categories — Quick Recall:**
- **Conventional:** DMC + DLC + MOH
- **Environmental (PAAF):** Prevention + Detection + Internal Failure + External Failure
- **Hidden:** Regulatory risk + stranded assets + brand erosion
- **Lifecycle (LCC):** Acquisition + Operating + Maintenance + Disposal − Salvage

### **Key Formulas — Quick Recall:**
- $NPV = \sum \dfrac{CF_t}{(1+r)^t}$ → **Accept if NPV > 0**
- $BCR = \dfrac{\sum PV(\text{Benefits})}{\sum PV(\text{Costs})}$ → **Accept if BCR > 1**
- $PP = \dfrac{I}{AAR}$ → **Accept if PP < cutoff**
- $TEC = C_{prev} + C_{det} + C_{int} + C_{ext}$
- $LCC = C_{acq} + C_{op} + C_{maint} + C_{dis} - S$

### **Golden Rules for KTU Exam:**
- **Always** present a **labeled block diagram** for component questions.
- **Always** state the **decision rule** for cost-evaluation questions.
- **Always** show **discount factors to 4 decimal places**.
- **Always** provide **at least one example** per theoretical category.
- **Remember:** Green manufacturing is *not* about higher costs — it is about **cost-shifting across the lifecycle** and **internalizing externalities**.

> [!TIP]
> **One-Line Mantra for Viva/Exam:** *"Green manufacturing components are the 'what' of sustainability; green manufacturing costs are the 'how much' — and the seven pillars + four cost categories together form the strategic framework for cradle-to-cradle production."*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Need and Control
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET753 - GREEN MANUFACTURING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Need and Control

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Green Manufacturing**, the term **"Need and Control"** refers to the systematic identification, justification, and implementation of environmental control mechanisms across the entire manufacturing value chain. It encapsulates two interlinked ideas:

- **Need:** The techno-economic-environmental drivers (regulatory, market, social, and ecological) that compel a manufacturing enterprise to adopt green practices.
- **Control:** The set of managerial, engineering, regulatory, and technological interventions used to measure, regulate, and minimize the negative environmental footprint of manufacturing operations.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** *Need and Control* addresses the rationale for proactive environmental stewardship and the hierarchy of control strategies — ranging from **pollution prevention at source** to **end-of-pipe treatment** — governed by standards like **ISO 14001:2015** and tools like **Life Cycle Assessment (LCA)** and **Environmental Management Systems (EMS)**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a manufacturing plant as a **human body**:

- The **need** for green manufacturing is like the body's need to stay healthy — driven by diet (regulations), fitness goals (market competitiveness), and avoiding disease (environmental damage).
- The **control** mechanisms are like the body's immune system and kidneys — they filter toxins (emissions), regulate temperature (energy use), and ensure waste is processed efficiently (recycling, treatment).

Just as a doctor first advises **prevention** (healthy lifestyle) before prescribing **treatment** (medicine), green manufacturing prioritizes **source reduction** before **end-of-pipe control**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Modern green manufacturing follows the **Pollution Prevention Hierarchy (P2 Hierarchy)** — Eliminate $\rightarrow$ Reduce $\rightarrow$ Reuse $\rightarrow$ Recycle $\rightarrow$ Treat $\rightarrow$ Dispose. Prevention is always preferred over control.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics and Constants

The following standard metrics are universally used to quantify "need" and measure "control" in green manufacturing:

- **Carbon Footprint (CF):** Measured in **kg CO₂-equivalent (kg CO₂e)** per functional unit.
- **Eco-Efficiency Indicator (EEI):** Ratio of product/service value to environmental influence.
- **Pollution Control Efficiency ($\eta$):** Expressed as a **percentage (%)**.
- **Specific Energy Consumption (SEC):** Measured in **kWh/ton** or **MJ/kg** of output.
- **Water Footprint (WF):** Measured in **litres/kg** of product.

> [!TIP]
> KTU examiners frequently test whether students can **distinguish between Need (drivers) and Control (interventions)**. A common trap is conflating the two in long-answer questions.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The "Need" for Green Manufacturing Control

The need for environmental control in manufacturing arises from four primary drivers, often called the **"Four Pillars of Need"**:

#### 2.1.1 Regulatory Driver
- Compliance with national and international laws (e.g., **Environment Protection Act, 1986** in India; **Clean Air Act** & **Clean Water Act** in the USA; **REACH** and **RoHS** in the EU).
- Penalties for non-compliance can reach **millions of dollars** and include imprisonment.

#### 2.1.2 Market Driver
- **Green consumerism:** 73% of global consumers (per a 2024 McKinsey survey) prefer sustainable brands.
- **Eco-labeling and green procurement** by governments and MNCs.
- Access to **export markets** requires ISO 14001 certification.

#### 2.1.3 Social/ Ethical Driver
- Corporate Social Responsibility (**CSR**) under Indian Companies Act, 2013 — **Section 135**.
- Community pressure, NGO activism, and ESG (Environmental, Social, Governance) reporting.

#### 2.1.4 Ecological Driver
- Resource depletion: many **Rare Earth Elements (REEs)** have less than **50 years** of reserves at current consumption rates.
- Climate change mitigation: limiting global warming to **1.5°C above pre-industrial levels** (Paris Agreement).

### 2.2 The "Control" Mechanisms in Green Manufacturing

Control is implemented via a **multi-layered hierarchy**:

| **Layer** | **Control Type** | **Description** | **Example** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **L1** | Source Reduction | Eliminate hazard at design stage | Substitute toxic solvent with water-based agent |
| **L2** | In-process Control | Real-time monitoring and feedback | Closed-loop coolant recycling in CNC machining |
| **L3** | End-of-Pipe Treatment | Capture pollutants before discharge | Electrostatic precipitator, scrubber, biofilter |
| **L4** | Disposal Management | Safe handling of residual waste | Secured landfill, incineration with energy recovery |
| **L5** | Management Systems | Policy, audit, and continuous improvement | ISO 14001:2015 EMS, Six Sigma Green Belt |

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Formula** | **Symbol Meaning** | **Unit** | **Application** |
|---|---|---|---|
| $\eta = \frac{C_{in} - C_{out}}{C_{in}} \times 100\%$ | $\eta$: Control Efficiency, $C_{in}$: Inlet Concentration, $C_{out}$: Outlet Concentration | **%** | Efficiency of scrubbers, filters, ESPs |
| $CF = \sum_{i} (A_i \times EF_i \times GWP_i)$ | $CF$: Carbon Footprint, $A_i$: Activity, $EF_i$: Emission Factor, $GWP_i$: Global Warming Potential | **kg CO₂e** | GHG inventory calculation |
| $SEC = \frac{E_{total}}{M_{output}}$ | $SEC$: Specific Energy Consumption, $E_{total}$: Total Energy, $M_{output}$: Mass of Product | **kWh/ton** | Energy benchmarking |
| $EEI = \frac{V_{product}}{E_{influence}}$ | $EEI$: Eco-Efficiency Index, $V$: Product Value, $E$: Environmental Influence | **₹/kg waste** | WBCSD eco-efficiency scoring |
| $R_{recycle} = \frac{M_{recycled}}{M_{total\_waste}} \times 100\%$ | $R_{recycle}$: Recycling Rate, $M$: Mass | **%** | Circular economy KPI |
| $W = \frac{W_{blue} + W_{green} + W_{grey}}{M_{product}}$ | $W$: Water Footprint, subscripts: blue (surface), green (rain), grey (polluted) | **L/kg** | ISO 14046 water footprint |

> [!WARNING]
> KTU examiners **deduct marks** for units written incorrectly. Always pair every numerical answer with its SI unit. Also, never use the vertical pipe $\vert x \vert$ inside markdown tables — use $\lvert x \rvert$ or $\text{abs}(x)$ instead.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

These control frameworks are deployed in:
- **Automotive:** Painting shops use **regenerative thermal oxidizers (RTOs)** with $\eta > 99\%$.
- **Electronics:** Lead-free soldering and **RoHS-compliant** PCB manufacturing.
- **Textile:** Zero Liquid Discharge (**ZLD**) systems recover > **95%** of process water.
- **Cement:** Alternative fuels reduce **CO₂ emissions by 30–40%**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Pollution Control Efficiency of a Wet Scrubber

A wet scrubber is used to remove **SO₂** from flue gas. The **inlet concentration** is $C_{in} = 2000 \text{ mg/m}^3$ and **outlet concentration** is $C_{out} = 200 \text{ mg/m}^3$. Determine the control efficiency.

**Step 1 — Write the governing efficiency equation.**

The control efficiency of any pollution abatement device is defined as the fraction of pollutant removed relative to the inlet load:

$$
\eta = \frac{C_{in} - C_{out}}{C_{in}} \times 100\%
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the numerical values.**

$$
\eta = \frac{2000 - 200}{2000} \times 100\%
$$

**Step 3 — Simplify the numerator.**

$$
\eta = \frac{1800}{2000} \times 100\%
$$

**Step 4 — Perform the division.**

$$
\eta = 0.90 \times 100\% = 90\%
$$

**Interpretation:** The scrubber achieves **90% removal** of SO₂, which is acceptable for most coal-fired boilers under Indian CPCB norms (which mandate $\eta \geq 80\%$ for SO₂ in power plants).

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Eco-Efficiency Index for a Manufacturing Process

A manufacturing unit produces **500 tonnes** of a product per month. The economic value generated is **₹50 lakh/month**, and the environmental impact (waste, emissions, water use monetized) is **₹10 lakh/month**. Calculate the **Eco-Efficiency Index (EEI)**.

**Step 1 — Recall the EEI formula.**

$$
EEI = \frac{V_{product}}{E_{influence}}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the values.**

$$
EEI = \frac{50 \text{ lakh}}{10 \text{ lakh}} = 5
$$

**Step 3 — Interpret.**

An **EEI = 5** means that for every **₹1** of environmental impact, the firm generates **₹5** of product value. Higher EEI indicates **more sustainable** production.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Hierarchy of Pollution Control

The **Pollution Prevention (P2) Hierarchy** is a decision matrix widely used in green manufacturing. The table below expands the logic:

| **Priority** | **Strategy** | **Cost** | **Effectiveness** | **KTU Frequency** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **1 (Highest)** | Elimination / Source Reduction | Low (long-term) | Very High | ★★★★★ |
| **2** | In-process Recycling / Reuse | Medium | High | ★★★★ |
| **3** | End-of-pipe Treatment | High | Medium | ★★★ |
| **4** | Safe Disposal | Very High | Low (post-fact) | ★★ |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** A 14-mark question often asks students to **"Discuss the P2 Hierarchy with examples from manufacturing."** Always justify the ranking using **cost-effectiveness** and **sustainability** arguments, not just definitions.

### 3.4 Comparative Analysis: EMS Frameworks

| **Framework** | **Origin** | **Focus** | **KTU Relevance** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **ISO 14001:2015** | International (ISO) | Generic EMS for any organization | **Most frequently asked** |
| **EMAS** | European Union | EMS + public reporting | Mentioned in elective modules |
| **ISO 14064** | International (ISO) | GHG accounting at organizational level | Carbon neutrality questions |
| **ISO 14046** | International (ISO) | Water footprint quantification | Water management questions |
| **Cradle to Cradle (C2C)** | McDonough & Braungart | Material health + circularity | Disruptive innovation answers |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: Hierarchy of Green Manufacturing Control

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Green Manufacturing Need and Control] --> B[Regulatory Pressure]
    A --> C[Market Demand]
    A --> D[Social Responsibility]
    A --> E[Ecological Urgency]
    A --> F[Control Mechanisms]
    F --> G[Source Reduction]
    F --> H[In-Process Control]
    F --> I[End-of-Pipe Treatment]
    F --> J[Management Systems]
    G --> G1[Material Substitution]
    G --> G2[Design for Environment]
    H --> H1[Closed-Loop Recycling]
    H --> H2[Real-Time Monitoring]
    I --> I1[Scrubbers]
    I --> I2[Electrostatic Precipitators]
    I --> I3[Biofilters]
    J --> J1[ISO 14001 EMS]
    J --> J2[ISO 14064 GHG Accounting]
    J --> J3[Six Sigma Green Belt]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: ISO 14001:2015 PDCA Cycle in Green Manufacturing

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Plan: Environmental Policy and Objectives] --> D1
    D1[Do: Implement Operational Controls] --> C1
    C1[Check: Monitor and Measure Performance] --> A1
    A1[Act: Continual Improvement] --> P1
    P1 -.-> E1[Context of Organization Clause 4]
    P1 -.-> L1[Leadership Clause 5]
    D1 -.-> S1[Support Clause 7]
    C1 -.-> P2[Performance Evaluation Clause 9]
    A1 -.-> I1[Improvement Clause 10]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Functional Architecture of an Integrated Environmental Control System

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    RawMat[Raw Material Input] --> Mfg[Manufacturing Process]
    Mfg --> Mon1[Sensor Layer: Flow, Temp, pH, VOC]
    Mon1 --> DSH[Data Acquisition and SCADA]
    DSH --> EMS[EMS Server ISO 14001 Compliant]
    EMS --> Ana[Analytics and KPI Engine]
    Ana --> R1[Regulatory Reporting]
    Ana --> R2[Internal Audit]
    Ana --> R3[Corrective Action CAPA]
    Mfg --> Emis[Emission Stream]
    Emis --> Trt[End-of-Pipe Treatment]
    Trt --> Rec[Recovered Material or Energy]
    Rec --> Mfg
    Trt --> Dis[Compliant Discharge to Environment]
    Ana --> FB[Feedback to Process Control]
    FB --> Mfg
```

> [!TIP]
> The above block-level diagram is the **model answer sketch** KTU examiners expect for a 14-mark question on *"Integrated environmental control in a smart factory."* Always label **feedback loops** — they are the heart of any EMS.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Define the term "Need and Control" in the context of green manufacturing. List any four drivers of need.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (1 mark):** Need and Control in green manufacturing refers to the identification of environmental drivers (need) and the implementation of pollution prevention and control strategies to minimize the ecological footprint of manufacturing.
- **Four drivers (2 marks — 0.5 each):**
  1. **Regulatory compliance** — Environment Protection Act, 1986; EU REACH.
  2. **Market competitiveness** — green consumer demand and eco-labels.
  3. **Social/CSR pressure** — Section 135 of Companies Act 2013.
  4. **Ecological urgency** — climate change and resource depletion.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*What is meant by the Pollution Prevention (P2) Hierarchy? Arrange its elements in order of priority.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Meaning (1.5 marks):** The P2 Hierarchy is a ranked framework of pollution control strategies, prioritizing prevention over treatment.
- **Order (1.5 marks — 0.25 each):**
  1. **Eliminate** at source.
  2. **Reduce** at source.
  3. **Reuse** internally.
  4. **Recycle** externally.
  5. **Treat** (end-of-pipe).
  6. **Dispose** safely.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

#### Question A — 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**
*(a)* Discuss in detail the **four primary drivers** that create the *need* for green manufacturing control. Provide one real-world example for each driver. **(7 marks)**

*(b)* Explain the **Pollution Prevention (P2) Hierarchy** with manufacturing examples. Justify why **source reduction** is prioritized over **end-of-pipe treatment**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Four Drivers of Need (7 marks):**

1. **Regulatory Driver (1.5 marks):** Laws such as India's Water (Prevention and Control of Pollution) Act, 1974 and the EU's REACH regulation mandate emission limits. Non-compliance attracts penalties, plant shutdowns, and criminal prosecution. *Example:* In 2023, a major Indian steel plant was fined **₹2.86 crore** by the CPCB for exceeding particulate matter limits.

2. **Market Driver (1.5 marks):** Consumer preference for green products and OEM requirements force suppliers to adopt cleaner processes. *Example:* Apple's Supplier Clean Energy Program mandates **100% renewable energy** for iPhone assembly by 2030.

3. **Social/CSR Driver (2 marks):** Under Section 135 of the Indian Companies Act, 2013, companies with profit > ₹5 crore or turnover > ₹1000 crore must spend 2% of profits on CSR activities including environmental projects. *Example:* Tata Motors' afforestation and rainwater harvesting in Jamshedpur.

4. **Ecological Driver (2 marks):** Climate change, ozone depletion, and resource scarcity demand urgent action. *Example:* Phase-out of **CFC refrigerants** under the Montreal Protocol saved the ozone layer — manufacturing had to switch to HFCs and now HFOs.

**(b) P2 Hierarchy with Justification (7 marks):**

The **P2 Hierarchy** ranks pollution control strategies from most preferred to least preferred:

$$
\text{Eliminate} > \text{Reduce} > \text{Reuse} > \text{Recycle} > \text{Treat} > \text{Dispose}
$$

| **Rank** | **Strategy** | **Manufacturing Example** |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Eliminate | Replace hexavalent chromium plating with trivalent chromium or laser-based coating |
| 2 | Reduce | Use low-VOC water-based paints in automotive shop |
| 3 | Reuse | Reuse rinse water in a pre-cleaning tank of a metal finishing line |
| 4 | Recycle | Recycle aluminium swarf into secondary ingots |
| 5 | Treat | Send wastewater through a chemical precipitation plant |
| 6 | Dispose | Landfill of treated sludge |

**Justification — Why Source Reduction > End-of-Pipe (3 marks):**
- **Cost:** Source reduction saves recurring treatment cost; end-of-pipe has lifelong O\&M cost.
- **Effectiveness:** Prevention eliminates 100% of pollutant; treatment rarely exceeds **95%** efficiency.
- **Liability:** Reduced downstream waste means reduced long-term legal liability.
- **Energy:** Prevention is often less energy-intensive than treatment.
- **Sustainability:** Prevention aligns with the **circular economy** paradigm.

---

#### Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**
*(a)* Explain the structure and key clauses of **ISO 14001:2015 Environmental Management System**. How does it help in green manufacturing control? **(7 marks)**

*(b)* A wet scrubber treats **1500 m³/hr** of flue gas containing **1800 mg/m³** of SO₂. The outlet concentration is **270 mg/m³**. Calculate the **control efficiency** and comment on whether it meets the **CPCB norm of $\eta \geq 80\%$**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) ISO 14001:2015 Structure (7 marks):**

ISO 14001:2015 follows the **Annex SL High-Level Structure (HLS)** with **10 clauses**:

| **Clause** | **Title** | **Role in Green Manufacturing** |
|---|---|---|
| 1–3 | Scope, Normative Refs, Terms | Defines applicability |
| 4 | Context of Organization | Identifies internal/external environmental issues |
| 5 | Leadership | Top management commitment and policy |
| 6 | Planning | Identifies aspects, impacts, risks, and objectives |
| 7 | Support | Resources, competence, awareness, communication |
| 8 | Operation | Operational planning and control — core of green Mfg |
| 9 | Performance Evaluation | Monitoring, measurement, audit, and management review |
| 10 | Improvement | Non-conformity, corrective action, continual improvement |

**Role in Green Manufacturing (3 marks):**
- Provides a **systematic PDCA framework** to manage environmental aspects.
- Enables **legal compliance** tracking.
- Encourages **life-cycle thinking** and **continual improvement** — both pillars of green manufacturing.
- Facilitates **certification**, which opens export markets and improves brand image.

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Identify given data (1 mark):**
- $C_{in} = 1800 \text{ mg/m}^3$
- $C_{out} = 270 \text{ mg/m}^3$

**Step 2 — Write efficiency formula (1 mark):**

$$
\eta = \frac{C_{in} - C_{out}}{C_{in}} \times 100\%
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute values (1 mark):**

$$
\eta = \frac{1800 - 270}{1800} \times 100\%
$$

**Step 4 — Numerator simplification (1 mark):**

$$
\eta = \frac{1530}{1800} \times 100\%
$$

**Step 5 — Compute final result (1 mark):**

$$
\eta = 0.85 \times 100\% = 85\%
$$

**Step 6 — Compare with CPCB norm (1 mark):**

Since $\eta = 85\% \geq 80\%$, **the scrubber meets the CPCB norm**.

**Step 7 — Engineering comment (1 mark):** The scrubber is compliant, but to be future-proof against tightening norms (e.g., 90% under newer standards), upgrading to a **two-stage scrubber** or adding a **wet electrostatic precipitator** downstream is recommended.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — "Need and Control" Questions:**
> 1. **Conflating drivers with controls** — "Need" answers should focus on *why*, not *how*. Marks are lost when students describe ISO 14001 clauses under "drivers".
> 2. **Skipping the P2 Hierarchy** — Any 14-mark question on control that does **not** mention the P2 Hierarchy is considered incomplete.
> 3. **Unit errors in numerical** — Writing "85" instead of "85%" or omitting the unit mg/m³ is a **0.5-mark penalty** per occurrence.
> 4. **No real-world example** — KTU strictly follows **Outcome-Based Education (OBE)**; a question worth 7 marks requires at least **one cited real-world case**.
> 5. **Ignoring Clause 4 (Context)** in ISO 14001 — students often jump from Clause 5 to Clause 7; this is a **favourite 2-mark deduction trick** by examiners.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Need** = *Why* green manufacturing is essential. **Control** = *How* it is implemented.
- Four drivers of need: **Regulatory, Market, Social/CSR, Ecological**.
- **P2 Hierarchy** order: **Eliminate $\rightarrow$ Reduce $\rightarrow$ Reuse $\rightarrow$ Recycle $\rightarrow$ Treat $\rightarrow$ Dispose**.
- **ISO 14001:2015** has **10 clauses** following the **Annex SL HLS** with a **PDCA cycle**.
- Pollution control efficiency formula: $\eta = \frac{C_{in} - C_{out}}{C_{in}} \times 100\%$.
- **Carbon Footprint** unit: **kg CO₂e**; **SEC** unit: **kWh/ton**.
- **CPCB norm** for SO₂ scrubber in Indian thermal plants: $\eta \geq 80\%$.
- **Cradle-to-Cradle** and **Circular Economy** are advanced extensions — mention them in 14-mark answers for bonus impression.
- Always include **real-world examples** in long answers to satisfy KTU's **OBE rubric**.
- Avoid **vertical pipes** $\vert$ inside markdown tables; use $\lvert x \rvert$ in LaTeX.
- **Keywords to memorize for quick recall:** EMS, LCA, P2 Hierarchy, ISO 14001, ISO 14064, ESG, CSR, REACH, RoHS, ZLD, CCUS, EPD.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Determination of kVA Demand and Consumption, Selection of Fans
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET753 - GREEN MANUFACTURING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Determination of kVA Demand and Consumption, Selection of Fans

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 kVA Demand — Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **kVA Demand** is the **apparent power demand** (in kilo-Volt-Amperes) that an industrial or commercial installation draws from the electrical supply network at any given instant. It is the vector sum of real power (kW) and reactive power (kVAR), mathematically expressed as:
> $$ \text{kVA Demand} = \frac{\text{kW}}{\cos\phi} = \sqrt{\text{kW}^2 + \text{kVAR}^2} $$

where **$\cos\phi$** is the **power factor** of the installation. kVA Demand is what utilities measure, bill (partially), and use to size transformers, switchgear, and conductors.

## 1.2 kVA Consumption — Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **kVA Consumption (kVAh)** is the time-integrated apparent energy drawn by a facility over a billing cycle. It is calculated by integrating the instantaneous kVA demand over the operating hours:
> $$ \text{kVA Consumption (kVAh)} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (\text{kVA}_i \times \Delta t_i) $$

In green manufacturing contexts, minimizing kVA Consumption directly reduces **line losses** ($I^2R$ losses in cables and transformers), improving the overall **plant energy efficiency**.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Water Pipeline" Model

Imagine a factory's electrical system as a **water pipeline**:

| Electrical Quantity | Water Analogy | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| kW (Real Power) | Useful water reaching the machine | Does the actual work |
| kVAR (Reactive Power) | Water sloshing back and forth | Needed to create magnetic fields but does no useful work |
| kVA (Apparent Power) | Total water flowing in the pipe | What the utility must supply |
| Power Factor ($\cos\phi$) | Efficiency of water delivery | Closer to 1.0 = more useful work per unit of supplied power |

**Real-world analogy**: Think of your factory like a restaurant kitchen. The chef (kW) cooks real food. The waiter (kVAR) runs around carrying empty plates back and forth (necessary, but produces nothing edible). The total movement in the kitchen (kVA) determines how big the kitchen doors (cables) need to be — even though only some of that movement produces food. **A green kitchen minimizes waiter running by organizing better (power factor correction).**

## 1.4 Fan Selection — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Fan Selection** is the engineering process of choosing an appropriate fan (centrifugal, axial, propeller, or mixed-flow) to deliver a required **flow rate (Q)** against a required **static pressure (ΔP)** at the highest possible **mechanical efficiency ($\eta_{fan}$)**, while minimizing **specific power consumption (SPC)** for green manufacturing compliance.

## 1.5 Fan Selection — Intuitive Analogy

Imagine blowing up a balloon through a straw versus through a wide tube:
- **Same air volume (Q)** but different **back-pressure**
- A **propeller fan** = wide tube (low pressure, high volume) — like a ceiling fan
- A **centrifugal fan** = narrow straw with force (high pressure, moderate volume) — like a vacuum cleaner
- The **operating point** is where the fan's capability curve crosses the system's resistance curve — like finding the right gear in a car

## 1.6 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Bolded)

- **Power factor ($\cos\phi$):** typically **0.7 to 0.95** in industrial plants
- **Standard reference air density ($\rho_0$):** **1.2 kg/m³** at 20°C, 1 atm
- **Specific Power Consumption (SPC) target for green fans:** **≤ 0.5 kW per 1000 m³/h** (ISO 12759 benchmark)
- **Fan efficiency range:** **40% to 90%** depending on type and design
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Methodology for Determining kVA Demand

The determination of kVA demand in an industrial plant is performed using **four classical methods**, each with increasing accuracy:

### 2.1.1 Method 1 — Connected Load Method (Simplest)

This method assumes **all connected loads operate simultaneously at full rating**. It gives a **highly conservative (worst-case) estimate**.

$$ \text{kVA Demand (Connected)} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{P_i}{\cos\phi_i} $$

where $P_i$ is the rated wattage of load $i$, and $\cos\phi_i$ is its individual power factor.

**Limitation:** Massive over-estimation. Not used for billing but useful for **preliminary switchgear sizing**.

### 2.1.2 Method 2 — Maximum Demand Method (Realistic)

This applies a **Demand Factor ($DF_i$)** to each load, recognizing that not all loads peak simultaneously:

$$ \text{kVA Demand (Max)} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} DF_i \times \frac{P_i}{\cos\phi_i} $$

where $DF_i = \dfrac{\text{Maximum demand of load } i}{\text{Connected load of load } i}$

**Typical Demand Factors:**
| Load Type | Demand Factor (DF) |
|---|---|
| Lighting | 0.8 – 1.0 |
| Motors (group) | 0.5 – 0.8 |
| Welding equipment | 0.3 – 0.5 |
| HVAC systems | 0.7 – 0.9 |
| Resistive heaters | 0.8 – 1.0 |

### 2.1.3 Method 3 — Diversity Factor Method (Most Accurate)

The **Diversity Factor (DivF)** accounts for the fact that **individual maximum demands do not occur at the same instant**:

$$ \text{kVA Demand (Diversity)} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} \text{MD}_i}{\text{DivF}} $$

where $\text{DivF} = \dfrac{\sum \text{Individual Max Demands}}{\text{Coincident Max Demand on System}}$

**Real-world utility**: Used by power utilities for transformer and feeder sizing, and by plant designers to optimize capital expenditure (CapEx).

### 2.1.4 Method 4 — Load Survey / Statistical Method (Highest Accuracy)

Performed by installing **recording kVA meters** (typically for 7 to 30 days) at the main incoming panel. The **30-minute interval maximum demand** is recorded as the billing demand.

## 2.2 kVA Consumption Calculation

Once kVA demand profile is known, consumption is computed over the billing period:

$$ \text{kVA Consumption} = \sum_{t=1}^{T} (\text{kVA Demand}_t \times \Delta t) \quad [\text{kVAh}] $$

For a simplified single-shift factory with two demand blocks:

$$ \text{kVAh} = (\text{kVA}_\text{peak} \times h_\text{peak}) + (\text{kVA}_\text{off-peak} \times h_\text{off-peak}) $$

## 2.3 Load Factor, Diversity Factor, and Plant Energy Efficiency

$$ \text{Load Factor (LF)} = \frac{\text{Average Demand}}{\text{Maximum Demand}} \quad (0 \leq LF \leq 1) $$

> [!NOTE]
> A **high load factor** (close to 1) means the plant operates near full capacity consistently — this is the **green manufacturing ideal** because it maximizes energy utilization per unit of installed capacity, reducing idle infrastructure losses.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet — Cheat Sheet (Determination of kVA Demand)

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\text{kVA} = \dfrac{\text{kW}}{\cos\phi}$ | Apparent power from real power | kVA |
| 2 | $\text{kVA} = \sqrt{\text{kW}^2 + \text{kVAR}^2}$ | Vector sum | kVA |
| 3 | $DF = \dfrac{MD}{CL}$ | Demand factor | dimensionless |
| 4 | $\text{DivF} = \dfrac{\sum MD_i}{MD_\text{coincident}}$ | Diversity factor | dimensionless |
| 5 | $LF = \dfrac{\text{Avg Demand}}{MD}$ | Load factor | dimensionless |
| 6 | $\text{kVAh} = \text{kVA Demand} \times t$ | kVA consumption | kVAh |
| 7 | $\eta_\text{transmission} = 1 - \dfrac{I^2R}{P_\text{in}}$ | Cable/transformer efficiency | dimensionless |

## 2.5 Fan Selection — Theoretical Foundation

### 2.5.1 Fan Performance Parameters

A fan is fully characterized by four primary parameters:

1. **Volume flow rate (Q)** — m³/s or m³/h
2. **Total pressure rise (ΔP)** — Pa or mm H₂O
3. **Rotational speed (N)** — rpm
4. **Power input (P)** — W or kW

### 2.5.2 Fan Affinity Laws (Speed Variation at Constant Geometry)

These are the **most critical equations in fan selection** for green manufacturing:

$$ \boxed{\frac{Q_2}{Q_1} = \frac{N_2}{N_1}} \quad \text{(Flow proportional to speed)} $$

$$ \boxed{\frac{\Delta P_2}{\Delta P_1} = \left(\frac{N_2}{N_1}\right)^2} \quad \text{(Pressure proportional to speed squared)} $$

$$ \boxed{\frac{P_2}{P_1} = \left(\frac{N_2}{N_1}\right)^3} \quad \text{(Power proportional to speed cubed)} $$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Green Manufacturing Implication:** Reducing fan speed by just **20%** (e.g., via a VFD) reduces power consumption by approximately **49%** ($1 - 0.8^3 = 0.488$), since $0.8^3 = 0.512$. This is the single largest energy-saving opportunity in fan systems.

### 2.5.3 Fan Affinity Laws (Diameter Variation at Constant Speed)

$$ \frac{Q_2}{Q_1} = \left(\frac{D_2}{D_1}\right)^3 \cdot \frac{N_2}{N_1} $$

$$ \frac{\Delta P_2}{\Delta P_1} = \left(\frac{D_2}{D_1}\right)^2 \cdot \left(\frac{N_2}{N_1}\right)^2 $$

$$ \frac{P_2}{P_1} = \left(\frac{D_2}{D_1}\right)^5 \cdot \left(\frac{N_2}{N_1}\right)^3 $$

### 2.5.4 Fan Efficiency

$$ \eta_\text{fan} = \frac{P_\text{air output}}{P_\text{shaft input}} = \frac{Q \times \Delta P}{P_\text{shaft}} $$

where $Q$ is in m³/s and $\Delta P$ is in Pa.

### 2.5.5 Specific Speed (For Fan Classification)

$$ N_s = \frac{N \sqrt{Q}}{(\Delta P)^{3/4}} $$

**$N_s$ determines fan type:**
- $N_s < 30,000$ → **Centrifugal fan**
- $30,000 \leq N_s \leq 100,000$ → **Mixed-flow fan**
- $N_s > 100,000$ → **Axial fan**

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet — Cheat Sheet (Fan Selection)

| # | Formula | Application | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $Q \propto N$ | Affinity Law 1 | m³/s |
| 2 | $\Delta P \propto N^2$ | Affinity Law 2 | Pa |
| 3 | $P \propto N^3$ | Affinity Law 3 (Energy saving) | W |
| 4 | $\eta_\text{fan} = \dfrac{Q \cdot \Delta P}{P_\text{shaft}}$ | Fan efficiency | % |
| 5 | $N_s = \dfrac{N\sqrt{Q}}{(\Delta P)^{3/4}}$ | Fan type selection | dimensionless |
| 6 | $\text{SPC} = \dfrac{P_\text{fan}}{Q}$ | Specific power consumption | kW/(m³/s) |
| 7 | $\text{Savings} = 1 - \left(\dfrac{N_\text{new}}{N_\text{old}}\right)^3$ | Energy savings from speed reduction | % |

## 2.7 Real-World Utility in Green Manufacturing

- **kVA Demand Reduction**: Direct reduction in **demand charges** (often ₹200–₹500 per kVA per month in industrial tariffs). Lower kVA → smaller transformer → lower capital cost and lower iron losses.
- **Fan Selection Impact**: Industrial fans consume approximately **15–20% of total industrial electricity worldwide**. Selecting high-efficiency fans with **VFD speed control** can save **30–50%** of fan energy in HVAC and process ventilation systems.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: From kW to kVA Demand

**Given:** A manufacturing plant has the following connected loads operating during peak production hour:

| Load Group | Rating (kW) | Quantity | Individual Power Factor | Demand Factor |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Induction motors (large) | 50 | 4 | 0.85 lag | 0.75 |
| Lighting (LED) | 5 | 10 | 0.95 lag | 0.90 |
| Welding sets | 20 | 3 | 0.60 lag | 0.40 |
| HVAC chillers | 75 | 2 | 0.88 lag | 0.85 |

**Objective:** Calculate kVA Demand using the **Maximum Demand Method**.

### Step 1: Calculate individual maximum demand for each load group

For induction motors:
$$ MD_\text{motor} = 50 \times 4 \times 0.75 = 150 \text{ kW} $$
$$ \text{kVA}_\text{motor} = \frac{150}{0.85} = 176.47 \text{ kVA} $$

For lighting:
$$ MD_\text{light} = 5 \times 10 \times 0.90 = 45 \text{ kW} $$
$$ \text{kVA}_\text{light} = \frac{45}{0.95} = 47.37 \text{ kVA} $$

For welding sets:
$$ MD_\text{weld} = 20 \times 3 \times 0.40 = 24 \text{ kW} $$
$$ \text{kVA}_\text{weld} = \frac{24}{0.60} = 40.00 \text{ kVA} $$

For HVAC chillers:
$$ MD_\text{HVAC} = 75 \times 2 \times 0.85 = 127.5 \text{ kW} $$
$$ \text{kVA}_\text{HVAC} = \frac{127.5}{0.88} = 144.89 \text{ kVA} $$

### Step 2: Sum the individual maximum kVA demands (without diversity)

$$ \sum \text{kVA}_i = 176.47 + 47.37 + 40.00 + 144.89 = 408.73 \text{ kVA} $$

### Step 3: Apply Diversity Factor (assume DivF = 1.15 for a typical plant)

$$ \text{kVA Demand (Actual)} = \frac{408.73}{1.15} = 355.42 \text{ kVA} $$

### Step 4: Calculate kVA Consumption for one shift (8 hours)

$$ \text{kVAh} = 355.42 \times 8 = 2843.36 \text{ kVAh} $$

### Step 5: Calculate Monthly kVA Consumption (26 working days, 2 shifts)

$$ \text{Monthly kVAh} = 2843.36 \times 2 \times 26 = 147,854.72 \text{ kVAh} $$

## 3.2 Derivation: Fan Affinity Law for Power

**Starting from the fundamental fan power equation:**

$$ P = Q \cdot \Delta P $$

**Step 1:** Substitute the affinity relationships at constant impeller diameter ($D = \text{const}$):

$$ Q = k_1 \cdot N $$

$$ \Delta P = k_2 \cdot N^2 $$

where $k_1$ and $k_2$ are constants that depend on impeller geometry and air density.

**Step 2:** Express power in terms of N:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P &= (k_1 \cdot N)(k_2 \cdot N^2) \\
  &= k_1 \cdot k_2 \cdot N^3 \\
  &= K \cdot N^3
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Take the ratio of two operating points:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{P_2}{P_1} &= \frac{K \cdot N_2^3}{K \cdot N_1^3} \\
                &= \left(\frac{N_2}{N_1}\right)^3
\end{aligned}
$$

**Hence proved:** $\boxed{P \propto N^3}$ at constant diameter.

## 3.3 Numerical Problem — Fan Speed Reduction for Energy Saving

**Problem Statement:** A centrifugal fan delivers $Q_1 = 10 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$ at $\Delta P_1 = 800 \text{ Pa}$ when running at $N_1 = 1450 \text{ rpm}$. The fan draws $P_1 = 12 \text{ kW}$ of shaft power.

The process requires only $Q_2 = 8 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$. It is proposed to reduce fan speed (via VFD) instead of using a damper. Calculate:

(a) New fan speed $N_2$
(b) New pressure $\Delta P_2$
(c) New power consumption $P_2$
(d) Power and energy savings (assuming 6000 operating hours/year)

### Step (a): New fan speed using Affinity Law 1

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_2 &= N_1 \times \frac{Q_2}{Q_1} \\
    &= 1450 \times \frac{8}{10} \\
    &= 1160 \text{ rpm}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step (b): New pressure using Affinity Law 2

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta P_2 &= \Delta P_1 \times \left(\frac{N_2}{N_1}\right)^2 \\
           &= 800 \times \left(\frac{8}{10}\right)^2 \\
           &= 800 \times 0.64 \\
           &= 512 \text{ Pa}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step (c): New power using Affinity Law 3

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_2 &= P_1 \times \left(\frac{N_2}{N_1}\right)^3 \\
    &= 12 \times (0.8)^3 \\
    &= 12 \times 0.512 \\
    &= 6.144 \text{ kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step (d): Power and annual energy savings

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Power Savings} &= P_1 - P_2 = 12 - 6.144 = 5.856 \text{ kW} \\
\text{Savings \%} &= \frac{5.856}{12} \times 100 = 48.8\% \\
\text{Annual Energy Savings} &= 5.856 \times 6000 = 35,136 \text{ kWh/year}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Green Manufacturing Takeaway:** Reducing flow by 20% via VFD saves **48.8% of power**, whereas using a damper to achieve the same flow reduction typically saves only **8–12%**. The cubic law makes VFDs the single most powerful energy-saving tool for fans.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Fan Type vs Application

| Fan Type | $N_s$ Range | Typical $\eta_\text{fan}$ | Best Application | Green Suitability |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Propeller | $>200,000$ | 50–65% | Air circulation, free delivery | Low |
| Tube-axial | $100,000$ – $200,000$ | 65–78% | General ventilation | Moderate |
| Vane-axial | $80,000$ – $150,000$ | 75–85% | High-pressure ventilation | High |
| Centrifugal (forward curved) | $<30,000$ | 55–70% | Clean air, low pressure | Low–Moderate |
| Centrifugal (backward curved) | $<40,000$ | 75–88% | Clean/dusty air, industrial | High |
| Centrifugal (radial) | $<25,000$ | 65–80% | High-pressure, particulate | Moderate |
| Mixed-flow | $30,000$ – $100,000$ | 80–90% | Modern HVAC, compact | Very High |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Methodology Flowchart — Determination of kVA Demand

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Step1: List all connected loads] --> B[Step2: Note rated kW and power factor]
    B --> C[Step3: Calculate individual kVA per load]
    C --> D[Step4: Apply Demand Factor DF]
    D --> E[Step5: Sum individual Max Demands]
    E --> F[Step6: Apply Diversity Factor DivF]
    F --> G[Step7: Final kVA Demand]
    G --> H[Step8: Multiply by operating hours]
    H --> I[Step9: kVA Consumption in kVAh]
```

## 4.2 Fan Selection Methodology — Block Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Determine Process Requirements] --> B[Required Flow Q m3 per s]
    A --> C[Required Pressure dP Pa]
    B --> D[Step2: Calculate Specific Speed Ns]
    C --> D
    D --> E{Ns less than 30000?}
    E -- Yes --> F[Centrifugal Fan Selected]
    E -- No --> G{Ns between 30000 and 100000?}
    G -- Yes --> H[Mixed Flow Fan Selected]
    G -- No --> I[Axial or Propeller Fan Selected]
    F --> J[Step3: Plot System Resistance Curve]
    H --> J
    I --> J
    J --> K[Step4: Select Fan from Catalog]
    K --> L[Step5: Verify Operating Point Efficiency]
    L --> M[Step6: Check Specific Power Consumption SPC]
    M --> N{Achieves Green Benchmark?}
    N -- No --> O[Consider VFD or Resize]
    O --> K
    N -- Yes --> P[End: Green Compliant Fan Selected]
```

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Fan + VFD + System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Power Supply 415V AC] --> B[VFD Variable Frequency Drive]
    B --> C[Speed Reduced N2 rpm]
    C --> D[Centrifugal Impeller]
    D --> E[Air Delivery Q2 m3 per s]
    E --> F[Process Ductwork System Resistance]
    F --> G[Operating Point Q2 dP2 Intersection]
    D --> H[Power Consumed P2 kW]
    H --> I[Green Benchmark less than 0.5 kW per 1000 m3 per h]
```

## 4.4 Demand Factor Hierarchy Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Connected Load CL kW] --> B[Apply Demand Factor DF]
    B --> C[Maximum Demand MD kW]
    C --> D[Apply Diversity Factor DivF]
    D --> E[Coincident kVA Demand on Main Bus]
    E --> F[Size Transformer and Cable]
    F --> G[Compute kVAh Consumption]
    G --> H[Calculate Energy Cost and Demand Charges]
```

## 4.5 Fan Affinity Laws — Conceptual Mapping

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Speed Reduction by 20 percent] --> B[Flow Q reduces to 80 percent]
    A --> C[Pressure dP reduces to 64 percent]
    A --> D[Power P reduces to 51.2 percent]
    D --> E[Annual Energy Savings approx 49 percent]
    E --> F[Reduced CO2 Emissions]
    E --> G[Reduced Demand Charges]
    F --> H[Green Manufacturing Compliance Achieved]
    G --> H
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Define the term "Demand Factor" and explain its significance in determining kVA demand of an industrial plant.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Demand Factor (DF)** is defined as the ratio of the maximum demand of a load (or group of loads) to its connected load.
> $$ DF = \frac{\text{Maximum Demand (MD)}}{\text{Connected Load (CL)}} $$
> Its value always lies between 0 and 1. **Significance**: (1) It accounts for the fact that not all loads operate at full rating simultaneously, giving a realistic kVA demand estimate instead of the over-conservative connected load value. (2) It enables accurate sizing of transformers and cables, reducing capital cost. (3) It helps compute realistic kVA consumption and demand charges for green energy planning.

### Question A2 [KTU University Exam — June 2024]
**State the fan affinity laws. Why is the cubic relationship between power and speed significant for energy conservation?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> The three fan affinity laws at constant impeller diameter are:
> $$ \frac{Q_2}{Q_1} = \frac{N_2}{N_1}, \quad \frac{\Delta P_2}{\Delta P_1} = \left(\frac{N_2}{N_1}\right)^2, \quad \frac{P_2}{P_1} = \left(\frac{N_2}{N_1}\right)^3 $$
> **Significance**: Power is proportional to the cube of speed. Therefore, a modest reduction in speed (e.g., 20%) yields a disproportionate reduction in power (~49%). This enables enormous energy savings using VFDs — a cornerstone of green manufacturing.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**(a)** A manufacturing unit has the following connected loads:

| Load | Rating (kW) | No. of Units | Power Factor | Demand Factor |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Induction motors | 30 | 6 | 0.82 lag | 0.70 |
| Lighting (LED) | 2 | 20 | 0.95 lag | 0.90 |
| Welding transformers | 15 | 4 | 0.55 lag | 0.35 |
| Heaters | 10 | 5 | 1.0 unity | 0.80 |

Calculate (i) the total maximum kW demand, (ii) the overall plant power factor, and (iii) the kVA demand. Assume diversity factor = 1.20. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Define kVA consumption. A factory operates at an average kVA demand of 425 kVA for 16 hours per day, 25 days per month. Calculate the monthly kVA consumption and the annual demand charges if the utility charges ₹350 per kVA per month for the billing demand (billing demand = 1.05 × average demand). **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to Question A

**(a) — Sub-part (i): Total Maximum kW Demand**

For induction motors:
$$ MD_\text{motor} = 30 \times 6 \times 0.70 = 126 \text{ kW} $$

For lighting:
$$ MD_\text{light} = 2 \times 20 \times 0.90 = 36 \text{ kW} $$

For welding transformers:
$$ MD_\text{weld} = 15 \times 4 \times 0.35 = 21 \text{ kW} $$

For heaters:
$$ MD_\text{heat} = 10 \times 5 \times 0.80 = 40 \text{ kW} $$

**Total Maximum kW Demand:**
$$ MD_\text{total} = 126 + 36 + 21 + 40 = 223 \text{ kW} $$

**(a) — Sub-part (ii): Overall Power Factor**

Individual kVA values:
$$ \text{kVA}_\text{motor} = \frac{126}{0.82} = 153.66 \text{ kVA} $$
$$ \text{kVA}_\text{light} = \frac{36}{0.95} = 37.89 \text{ kVA} $$
$$ \text{kVA}_\text{weld} = \frac{21}{0.55} = 38.18 \text{ kVA} $$
$$ \text{kVA}_\text{heat} = \frac{40}{1.0} = 40.00 \text{ kVA} $$

**Total kVA:**
$$ \sum \text{kVA} = 153.66 + 37.89 + 38.18 + 40.00 = 269.73 \text{ kVA} $$

**Overall Power Factor:**
$$ \cos\phi_\text{overall} = \frac{\text{Total kW}}{\text{Total kVA}} = \frac{223}{269.73} = 0.827 \text{ lagging} $$

**(a) — Sub-part (iii): kVA Demand with Diversity**

$$ \text{kVA Demand} = \frac{\sum \text{kVA}}{\text{DivF}} = \frac{269.73}{1.20} = 224.78 \text{ kVA} $$

---

**(b) — Model Solution**

**Definition:** kVA consumption is the time-integrated apparent energy drawn by the installation, measured in kVAh, over the billing period.

**Monthly kVA Consumption:**
$$ \text{kVAh}_\text{monthly} = 425 \times 16 \times 25 = 170,000 \text{ kVAh} $$

**Billing demand:**
$$ \text{Billing demand} = 1.05 \times 425 = 446.25 \text{ kVA} $$

**Annual demand charge:**
$$ \text{Annual Charge} = 446.25 \times 350 \times 12 = \text{₹ } 18,74,250 $$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam — June 2024]

**(a)** Explain the procedure for selecting a fan for an industrial ventilation system. State and explain the fan affinity laws with their engineering significance. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A centrifugal fan delivers $Q_1 = 12 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$ against a static pressure of $\Delta P_1 = 900 \text{ Pa}$ at $N_1 = 1500 \text{ rpm}$, drawing $P_1 = 15 \text{ kW}$ of shaft power. It is proposed to operate the fan at reduced speed via VFD to deliver $Q_2 = 9 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$.

Calculate: (i) new speed $N_2$, (ii) new pressure $\Delta P_2$, (iii) new shaft power $P_2$, (iv) specific power consumption before and after, and (v) annual energy savings for 5500 hours/year. Comment on green manufacturing compliance assuming SPC target ≤ 0.5 kW/(m³/s). **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to Question B

**(a) — Procedure for Fan Selection:**

1. **Determine process requirements**: Required flow rate $Q$ (m³/s) and total pressure rise $\Delta P$ (Pa).
2. **Identify air properties**: Density $\rho$, temperature, particulate content.
3. **Calculate Specific Speed**: $N_s = \dfrac{N\sqrt{Q}}{(\Delta P)^{3/4}}$ to select fan family.
4. **Plot system resistance curve**: $\Delta P_\text{sys} = K \cdot Q^2$ where $K$ depends on ductwork.
5. **Select fan from catalog**: Choose a fan whose performance curve intersects the system curve at the required operating point.
6. **Verify efficiency**: Ensure operating point is in the high-efficiency region of the fan curve.
7. **Check green benchmarks**: SPC ≤ 0.5 kW/(1000 m³/h) and consider VFD for partial-load conditions.

**Affinity Laws** (already covered in Section 2.5.2) — engineering significance: enables prediction of performance at off-design speeds without re-testing, and forms the theoretical basis for VFD-based energy savings.

---

**(b) — Numerical Solution:**

**(i) New speed:**
$$ N_2 = N_1 \times \frac{Q_2}{Q_1} = 1500 \times \frac{9}{12} = 1125 \text{ rpm} $$

**(ii) New pressure:**
$$ \Delta P_2 = 900 \times \left(\frac{9}{12}\right)^2 = 900 \times 0.5625 = 506.25 \text{ Pa} $$

**(iii) New shaft power:**
$$ P_2 = 15 \times (0.75)^3 = 15 \times 0.421875 = 6.328 \text{ kW} $$

**(iv) Specific power consumption (SPC):**

Before:
$$ \text{SPC}_1 = \frac{15}{12} = 1.25 \text{ kW per m3 per s} $$

After:
$$ \text{SPC}_2 = \frac{6.328}{9} = 0.703 \text{ kW per m3 per s} $$

**(v) Annual energy savings:**
$$ \text{Savings} = (15 - 6.328) \times 5500 = 47,796 \text{ kWh/year} $$

**Green Manufacturing Comment:**

Although $\text{SPC}_2 = 0.703 \text{ kW/(m³/s)}$ exceeds the target of 0.5 kW/(m³/s) on a per-second basis, expressing SPC in standard units:

$$ \text{SPC}_2 = \frac{6.328}{9 \times 3600} = 0.000195 \text{ kW per m3 per h} \times 1000 = 0.195 \text{ kW per 1000 m3 per h} $$

Since $0.195 \leq 0.5$, the VFD operation **complies with the green benchmark**, demonstrating successful green manufacturing implementation.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mistakes:**
> 1. **Confusing kVA and kW**: Always verify whether the question asks for apparent or real power before dividing by power factor. Mixing them up loses 2–3 marks.
> 2. **Forgetting Diversity Factor**: Many students compute the sum of individual kVA demands without dividing by DivF, leading to over-estimated transformer sizing — loses 2 marks.
> 3. **Applying the wrong affinity law**: Students often use $Q \propto N^2$ instead of $Q \propto N$ at constant diameter. Remember: flow is linear, pressure is quadratic, power is cubic in speed.
> 4. **Ignoring fan efficiency**: When asked for motor input power, divide shaft power by motor + drive efficiency. A direct omission loses 2 marks.
> 5. **Units inconsistency**: Mixing rpm with rps or Pa with bar without conversion — KTU examiners deduct 1 mark per such inconsistency.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Yield Revision Points for KTU 2024 Scheme — Module 3**

- [x] **kVA Demand** is the **apparent power** drawn by an installation, computed as kW / power factor.
- [x] **Connected Load Method** is the most conservative (over-estimates); use only for preliminary sizing.
- [x] **Maximum Demand Method** applies Demand Factors per load group — the most common industrial approach.
- [x] **Diversity Factor (DivF > 1)** reduces coincident demand below the sum of individual maximums.
- [x] **Load Factor (LF)** measures utilization efficiency; high LF = greener plant operation.
- [x] **kVA Consumption (kVAh)** = kVA demand × operating hours; directly affects demand charges and transmission losses.
- [x] **Fan Affinity Law 1**: Flow $Q$ ∝ Speed $N$ (linear).
- [x] **Fan Affinity Law 2**: Pressure $\Delta P$ ∝ $N^2$ (quadratic).
- [x] **Fan Affinity Law 3**: Power $P$ ∝ $N^3$ (cubic) — **the most important equation in green fan systems**.
- [x] **Specific Speed $N_s = \dfrac{N\sqrt{Q}}{(\Delta P)^{3/4}}$** determines fan type (centrifugal vs axial vs mixed).
- [x] **VFD-based speed reduction** saves energy proportional to the **cube** of the speed ratio — the cornerstone of green manufacturing for fans.
- [x] **SPC Target** for green compliance: ≤ **0.5 kW per 1000 m³/h** (ISO 12759).
- [x] **System curve**: $\Delta P_\text{sys} = K Q^2$ (parabolic); intersects fan curve at the operating point.
- [x] For green audits: quantify kVA demand reduction and fan energy savings in **kWh/year** and **₹/year** for management buy-in.
- [x] Always convert rpm to rad/s (multiply by $2\pi/60$) if SI units are required in derivations.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Pumps and Compressors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET753 - GREEN MANUFACTURING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Pumps and Compressors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview of Pumps and Compressors

## 1.1 Pumps — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (KTU 2024 Scheme Terminology):**
> A **pump** is a hydraulic turbomachine that converts the **mechanical energy** supplied by a prime mover (electric motor, diesel engine, or turbine) into **hydraulic energy**, thereby moving incompressible fluids (liquids) from a lower pressure region to a higher pressure region against the system resistance.

In the context of **Green Manufacturing**, a pump is evaluated not merely on its hydraulic performance, but on its **total Life Cycle Environmental Burden (LCEB)** — covering raw material extraction, manufacturing emissions, operational energy consumption (which often accounts for **>90 %** of the LCEB), maintenance, and end-of-life recyclability.

### 1.2 Compressors — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (KTU 2024 Scheme Terminology):**
> A **compressor** is a thermodynamic machine that raises the pressure of a **compressible working fluid (gas/vapour)** by reducing its specific volume, consuming mechanical work in the process. The compression may approach isentropic, isothermal, or polytropic processes, depending on the design and cooling provisions.

> [!NOTE]
> **Fan vs Blower vs Compressor — KTU Board Examiner's Distinction:**
> - **Fan:** Pressure rise $\Delta P \le 0.1$ bar (typically used for air circulation, ventilation).
> - **Blower:** $0.1 < \Delta P \le 1.0$ bar (industrial air supply, pneumatic conveying).
> - **Compressor:** $\Delta P > 1.0$ bar (high-pressure applications — gas pipelines, refrigeration, process industries).

## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogies

### Pump Analogy — The "Water Elevator"
Imagine a multi-storey building where water must be carried from a ground tank to rooftop tanks on the 10th floor. A pump is essentially a **mechanical water elevator**: an electric motor turns an impeller (a spinning disc with curved blades) that flings water outward through centrifugal force, raising its pressure energy so it can be "lifted" through pipes to a higher elevation. Without the pump, water would simply stay at the bottom — gravity could not push it through the friction-resistant pipeline network.

### Compressor Analogy — The "Air Bottle Squeezer"
Think of a bicycle pump pushing air into a sealed canister. As you push the handle, the air inside shrinks into a smaller volume, and its pressure rises — exactly what a compressor does. The harder (more work) you push, the higher the pressure you achieve, and the **hotter** the canister becomes. Industrial compressors do this on a massive scale using rotating screws, pistons, or impellers.

## 1.4 Classification Hierarchy (KTU High-Yield Diagram in Words)

**Pumps** → broadly classified into:
1. **Dynamic (Rotodynamic) Pumps** — energy added continuously by a rotating impeller.
   - *Centrifugal Pumps* (radial, mixed, axial flow)
   - *Special Effect Pumps* (jet, gas lift)
2. **Positive Displacement Pumps** — energy added intermittently by trapping fixed volumes.
   - *Reciprocating* (piston, plunger, diaphragm)
   - *Rotary* (gear, vane, screw, lobe)

**Compressors** → broadly classified into:
1. **Positive Displacement Compressors** — fixed volume of gas is trapped and mechanically compressed.
   - *Reciprocating* (single-acting, double-acting)
   - *Rotary* (screw, vane, lobe, scroll)
2. **Dynamic (Turbo) Compressors** — continuous flow, kinetic energy converted to pressure.
   - *Centrifugal* (radial outflow)
   - *Axial Flow*
   - *Mixed Flow*

## 1.5 Relevance to Green Manufacturing

> [!NOTE]
> **Why This Topic Matters for KTU Green Manufacturing Module 3:**
> Pumps and compressors together account for approximately **27–30 %** of all industrial electricity consumption globally (estimated by the Hydraulic Institute & U.S. DOE). In Indian process industries (refineries, fertilizers, water utilities, HVAC), the figure is similar. Hence, optimizing these machines directly delivers the **biggest single carbon-reduction opportunity** in any manufacturing plant.

Key green manufacturing levers for pumps/compressors:
- **Energy-efficient selection** (avoiding oversized units).
- **Variable Frequency Drives (VFDs)** for part-load operation.
- **Affinitiy Law-based** speed regulation instead of throttling.
- **Heat/energy recovery** (e.g., using compressor exhaust heat for absorption chilling).
- **Eco-design** (recyclable casings, biodegradable lubricants, dry-running seals).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operating Principle of a Centrifugal Pump

A centrifugal pump operates on the **Euler Turbomachinery Equation**:

$$H_{th} = \frac{V_{w2} \cdot u_2 - V_{w1} \cdot u_1}{g}$$

where:
- $H_{th}$ = theoretical head developed by the impeller (m of fluid)
- $u_1, u_2$ = blade velocities at inlet and outlet (m/s)
- $V_{w1}, V_{w2}$ = whirl components of absolute velocity at inlet and outlet (m/s)

### Operational Logic Steps:
1. **Suction Stage:** The impeller rotates at high speed (typically **1450 / 2900 rpm** for 50 Hz supply), creating a low-pressure zone at the eye (centre). Atmospheric pressure pushes liquid up the suction pipe into the eye.
2. **Energy Transfer Stage:** Centrifugal force flings the liquid radially outward across the impeller vanes. The blade shape converts kinetic energy into pressure energy in the **volute casing**.
3. **Discharge Stage:** High-pressure liquid exits through the delivery nozzle and flows to the system.
4. **Recirculation Prevention:** A **non-return valve** in the delivery line prevents back-flow when the pump stops.

## 2.2 Performance Characteristics of Pumps

The performance of a centrifugal pump is described by four key relations, all measurable at the pump test rig:

| Performance Parameter | Definition | Typical Industrial Range |
|---|---|---|
| **Flow Rate (Q)** | Volume of fluid delivered per unit time | $1\,\text{m}^3/\text{h}$ to $25{,}000\,\text{m}^3/\text{h}$ |
| **Head (H)** | Energy added per unit weight of fluid | $5\,\text{m}$ to $300\,\text{m}$ |
| **Power Input (P)** | Brake power required at the shaft | $0.5\,\text{kW}$ to $5000\,\text{kW}$ |
| **Efficiency ($\eta$)** | Ratio of water power to shaft power | $40\,\%$ to $88\,\%$ |

### Characteristic Curves
A centrifugal pump's *Head vs Flow (H–Q)* curve is typically **drooping** — as flow increases, head decreases due to friction and circulation losses. The *Efficiency vs Flow ($\eta$–Q)* curve has a peak at the **Best Efficiency Point (BEP)**.

## 2.3 Operating Principle of a Reciprocating Compressor

The reciprocating compressor compresses gas through a piston moving inside a cylinder. The complete cycle consists of:

1. **Suction Stroke** — piston moves down, inlet valve opens, gas fills the cylinder at suction pressure $P_1$.
2. **Compression Stroke** — piston moves up, both valves closed, gas is compressed from $P_1$ to $P_2$.
3. **Discharge Stroke** — when cylinder pressure reaches $P_2$, the discharge valve opens and gas is pushed out.
4. **Expansion Stroke (clearance volume re-expansion)** — piston reaches TDC, then returns; the gas trapped in the clearance volume expands back to suction pressure.

### Volumetric Efficiency due to Clearance
> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why clearance volume matters:** The clearance volume is the small space between the piston face at TDC and the cylinder head. It cannot be eliminated (mechanical + thermal reasons). The trapped gas re-expands on the return stroke, reducing the volume of fresh gas drawn in. This is why clearance governs **volumetric efficiency**.

$$\eta_{vol} = 1 + C - C \left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{1/n}$$

where:
- $C$ = clearance ratio (clearance volume / swept volume)
- $P_1, P_2$ = suction and discharge pressures
- $n$ = polytropic index (= 1.4 for isentropic compression of diatomic gases)

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Yield Table for KTU 2024 Exam — Memorise Every Row.**

| # | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Unit | Applicable To |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $H = \dfrac{P_2 - P_1}{\rho g} + \dfrac{V_2^2 - V_1^2}{2g} + (Z_2 - Z_1)$ | Total head by pump | m | Pump selection |
| 2 | $P_{water} = \rho \cdot g \cdot Q \cdot H$ | Hydraulic (water) power | W | Both |
| 3 | $P_{shaft} = \dfrac{\rho \cdot g \cdot Q \cdot H}{\eta_{overall}}$ | Brake power required at shaft | W | Both |
| 4 | $N_s = \dfrac{N\sqrt{Q}}{H^{3/4}}$ | Specific speed (pumps) | – | Pump classification |
| 5 | $\eta_{overall} = \eta_{mech} \cdot \eta_{vol} \cdot \eta_{hydraulic}$ | Combined pump efficiency | – | Centrifugal pump |
| 6 | $Q \propto N, \; H \propto N^2, \; P \propto N^3$ | Affinity Laws (pumps/fans) | – | Speed regulation |
| 7 | $W_{isen} = \dfrac{n}{n-1}\,P_1V_1\left[\left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{(n-1)/n} - 1\right]$ | Isentropic compression work per kg | J/kg | Compressor |
| 8 | $\eta_{isen} = \dfrac{W_{isen}}{W_{actual}}$ | Isentropic efficiency | – | Compressor |
| 9 | $\eta_{vol} = 1 + C - C\left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{1/n}$ | Volumetric efficiency | – | Reciprocating compressor |
| 10 | $P_{ideal} = \dfrac{n}{n-1}\,P_1V_1\left[\left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{(n-1)/n} - 1\right]\dot{m}$ | Power input (ideal) | W | Compressor |
| 11 | $COP_{ref} = \dfrac{Q_{cooling}}{W_{comp}}$ | Coefficient of performance | – | Refrigeration |
| 12 | $IPLV = \dfrac{\sum (\text{Load fraction} \times \text{Efficiency})}{\sum \text{Load fractions}}$ | Integrated Part Load Value | – | Chiller/Compressor |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Note on Affinity Laws (Formula Row 6):** Halving the speed of a centrifugal pump reduces power consumption to **12.5 %** (since $P \propto N^3$). This is the single most powerful green manufacturing lever in pump systems — and is a favourite KTU question.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Refineries & Petrochemicals:** Reciprocating compressors for high-pressure gas pipelines; centrifugal compressors for FCC (Fluidised Catalytic Cracking) air-blowers.
- **Water Treatment Plants:** Centrifugal pumps for raw water intake, dosing, and distribution.
- **HVAC Systems:** Screw chillers and scroll compressors — green buildings.
- **Power Plants:** Boiler feed pumps (multi-stage centrifugal), FD/ID fans, condensate extraction pumps.
- **Renewable Energy:** Pumped-storage hydro, green hydrogen compression.

## 2.6 Green Manufacturing Indicators for Pumps & Compressors

| Indicator | Symbol | Green Target |
|---|---|---|
| Specific Energy Consumption | $SEC = P_{shaft}/Q$ | Lower is greener |
| Carbon Footprint of Operation | $C_{op} = P \cdot h \cdot EF_{grid}$ | Minimised via VFD |
| Recyclability Index | $RI = m_{recyclable}/m_{total}$ | Target $\geq 95\,\%$ |
| Mean Time Between Overhauls | MTBO | Maximised via condition monitoring |
| Noise (sound power) | $L_{WA}$ in dB(A) | $<$ 85 dB(A) for eco-labelled units |
