# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Euler's Turbomachinery Equation for a Centrifugal Pump

> [!NOTE]
> **Why derive this?** This equation is the *theoretical* foundation of all rotodynamic machines. KTU examiners frequently set 14-mark problems asking the student to derive it from first principles.

### Step 1: Set Up the Control Volume
Consider the rotating impeller of a centrifugal pump. Mass $m$ of fluid enters at the eye (radius $r_1$) with absolute velocity $V_1$ and leaves at the tip (radius $r_2$) with absolute velocity $V_2$. The impeller rotates at angular velocity $\omega$.

### Step 2: Apply Angular Momentum Theorem (Euler's Turbine Equation)
The torque acting on the fluid equals the rate of change of angular momentum:

$$\tau = \dot{m}\left(r_2 V_{w2} - r_1 V_{w1}\right)$$

### Step 3: Convert Torque to Work per Unit Mass
Work done per unit mass (specific work) equals torque times angular velocity divided by mass flow rate:

$$W_{specific} = \dfrac{\tau \cdot \omega}{\dot{m}}$$

Since $u = r\omega$, substituting:

$$W_{specific} = \dfrac{\omega \cdot \dot{m}\left(r_2 V_{w2} - r_1 V_{w1}\right)}{\dot{m}} = u_2 V_{w2} - u_1 V_{w1}$$

### Step 4: Express in Terms of Head
Head developed is specific work per unit weight:

$$\boxed{H_{th} = \dfrac{u_2 V_{w2} - u_1 V_{w1}}{g}}$$

### Step 5: Special Case — Radial Inlet
At the inlet, fluid normally enters without pre-whirl, so $V_{w1} \approx 0$:

$$H_{th} = \dfrac{u_2 V_{w2}}{g}$$

If there is no slip and the blades are radial at outlet, $V_{w2} = u_2$:

$$H_{th,max} = \dfrac{u_2^2}{g}$$

## 3.2 Derivation: Isentropic Compression Work for a Reciprocating Compressor

### Step 1: Steady-Flow Energy Equation
For a compressor as an open system (control volume with one inlet, one outlet, neglecting kinetic and potential energy changes):

$$W_{in} = h_2 - h_1$$

### Step 2: For an Ideal Gas with Constant Specific Heats
For a reversible polytropic process $PV^n = $ constant:

$$\int_{P_1}^{P_2} V\,dP = \dfrac{n}{n-1}\,P_1V_1\left[\left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{(n-1)/n} - 1\right]$$

### Step 3: Integrating Polytropic Relation
From $PV^n = P_1V_1^n$, we get $V = P_1V_1^n / P^n$. Substituting in the integral:

$$\int_{P_1}^{P_2} V\,dP = \int_{P_1}^{P_2} \dfrac{P_1V_1^n}{P^n}\,dP = P_1V_1^n \int_{P_1}^{P_2} P^{-n}\,dP$$

$$= P_1V_1^n \left[\dfrac{P^{1-n}}{1-n}\right]_{P_1}^{P_2}$$

$$= \dfrac{P_1V_1^n}{1-n}\left[P_2^{1-n} - P_1^{1-n}\right]$$

### Step 4: Final Closed-Form Expression
Using $P_1V_1^n = P_2V_2^n$:

$$\boxed{W_{isen} = \dfrac{n}{n-1}\,P_1V_1\left[\left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{(n-1)/n} - 1\right]}$$

This is the **minimum possible work** of compression for the given pressure ratio. Real compressors always exceed this due to friction, leakage, and heat transfer.

## 3.3 Numerical Worked Example — KTU Past-Year Style

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** Always carry **4 significant figures** in intermediate steps. Marks are awarded for clarity of substitution, NOT for the final answer alone.

### Problem:
A single-stage reciprocating compressor compresses **0.5 kg/s** of air from **1 bar** to **8 bar**. The clearance ratio is **5 %**, and the compression follows the law $PV^{1.3} = $ constant. Calculate:
1. Volumetric efficiency
2. Indicated power required
3. Isentropic efficiency, assuming the actual compression index is **1.35**.
4. Comment on the green manufacturing implications.

**Given:** $\dot{m} = 0.5$ kg/s, $P_1 = 1$ bar $= 10^5$ Pa, $P_2 = 8$ bar $= 8 \times 10^5$ Pa, $C = 0.05$, $n_{polytropic} = 1.3$, $n_{actual} = 1.35$.

$R_{air} = 287$ J/(kg·K).

### Solution:

#### Part 1: Volumetric Efficiency

$$\eta_{vol} = 1 + C - C\left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{1/n}$$

Substituting:

$$\eta_{vol} = 1 + 0.05 - 0.05 \times \left(\dfrac{8}{1}\right)^{1/1.3}$$

Compute exponent: $1/1.3 = 0.7692$.

$(8)^{0.7692} = e^{0.7692 \times \ln 8} = e^{0.7692 \times 2.0794} = e^{1.5995} = 4.952$

Therefore:

$$\eta_{vol} = 1.05 - 0.05 \times 4.952 = 1.05 - 0.2476 = 0.8024$$

$$\boxed{\eta_{vol} \approx 80.24\,\%}$$

#### Part 2: Indicated Power (using $n = 1.3$)

Specific volume at inlet:

$$V_1 = \dfrac{R T_1}{P_1}$$

Assuming $T_1 = 300$ K:

$$V_1 = \dfrac{287 \times 300}{10^5} = 0.861\,\text{m}^3/\text{kg}$$

Specific work (polytropic):

$$W = \dfrac{n}{n-1}\,P_1V_1\left[\left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{(n-1)/n} - 1\right]$$

Exponent: $(n-1)/n = 0.3/1.3 = 0.2308$.

$(8)^{0.2308} = e^{0.2308 \times 2.0794} = e^{0.4799} = 1.6159$

$$W = \dfrac{1.3}{0.3} \times 10^5 \times 0.861 \times (1.6159 - 1)$$

$$W = 4.333 \times 10^5 \times 0.861 \times 0.6159$$

$$W = 4.333 \times 86100 \times 0.6159 \approx 229{,}780\,\text{J/kg}$$

Indicated power:

$$P_{ind} = \dot{m} \cdot W = 0.5 \times 229{,}780 = 114{,}890\,\text{W}$$

$$\boxed{P_{ind} \approx 114.9\,\text{kW}}$$

#### Part 3: Isentropic Efficiency

Isentropic work using $n = 1.4$ (isentropic for air):

Exponent: $(1.4-1)/1.4 = 0.4/1.4 = 0.2857$.

$(8)^{0.2857} = e^{0.2857 \times 2.0794} = e^{0.5941} = 1.8114$

$$W_{isen} = \dfrac{1.4}{0.4} \times 10^5 \times 0.861 \times (1.8114 - 1)$$

$$W_{isen} = 3.5 \times 86100 \times 0.8114$$

$$W_{isen} \approx 244{,}535\,\text{J/kg}$$

Isentropic efficiency:

$$\eta_{isen} = \dfrac{W_{isen}}{W_{actual}} = \dfrac{244{,}535}{229{,}780}$$

$$\boxed{\eta_{isen} \approx 1.064\,\,(\text{or }106.4\,\%)}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Why is $\eta_{isen} > 1$?** Because actual compression has a *higher* polytropic index (1.35 > 1.3), meaning the process is closer to isothermal than the assumed $n=1.3$. To correct: redefine reference work using actual $n=1.35$ or use $n=1.4$ for both. In exam answers, **state the assumption clearly** to score full marks.

#### Part 4: Green Manufacturing Comment

- The compressor consumes **~115 kW** continuously. At **₹8/kWh** and **8000 hours/year**, annual energy cost = **₹73.6 lakh/year**.
- Switching to a **two-stage compression with intercooling** would reduce work by **15–20 %**, saving **~₹12 lakh/year**.
- Installing a **Variable Speed Drive (VSD)** for part-load operations can save another **15 %**.
- Using a **screw compressor instead of reciprocating** improves isentropic efficiency by **3–5 %** with lower noise (better eco-labelling).

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: When to Use Which Machine?

| Parameter | Centrifugal Pump | Reciprocating Pump | Centrifugal Compressor | Reciprocating Compressor |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Pressure ratio capability | Up to 30 | Up to 100+ | Up to 20 | Up to 1000+ |
| Flow rate | High (1000+ m³/h) | Low–medium | High | Low–medium |
| Efficiency at design | 80–88 % | 85–92 % | 78–84 % | 80–90 % |
| Part-load efficiency | Good (flat) | Constant | Drops sharply | Constant |
| Maintenance | Low | High | Medium | High |
| Pulsation | None | High | None | High (needs pulsation dampener) |
| Green preference (variable flow) | ⭐⭐⭐ | ⭐ | ⭐⭐ | ⭐ |

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Functional Architecture of a Two-Stage Reciprocating Compressor (Green Configuration)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    AIR[AMBIENT AIR INTAKE] --> F1[INTAKE FILTER]
    F1 --> LPP[LOW PRESSURE CYLINDER LPC STAGE 1]
    LPP --> IC1[INTERCOOLER 1 HEAT RECOVERY HEAT EXCHANGER]
    IC1 --> LPC2[LOW PRESSURE CYLINDER LPC STAGE 2]
    LPC2 --> IC2[INTERCOOLER 2]
    IC2 --> HPC[HIGH PRESSURE CYLINDER HPC STAGE 3]
    HPC --> AR[AFTERCOOLER]
    AR --> RV[RECEIVER TANK]

    IC1 -.HEAT.-> WH1[HEATED WATER FOR BOILER FEED]

    classDef recovery fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#bf9000,color:#000
    class IC1,IC2,AR,WH1,ABS,DHW recovery
```

> **Interpretation:** Yellow-shaded blocks are **Waste Heat Recovery (WHR)** modules — a flagship green manufacturing strategy. By capturing the heat rejected during compression (which is otherwise dumped to the environment via cooling towers), the system achieves **near-zero thermal waste** and improves the plant's **Overall Energy Efficiency (OEE)**.

## 4.4 Pump Performance Characteristic Curve (Schematic)

```mermaid
graph LR
    P1[Head H axis] --> P2[Flow Q axis]
    Q0[Q at shutoff 0] --> HQ[HEAD Q DROOPING CURVE]
    Q1[Q at BEP] --> BEP[BEST EFFICIENCY POINT]
    Q2[Q max] --> END[END OF CURVE]
    PWR[POWER CURVE RISING] --- Q0
    EFF[EFFICIENCY CURVE PEAKED AT BEP] --- Q0
    BEP --- EFF

    classDef best fill:#b4c7e7,stroke:#1f4e79,color:#000
    class BEP best
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- **H–Q curve** falls from the shut-off head at zero flow to a lower head at maximum flow.
- **Power curve** rises from a low value (just the no-flow friction losses) to a maximum at the right.
- **Efficiency curve** is bell-shaped, peaking at the **BEP**. Always select a pump whose BEP matches the **most frequently operated duty point**.

## 4.5 Pump–System Curve Interaction (Operating Point Determination)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S[SYSTEM CURVE H = HS + kQ^2] --> OP[OPERATING POINT INTERSECTION]
    P[PUMP CURVE H = aQ^2 + bQ + c] --> OP
    OP --> QOP[DUTY POINT: Q_op HEAD H_op]
    OP --> POV[POWER AT OPERATING POINT]
    OP --> EFFV[EFFICIENCY AT OPERATING POINT]

    classDef op fill:#d9ead3,stroke:#38761d,color:#000
    class OP,QOV op
```

**Explanation:** The **operating point** of a pump in a piping system is determined by the intersection of the pump characteristic curve (provided by the manufacturer) and the system resistance curve (calculated from pipe geometry, fittings, elevation). Green manufacturing best practice: **shift the operating point to the BEP** by trimming the impeller, using a VFD, or selecting a more appropriately sized pump.

# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**"Distinguish clearly between a centrifugal pump and a positive displacement pump. State two applications of each."** (3 Marks)

#### Model Answer (3 Marks):
A **centrifugal pump** is a *rotodynamic* machine that imparts kinetic energy to the fluid using a rotating impeller and converts it into pressure in the volute casing. It delivers a *variable flow* at *relatively constant head* and is suited for **high-flow, low-to-medium head** applications such as **boiler feed in thermal power stations** and **municipal water supply**.

A **positive displacement pump** traps a *fixed volume* of fluid and forces it into the discharge line using reciprocating or rotary motion. It delivers a *constant flow* independent of pressure (up to a maximum), making it ideal for **high-pressure, low-flow** applications such as **hydraulic systems** and **oil pipelines**.

> **Mark Distribution:** 1 mark each for definition + distinguishing feature; 0.5 mark per application × 2.

---

### Question A2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**"Define volumetric efficiency of a reciprocating compressor. Why does it decrease with increasing pressure ratio?"** (3 Marks)

#### Model Answer (3 Marks):
**Volumetric efficiency** $\eta_{vol}$ is the ratio of the *actual volume of gas drawn in per cycle* to the *swept volume of the piston*.

$$\eta_{vol} = \dfrac{V_{actual}}{V_{swept}}$$

As the **pressure ratio $P_2/P_1$ increases**, the gas trapped in the clearance volume $V_c$ re-expands to a *larger* volume on the return stroke (since $V_{re-expand} = V_c(P_2/P_1)^{1/n}$), thereby **reducing the volume of fresh gas** that can be drawn in. Mathematically, $\eta_{vol} = 1 + C - C(P_2/P_1)^{1/n}$ — the second term grows with pressure ratio, reducing the overall efficiency. Hence **$\eta_{vol}$ decreases** with higher pressure ratios. (1.5 + 1.5 marks)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module Pattern:** Each Part B question carries **two sub-parts of 7 marks each**. The student answers **either** Question A **or** Question B.

---

### Question A. [KTU University Exam — May 2024] — 14 Marks

**Question A(a) [7 Marks]:** *A centrifugal pump delivers 0.05 m³/s of water against a head of 20 m. The pump runs at 1450 rpm with an overall efficiency of 75 %. Calculate (i) the power input to the pump in kW, and (ii) the specific speed. Comment on the type of pump based on specific speed.*

**Question A(b) [7 Marks]:** *With the help of a neat sketch, explain the working of a two-stage reciprocating air compressor with intercooling. State the green manufacturing benefit of intercooling.*

---

#### Model Answer — A(a):

**Given:** $Q = 0.05\,\text{m}^3/\text{s}$, $H = 20\,\text{m}$, $N = 1450\,\text{rpm}$, $\eta_{overall} = 0.75$, $\rho_{water} = 1000\,\text{kg/m}^3$.

**Step 1: Water Power**

$$P_{water} = \rho \cdot g \cdot Q \cdot H$$

$$P_{water} = 1000 \times 9.81 \times 0.05 \times 20 = 9810\,\text{W} = 9.81\,\text{kW}$$

**Step 2: Shaft (Brake) Power**

$$P_{shaft} = \dfrac{P_{water}}{\eta_{overall}} = \dfrac{9.81}{0.75} = 13.08\,\text{kW}$$

**Step 3: Specific Speed**

$$N_s = \dfrac{N\sqrt{Q}}{H^{3/4}}$$

$N_s = \dfrac{1450 \times \sqrt{0.05}}{(20)^{3/4}}$

$\sqrt{0.05} = 0.2236$

$(20)^{0.75} = e^{0.75 \times \ln 20} = e^{0.75 \times 2.9957} = e^{2.2468} = 9.457$

$$N_s = \dfrac{1450 \times 0.2236}{9.457} = \dfrac{324.22}{9.457} = 34.29 \approx 34.3$$

**Comment:** A specific speed of ~34 (in SI metric units) indicates a **radial-flow (centrifugal) pump**. As per the specific speed classification chart:
- $N_s < 30$: Radial flow (Francis-type impeller)
- $30 < N_s < 300$: Mixed flow
- $N_s > 300$: Axial flow

Hence this pump is a **radial-flow centrifugal pump**, suitable for medium head and moderate flow applications like HVAC circulation, boiler feed, and general water transfer.

---

#### Model Answer — A(b):

**Working of a Two-Stage Reciprocating Compressor with Intercooling:**

1. **Air Intake (Stage 1):** Atmospheric air is drawn into the Low-Pressure Cylinder (LPC) through an inlet filter during the suction stroke. It is compressed from $P_1$ (atmospheric) to an intermediate pressure $P_i$.

2. **Intercooling:** The hot, compressed air leaving the LPC passes through an **intercooler** — a heat exchanger that reduces its temperature back to (approximately) the original inlet temperature $T_1$. This is critical because:
   - The density of cooled air is higher, so a smaller volume of air enters the next stage for the same mass flow.
   - The work required in Stage 2 is reduced (since cooler inlet requires less compression work).

3. **Second-Stage Compression:** The cooled air enters the High-Pressure Cylinder (HPC) and is compressed from $P_i$ to the final discharge pressure $P_2$.

4. **Discharge and Aftercooling:** High-pressure air leaves the HPC, passes through an aftercooler (if fitted), and is delivered to the receiver tank.

**Green Manufacturing Benefit of Intercooling:**
- **Energy savings of 10–20 %** compared to single-stage compression at the same pressure ratio.
- **Reduced carbon footprint** of compressed air generation (compressed air accounts for ~10 % of industrial electricity globally).
- **Enables heat recovery** from the intercooler — captured heat can be used for space heating, water heating, or absorption cooling.
- **Extends equipment life** by reducing thermal stress on cylinder walls and valves.

---

### Question B. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] — 14 Marks

**Question B(a) [7 Marks]:** *A single-stage reciprocating compressor takes in air at 1 bar and 30 °C, and delivers it at 8 bar. The clearance ratio is 4 % and the compression follows PV^1.3 = constant. The compressor delivers 0.4 m³ of free air per minute. Calculate: (i) the swept volume, (ii) the volumetric efficiency, and (iii) the power required to drive the compressor.*

**Question B(b) [7 Marks]:** *Explain the Affinity Laws of a centrifugal pump. How do these laws support green manufacturing in variable-load pumping applications?*

---

#### Model Answer — B(a):

**Given:** $P_1 = 1\,\text{bar} = 10^5\,\text{Pa}$, $T_1 = 303\,\text{K}$, $P_2 = 8\,\text{bar} = 8 \times 10^5\,\text{Pa}$, $C = 0.04$, $n = 1.3$, $V_{FAD} = 0.4\,\text{m}^3/\text{min}$, $R = 287\,\text{J/(kg·K)}$.

**Step 1: Swept Volume**

Volumetric efficiency:

$$\eta_{vol} = 1 + C - C\left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{1/n} = 1.04 - 0.04 \times (8)^{1/1.3}$$

$1/1.3 = 0.7692$

$(8)^{0.7692} = 4.952$ (from Section 3.3 calculation)

$$\eta_{vol} = 1.04 - 0.04 \times 4.952 = 1.04 - 0.1981 = 0.8419 \approx 84.19\,\%$$

Swept volume:

$$V_s = \dfrac{V_{FAD}}{\eta_{vol}} = \dfrac{0.4}{0.8419} = 0.4751\,\text{m}^3/\text{min} = 0.00792\,\text{m}^3/\text{s}$$

$$\boxed{V_s \approx 0.00792\,\text{m}^3/\text{s}\,\,(0.4751\,\text{m}^3/\text{min})}$$

**Step 2: Mass Flow Rate**

Inlet specific volume:

$$v_1 = \dfrac{RT_1}{P_1} = \dfrac{287 \times 303}{10^5} = 0.8696\,\text{m}^3/\text{kg}$$

$$\dot{m} = \dfrac{V_{FAD}}{60 \times v_1} = \dfrac{0.4}{60 \times 0.8696} = 0.00767\,\text{kg/s}$$

**Step 3: Power Required**

$$W_{specific} = \dfrac{n}{n-1} P_1 v_1 \left[\left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{(n-1)/n} - 1\right]$$

$(n-1)/n = 0.2308$, $(8)^{0.2308} = 1.6159$ (from Section 3.3)

$$W_{specific} = \dfrac{1.3}{0.3} \times 10^5 \times 0.8696 \times (1.6159 - 1)$$

$$W_{specific} = 4.333 \times 10^5 \times 0.8696 \times 0.6159$$

$$W_{specific} \approx 232{,}030\,\text{J/kg}$$

Power:

$$P = \dot{m} \cdot W_{specific} = 0.00767 \times 232{,}030$$

$$\boxed{P \approx 1779.7\,\text{W} \approx 1.78\,\text{kW}}$$

---

#### Model Answer — B(b):

**Affinity Laws (Centrifugal Pump):**

When the operating speed $N$ of a centrifugal pump is changed (or the impeller diameter $D$ is trimmed), the flow $Q$, head $H$, and power $P$ change according to three mathematical relationships:

$$Q \propto N \quad ; \quad H \propto N^2 \quad ; \quad P \propto N^3$$

Or in terms of impeller diameter (geometrically similar pumps):

$$Q \propto D \quad ; \quad H \propto D^2 \quad ; \quad P \propto D^3$$

**Green Manufacturing Implications in Variable-Load Applications:**

In most industrial pumping systems (HVAC, water distribution, process plants), the **load is rarely constant**. Traditional flow control uses a **throttling valve** — which artificially increases the system resistance, wasting energy in the form of pressure drop across the valve.

Using the Affinity Laws via a **Variable Frequency Drive (VFD)**:

| Speed Reduction | Flow Reduction | Power Reduction | Energy Saved |
|---|---|---|---|
| 10 % | 10 % | 27.1 % | 27.1 % |
| 25 % | 25 % | 57.8 % | 57.8 % |
| 50 % | 50 % | 87.5 % | 87.5 % |

This **cubic relationship between power and speed** is the cornerstone of green pump selection. Industry case studies report **30–50 %** annual energy savings by replacing throttle valves with VFDs — directly translating to reduced CO₂ emissions, lower electricity bills, and shorter payback periods (typically < 2 years).

Additionally, **soft starting** via VFDs reduces inrush current by 4–6×, lowering peak demand charges and extending motor insulation life.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert units** in compressor problems — students often use $P_1 = 1$ bar without converting to Pa, leading to answers off by a factor of $10^5$. **Always convert to SI (Pa) before calculation.**
> 2. **Confusing isentropic and polytropic indices** — use $n = 1.4$ for isentropic (adiabatic, reversible) of air; use $n$ as given for polytropic. Clearly state which one is being used.
> 3. **Misapplying Affinity Laws** — they apply ONLY to **dynamic (rotodynamic) machines**, NOT to positive displacement pumps/compressors. Writing them for a reciprocating compressor will fetch zero marks.
> 4. **Missing the green manufacturing angle** — KTU 2024 scheme questions explicitly award marks for linking a numerical answer to its environmental/energy-saving implication. Always add a concluding green statement.
> 5. **Skipping BEP mention** in pump selection — evaluators explicitly look for "Best Efficiency Point" identification when commenting on pump suitability.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Final 5-Minute Revision Before Entering the KTU Exam Hall — Memorise These Bullets.**

- [ ] **Pumps** handle incompressible fluids; **compressors** handle compressible gases. Fans and blowers are lower-pressure cousins of compressors.
- [ ] **Centrifugal pumps** → high flow, moderate head, best for continuous duty. **Reciprocating pumps** → high pressure, low flow, best for viscous/dirty fluids.
- [ ] **Centrifugal compressors** → high flow, moderate pressure ratio (< 8). **Reciprocating compressors** → high pressure ratio (> 8), low flow.
- [ ] **Affinity Laws**: $Q \propto N$, $H \propto N^2$, $P \propto N^3$ — only for rotodynamic machines.
- [ ] **Specific speed** classifies pumps: $<30$ radial, $30$–$300$ mixed, $>300$ axial.
- [ ] **Volumetric efficiency** decreases with pressure ratio due to clearance volume re-expansion.
- [ ] **Isentropic efficiency** of a compressor is the ratio of ideal (reversible adiabatic) work to actual work.
- [ ] **Two-stage compression with intercooling** saves **10–20 %** energy versus single-stage — a top KTU green manufacturing topic.
- [ ] **VFD-based speed control** saves up to **50 %** energy versus throttling — anchored on the cubic Affinity Law.
- [ ] **Heat recovery** from intercoolers and aftercoolers converts waste heat into useful thermal energy — cornerstone of green compressor design.
- [ ] Always convert pressures to **Pa (SI units)** and temperatures to **K** before plugging into formulas.
- [ ] The **Isentropic Process** uses $n = 1.4$ for air; the **Polytropic Process** uses the given $n$ (typically $1.25$–$1.35$).
- [ ] **Pump power formula**: $P = \rho g Q H / \eta_{overall}$ — remember the denominator; forgetting efficiency loses 2 marks.
- [ ] **Green audit indicators**: SEC (specific energy consumption), RI (recyclability index), MTBO (mean time between overhauls), $L_{WA}$ (sound power).
- [ ] **Sankey diagram understanding**: A 75 % efficient pump feeding 100 kW of shaft power delivers 75 kW to the fluid and rejects 25 kW as heat — this 25 kW is recoverable in green designs.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Line Exam Tip:** In numerical answers, always box the final value and **bold the unit**. In descriptive answers, structure your response as **Definition → Principle → Formula → Green Relevance** to ensure every KTU marking tier is addressed.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Performance Evaluation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEIET753 - GREEN MANUFACTURING > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Performance Evaluation # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Performance Evaluation** in Green Manufacturing is defined as the systematic, quantitative, and qualitative assessment of a manufacturing system, process, or product against predefined environmental, economic, and operational benchmarks. It involves measuring resource efficiency, waste generation, energy consumption, emissions, and social impact across the entire product life cycle to determine the degree of "greenness" or sustainability of the manufacturing operation. According to the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for **PEIET753 – Green Manufacturing**, Performance Evaluation forms the critical feedback loop that transforms green manufacturing from a conceptual philosophy into a measurable, controllable engineering discipline. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):** Performance Evaluation is the backbone of any green manufacturing initiative. Without quantifiable metrics, sustainable practices cannot be benchmarked, audited, certified (e.g., **ISO 14001**), or improved upon. ## 1.2 Key Terminology Decoded | Term | Working Definition (KTU Context) | |---|---| | **Benchmarking** | The process of comparing one's business processes and performance metrics to industry best practices. | | **KPI (Key Performance Indicator)** | A quantifiable measure used to evaluate the success of an organization in meeting specific green objectives. | | **EPI (Environmental Performance Indicator)** | A specific type of KPI that provides information about an organization's environmental performance. | | **LCA (Life Cycle Assessment)** | A cradle-to-grave analysis technique to assess environmental impacts associated with all stages of a product's life. | | **Eco-Efficiency** | The ratio of environmental performance to economic performance; doing more with less. | | **Carbon Footprint (CF)** | The total greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions caused directly and indirectly by an organization, event, or product. | ## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine you bought a new car and you want to know if it is truly "fuel-efficient." You do not just look at the brochure; you actually track how many **kilometers per liter** it gives over a month, measure the **CO2 emissions from the exhaust**, check how much **waste oil** is generated during servicing, and see how **recyclable** the parts are when scrapped. Performance Evaluation in Green Manufacturing works exactly the same way. A factory claims to be "green," but a Performance Evaluation uses hard numbers — like **kg of waste per unit produced**, **kWh consumed per kg of output**, or **liters of water reused per cycle** — to verify that claim. Think of it as the **"health report card"** of a manufacturing system. Just as a doctor measures blood pressure, cholesterol, and sugar to evaluate your health, a green manufacturing engineer measures energy, emissions, and waste to evaluate the plant's ecological health. > [!NOTE] > **Core Idea:** If you cannot measure it, you cannot manage it. Performance Evaluation converts the abstract idea of "sustainability" into concrete engineering numbers. ## 1.4 Significance in Green Manufacturing The primary objectives of Performance Evaluation are: 1. **Quantification:** Converting qualitative green initiatives (e.g., "we recycle") into measurable metrics. 2. **Compliance:** Meeting regulatory standards such as the **Cleaner Production Act** and international standards. 3. **Continuous Improvement:** Establishing a baseline so that **reduce, reuse, recycle (3R)** strategies can be tracked and verified. 4. **Certification:** Providing documented evidence for green certifications like **ISO 14001**, **ISO 50001**, or **LEED**. 5. **Stakeholder Communication:** Reporting to investors, customers, and government bodies in standardized formats. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Three Pillars of Green Performance Evaluation Performance Evaluation is not a single test; it is a multi-dimensional framework built on **three pillars** as defined by the **Brundtland Commission** and adopted by KTU 2024: 1. **Environmental Performance** (Planet): Pollution, emissions, waste, resource depletion. 2. **Economic Performance** (Profit): Cost savings from efficiency, market share of green products, ROI on green investments. 3. **Social Performance** (People): Worker safety, community impact, fair labor. ## 2.2 Hierarchical Levels of Evaluation Performance Evaluation operates at four hierarchical levels within a manufacturing enterprise: - **Level 1 — Process Level:** Evaluation of a single machine or workstation (e.g., CNC machine energy efficiency). - **Level 2 — Facility/Plant Level:** Evaluation of an entire factory's environmental footprint. - **Level 3 — Corporate Level:** Evaluation across multiple facilities of the same company. - **Level 4 — Product/Supply Chain Level:** Evaluation across the entire life cycle of a product (cradle-to-grave). ## 2.3 Core Performance Metrics — Detailed Breakdown ### A. Energy Efficiency Metrics Energy is the most direct and easily measurable resource. Key metrics include: - **Specific Energy Consumption (SEC):** Energy used per unit of output. - **Energy Productivity:** Economic value generated per unit of energy consumed. - **Renewable Energy Share:** Percentage of total energy from renewable sources. ### B. Material Efficiency Metrics - **Material Utilization Ratio:** Useful material in finished product versus raw material input. - **Scrap Rate:** Percentage of input material that becomes waste. - **Recycled Content Ratio:** Proportion of recycled material in the final product. ### C. Waste & Emission Metrics - **Waste Intensity:** Waste generated per unit of production. - **Hazardous Waste Ratio:** Proportion of toxic waste in total waste stream. - **Volatile Organic Compound (VOC) Emissions:** Air pollutants released per unit. ### D. Water Efficiency Metrics - **Water Consumption Ratio:** Liters of fresh water consumed per unit of product. - **Water Recycling Index:** Percentage of process water reused. ### E. Economic Indicators - **Green Investment ROI:** Return on investment from green technology adoption. - **Cost of Poor Quality (COPQ) — Environmental:** Penalty costs from environmental non-compliance. ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Yield Table for KTU University Exam:** Memorize the units and the direction of the ratio (higher is better for efficiency, lower is better for waste/emissions). | # | Metric Name | Formula | Units | Direction | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Specific Energy Consumption (SEC) | $SEC = \frac{E_{total}}{N_{units}}$ | kWh/unit | Lower is better | | 2 | Energy Productivity (EP) | $EP = \frac{V_{output}}{E_{total}}$ | ₹ / kWh | Higher is better | | 3 | Material Utilization Ratio (MUR) | $MUR = \frac{M_{product}}{M_{input}} \times 100$ | % | Higher is better | | 4 | Scrap Rate (SR) | $SR = \frac{M_{scrap}}{M_{input}} \times 100$ | % | Lower is better | | 5 | Waste Intensity (WI) | $WI = \frac{W_{total}}{N_{units}}$ | kg/unit | Lower is better | | 6 | Water Recycling Index (WRI) | $WRI = \frac{W_{recycled}}{W_{total}} \times 100$ | % | Higher is better | | 7 | Carbon Footprint (CF) | $CF = \sum (A_i \times EF_i)$ | kg CO₂e | Lower is better | | 8 | Eco-Efficiency Index (EEI) | $EEI = \frac{V_{product/service}}{E_{environmental\,impact}}$ | ₹ / kg waste | Higher is better | | 9 | Recycled Content (RC) | $RC = \frac{M_{recycled}}{M_{total}} \times 100$ | % | Higher is better | | 10 | Hazardous Waste Ratio (HWR) | $HWR = \frac{W_{hazard}}{W_{total}} \times 100$ | % | Lower is better | > **Notation Key:** $E_{total}$ = Total energy consumed; $N_{units}$ = Number of units produced; $V_{output}$ = Monetary value of output; $M$ = Mass; $W$ = Waste; $A_i$ = Activity data for source $i$; $EF_i$ = Emission factor for source $i$. ## 2.5 Real-World Utility In real-world engineering, these formulas are used to: 1. **Compare manufacturing routes:** Selecting between CNC machining and additive manufacturing based on SEC. 2. **Audit ISO 14001 compliance:** Generating verified reports for environmental management systems. 3. **Design for Environment (DfE):** Helping product designers choose materials with better MUR and lower CF. 4. **Government reporting:** Submitting data to the **Central Pollution Control Board (CPCB)** in India. 5. **Lean-Green integration:** Combining **Kaizen** (lean) metrics with environmental KPIs. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1: Specific Energy Consumption (SEC) **Conceptual Basis:** SEC measures the energy intensity of a manufacturing process. It is the most fundamental metric in green manufacturing evaluation because almost every environmental burden (emissions, water, waste) can be traced back to energy use. **Starting Premise:** Let the total energy consumed by a manufacturing process over a billing/operational period be $E_{total}$ measured in **kWh**. Let the number of finished products produced in the same period be $N_{units}$. **Logical Construction:** $$ \text{Energy per unit} \;=\; \frac{\text{Total Energy}}{\text{Total Units Produced}} $$ $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Step 1:} \quad & \text{Define total energy input as } E_{total} = E_{electricity} + E_{fuel} + E_{steam} \\ \text{Step 2:} \quad & \text{Define production output as } N_{units} \text{ (dimensionless count)} \\ \text{Step 3:} \quad & \text{Form the ratio to normalize energy against production volume} \\ \text{Step 4:} \quad & \text{Apply unit conversion if multiple energy forms are used:} \\ & E_{total} \; (kWh) \;=\; E_{electricity} \; (kWh) \;+\; E_{fuel} \; (L) \times CV_{fuel} \times \eta_{boiler} \end{aligned} $$ **Final Working Equation:** $$ SEC = \frac{E_{total}}{N_{units}} $$ **Engineering Interpretation:** A reduction in SEC over successive quarters indicates successful energy conservation. It is the **cornerstone metric** for any plant pursuing **ISO 50001 (Energy Management)** certification. --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2: Carbon Footprint (CF) — Activity-Based Method **Conceptual Basis:** Carbon footprint quantifies the total greenhouse gases (expressed as **CO₂ equivalents, CO₂e**) released by a process. The IPCC Tier 1 method uses emission factors. **Starting Premise:** A manufacturing process consumes multiple types of resources — electricity, diesel, natural gas. Each resource type has a known **emission factor (EF)** which converts the activity data (liters, kWh, m³) into kg of CO₂e. $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Step 1:} \quad & \text{Identify all emission sources: } i = 1, 2, 3, \dots, n \\ \text{Step 2:} \quad & \text{Collect activity data } A_i \text{ for each source (e.g., kWh of electricity)} \\ \text{Step 3:} \quad & \text{Look up emission factor } EF_i \text{ (e.g., for Indian grid, EF} \approx 0.82 \; kg \; CO_2/kWh) \\ \text{Step 4:} \quad & \text{Multiply activity by emission factor for each source} \\ \text{Step 5:} \quad & \text{Sum across all sources to obtain total Carbon Footprint} \end{aligned} $$ **Final Working Equation:** $$ CF_{total} \;=\; \sum_{i=1}^{n} A_i \times EF_i $$ **Worked Numerical Example:** A factory uses **10,000 kWh** of grid electricity ($EF_{grid} = 0.82 \; kg \; CO_2/kWh$) and **500 liters** of diesel ($EF_{diesel} = 2.68 \; kg \; CO_2/L$) in one month. $$ \begin{aligned} CF_{electricity} &= 10{,}000 \times 0.82 \;=\; 8{,}200 \; kg \; CO_2e \\ CF_{diesel} &= 500 \times 2.68 \;=\; 1{,}340 \; kg \; CO_2e \\ CF_{total} &= 8{,}200 + 1{,}340 \;=\; 9{,}540 \; kg \; CO_2e \end{aligned} $$ If 2,000 units were produced: $$ CF_{per\;unit} = \frac{9{,}540}{2{,}000} = 4.77 \; kg \; CO_2e / unit $$ --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3: Eco-Efficiency Index (EEI) **Conceptual Basis:** Coined by the **World Business Council for Sustainable Development (WBCSD)**, eco-efficiency is the core philosophy of green manufacturing — deliver more value with less environmental impact. **Starting Premise:** Let $V_{product/service}$ = Value added (in ₹ or any monetary unit). Let $E_{environmental\,impact}$ = Total environmental burden (in kg of waste, or kg CO₂e, or kg of resource consumed). **Logical Construction:** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Step 1:} \quad & \text{Quantify economic value generated by the process.} \\ \text{Step 2:} \quad & \text{Quantify total environmental burden (aggregated across all categories).} \\ \text{Step 3:} \quad & \text{Form the ratio. An eco-efficient process maximizes value while minimizing impact.} \\ \text{Step 4:} \quad & \text{For valid comparison, normalize both numerator and denominator to "per unit of product."} \end{aligned} $$ **Final Working Equation:** $$ EEI = \frac{V_{product/service}}{E_{environmental\,impact}} $$ **Worked Numerical Example:** A green machining cell produces components worth **₹5,00,000** while generating **50 kg** of total waste (chips + coolant + sludge). $$ EEI = \frac{5{,}00{,}000}{50} = 10{,}000 \; ₹ / kg \; of \; waste $$ If a conventional cell produces the same value but generates **200 kg** of waste: $$ EEI_{conventional} = \frac{5{,}00{,}000}{200} = 2{,}500 \; ₹ / kg \; of \; waste $$ **Conclusion:** The green cell is **4 times more eco-efficient** than the conventional cell. --- ## 3.4 Derivation 4: Material Utilization Ratio (MUR) with Scrap Analysis **Starting Premise:** A raw material input of mass $M_{input}$ enters the process. The finished product has mass $M_{product}$. The difference is scrap $M_{scrap} = M_{input} - M_{product}$. **Logical Construction:** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Step 1:} \quad & \text{Define } M_{input} = \text{mass of raw stock (e.g., billet before machining)} \\ \text{Step 2:} \quad & \text{Define } M_{product} = \text{mass of finished component} \\ \text{Step 3:} \quad & \text{By conservation of mass: } M_{input} = M_{product} + M_{scrap} \\ \text{Step 4:} \quad & \text{Express utilization as a percentage ratio for benchmarking.} \end{aligned} $$ **Final Working Equations:** $$ MUR = \frac{M_{product}}{M_{input}} \times 100 \;\% \qquad \qquad SR = \frac{M_{scrap}}{M_{input}} \times 100 \;\% \;=\; (100 - MUR) \;\% $$ **Worked Numerical Example:** A steel billet of **10 kg** is machined into a finished gear of **6.5 kg**. $$ \begin{aligned} M_{scrap} &= 10 - 6.5 \;=\; 3.5 \; kg \\ MUR &= \frac{6.5}{10} \times 100 \;=\; 65 \;\% \\ SR &= 35 \;\% \quad \text{(35\% of expensive steel became chips)} \end{aligned} $$ **Engineering Insight:** Near-Net Shape (NNS) manufacturing like forging or additive manufacturing can push MUR above **90%**, dramatically reducing material waste. --- ## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Metric Selection Guide > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Tip:** In the 14-mark question, examiners often ask "select appropriate metrics." Use this matrix as a decision-making aid. | Manufacturing Concern | Primary Metric | Secondary Metric | Benchmarking Standard | |---|---|---|---| | High electricity bills | Specific Energy Consumption | Energy Productivity | ISO 50001 | | Excessive material waste | Material Utilization Ratio | Scrap Rate | ISO 14001 | | High CO₂ emissions | Carbon Footprint | Emission Intensity | GHG Protocol | | Water scarcity | Water Recycling Index | Water Consumption Ratio | CDP Water Disclosure | | Toxic waste concerns | Hazardous Waste Ratio | Waste Intensity | Basel Convention | | Community complaints | VOC Emissions | Noise & Dust Levels | CPCB Norms | | Green marketing claims | Eco-Efficiency Index | Recycled Content Ratio | ISO 14021 | # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Hierarchical Framework of Performance Evaluation The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the **four-level hierarchy** of performance evaluation in a green manufacturing enterprise, along with the metrics used at each level. ```mermaid graph TD L4["LEVEL 4: PRODUCT LIFE CYCLE
Cradle to Grave Analysis
Metric: LCA Impact Score"] L3["LEVEL 3: CORPORATE LEVEL
Multi-Plant Aggregated KPIs
Metric: Corporate Carbon Footprint"] L2["LEVEL 2: FACILITY OR PLANT LEVEL
Whole Factory Footprint
Metric: Plant SEC, Plant CF"] L1["LEVEL 1: PROCESS OR MACHINE LEVEL
Single Machine Workstation
Metric: Machine SEC, MUR"] L4 --> L3 L3 --> L2 L2 --> L1 L1 --> F1["Feedback Loop:
Energy, Material, Waste Data"] F1 --> L2 F1 --> L3 F1 --> L4 L4 --> R1["External Reporting:
ISO 14001, GRI Standards"] L3 --> R1 L2 --> R1 L1 --> R1 ``` ## 4.2 The PDCA-Based Evaluation Cycle Performance Evaluation is not a one-time audit; it is a continuous cycle aligned with the **Plan-Do-Check-Act (PDCA)** framework of ISO 14001. ```mermaid graph LR P["PLAN
Define KPIs
Set Baselines
Choose Metrics"] D["DO
Collect Activity Data
Run Processes
Log Energy and Material"] C["CHECK
Compute Metrics
SEC, MUR, CF, EEI
Compare with Targets"] A["ACT
Correct Deviations
Implement Improvements
Update Baselines"] P --> D D --> C C --> A A --> P ``` ## 4.3 Triple Bottom Line Evaluation Model The classic sustainability model evaluates performance across three interconnected dimensions. ```mermaid graph TD TBL["GREEN PERFORMANCE EVALUATION
Triple Bottom Line Framework"] TBL --> E["ENVIRONMENTAL DIMENSION
Energy, Emissions, Waste, Water
Metrics: SEC, CF, WI"] TBL --> ECO["ECONOMIC DIMENSION
Cost Savings, ROI, Market Share
Metrics: EEI, Green ROI"] TBL --> S["SOCIAL DIMENSION
Worker Safety, Community Impact
Metrics: Lost Time Injury Frequency"] E --> R["Aggregated Green Performance Score"] ECO --> R S --> R R --> DEC["Strategic Decision:
Invest, Certify, Improve, Discontinue"] ``` ## 4.4 Data Flow Architecture for Performance Evaluation This diagram shows how raw operational data is transformed into actionable green performance intelligence. ```mermaid graph TD SRC1["Smart Energy Meters"] SRC2["Material Weighing Scales"] SRC3["Waste Sorting Sensors"] SRC4["Water Flow Meters"] SRC5["Air Quality Monitors"] SRC1 --> DAQ["DATA ACQUISITION LAYER
IoT Sensors and SCADA"] SRC2 --> DAQ SRC3 --> DAQ SRC4 --> DAQ SRC5 --> DAQ DAQ --> DB["CENTRAL DATABASE
Time Series Storage"] DB --> ENG["ANALYTICS ENGINE
Apply Formulas:
SEC, MUR, CF, EEI"] ENG --> DASH["REAL TIME DASHBOARD
KPIs and Charts"] DASH --> MGT["MANAGEMENT REVIEW
Monthly Sustainability Meeting"] ENG --> REP["REGULATORY REPORTS
ISO 14001, GRI, CPCB"] ENG --> BEN["BENCHMARKING
Compare with Industry Peers"] ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper 2]` **Define Specific Energy Consumption (SEC) and state its significance in evaluating green manufacturing performance.** **Model Answer:** Specific Energy Consumption (SEC) is the ratio of total energy consumed by a manufacturing process to the number of units produced in a given period. $$ SEC = \frac{E_{total}}{N_{units}} \quad [units: kWh/unit] $$ **Significance:** 1. It quantifies the **energy intensity** of a process, which directly correlates with CO₂ emissions and operating cost. 2. It serves as the primary KPI for **ISO 50001 (Energy Management Systems)** certification. 3. Reduction in SEC over time provides direct evidence of successful **Cleaner Production** initiatives. 4. It enables fair **benchmarking** between different machines, shifts, or plants. --- ### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Supplementary]` **What is an Eco-Efficiency Index? Why is it considered the most holistic green manufacturing metric?** **Model Answer:** Eco-Efficiency Index (EEI) is defined by the World Business Council for Sustainable Development (WBCSD) as: $$ EEI = \frac{\text{Value of Product or Service}}{\text{Environmental Impact}} $$ **Why it is the most holistic metric:** 1. It simultaneously captures **economic performance** (numerator) and **environmental performance** (denominator). 2. It aligns with the **Brundtland definition** of sustainable development — meeting present needs without compromising the future. 3. A single composite number allows **easy communication** to non-technical stakeholders and top management. 4. It inherently rewards innovation: any process improvement that either raises value OR reduces impact improves EEI. --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) ### Question A (Choice 1): `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Main Exam]` **Q. (a)** Explain in detail the **hierarchical framework of Performance Evaluation** in green manufacturing. Describe at least **three specific metrics** used at the process level with their formulas. **Q. (b)** A CNC machining cell produces **500 components per shift**. The total electricity consumed is **1,200 kWh** and **50 liters of diesel** is used for the standby generator (energy content = 36 MJ/L, boiler efficiency for equivalent = 30%, 1 kWh = 3.6 MJ). The input billet mass is **2.5 kg/unit** and finished component mass is **1.8 kg/unit**. Calculate: (i) Specific Energy Consumption (ii) Material Utilization Ratio (iii) Scrap Rate (iv) Comment on the greenness of the cell. **Model Answer:** **(a) Hierarchical Framework & Process-Level Metrics:** Performance Evaluation in green manufacturing operates at **four hierarchical levels**: - **Level 1 — Process/Machine Level:** Focuses on individual workstations. Metrics include Specific Energy Consumption (SEC), Material Utilization Ratio (MUR), and Scrap Rate (SR). - **Level 2 — Facility/Plant Level:** Aggregates process data into plant-wide KPIs like Plant Carbon Footprint and Total Waste Generated. - **Level 3 — Corporate Level:** Combines multiple plants for strategic ESG reporting. - **Level 4 — Product Life Cycle Level:** Full cradle-to-grave assessment using LCA methodology. **Three Process-Level Metrics with Formulas:** 1. **Specific Energy Consumption:** $$ SEC = \frac{E_{total}}{N_{units}} \quad [kWh/unit] $$ 2. **Material Utilization Ratio:** $$ MUR = \frac{M_{product}}{M_{input}} \times 100 \quad [\%] $$ 3. **Scrap Rate:** $$ SR = \frac{M_{scrap}}{M_{input}} \times 100 \;=\; (100 - MUR) \quad [\%] $$ **(b) Numerical Solution:** **Step 1: Convert diesel energy to kWh.** $$ \begin{aligned} E_{diesel\;(MJ)} &= 50 \; L \times 36 \; MJ/L \times 0.30 \;=\; 540 \; MJ \\ E_{diesel\;(kWh)} &= \frac{540}{3.6} \;=\; 150 \; kWh \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2: Compute total energy.** $$ E_{total} = 1{,}200 + 150 = 1{,}350 \; kWh $$ **(i) Specific Energy Consumption:** $$ SEC = \frac{1{,}350}{500} = 2.70 \; kWh/unit $$ **Step 3: Compute mass balance.** $$ \begin{aligned} M_{input} &= 500 \times 2.5 = 1{,}250 \; kg \\ M_{product} &= 500 \times 1.8 = 900 \; kg \\ M_{scrap} &= 1{,}250 - 900 = 350 \; kg \end{aligned} $$ **(ii) Material Utilization Ratio:** $$ MUR = \frac{900}{1{,}250} \times 100 = 72 \; \% $$ **(iii) Scrap Rate:** $$ SR = 100 - 72 = 28 \; \% $$ **(iv) Comment on Greenness:** The cell consumes **2.70 kWh/unit** and discards **28%** of its input material. The MUR of 72% is below the industry benchmark of **85–90%** for precision machining. The cell has significant scope for improvement through **near-net-shape processes**, **chip recycling**, and **energy-efficient CNC machines (with VFD drives)**. A rough estimate suggests that 350 kg of steel scrap is being landfilled per shift instead of being sold as recyclable material. --- ### Question B (Choice 2): `[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Supplementary]` **Q. (a)** Discuss the **Environmental Performance Indicators (EPIs)** under the categories of: (i) Operational indicators, (ii) Management indicators, and (iii) Environmental condition indicators. Give **two examples** for each category. **Q. (b)** A factory produces **1,000 units/month** and generates an economic value of **₹15,00,000/month**. Total environmental burden is: **8,000 kg CO₂e** emissions, **500 kg solid waste**, and **2,000 kWh** of energy consumed. Compute: (i) Carbon Footprint per unit (ii) Eco-Efficiency Index (using CF as impact) (iii) If the company invests in solar power and reduces energy consumption to **1,000 kWh** (and consequently emissions to **4,000 kg CO₂e**), what is the **percentage improvement in EEI**? **Model Answer:** **(a) Environmental Performance Indicators (EPIs):** EPIs are classified into three categories by the **ISO 14031 standard**: **(i) Operational Performance Indicators (OPIs):** These measure the environmental performance of an organization's operations. - Examples: Specific Energy Consumption, Water Consumption Ratio, Waste Intensity. **(ii) Management Performance Indicators (MPIs):** These measure management's efforts to influence operational environmental performance. - Examples: Number of environmental training hours per employee, Percentage of suppliers audited for green compliance, Budget spent on R&D for cleaner production. **(iii) Environmental Condition Indicators (ECIs):** These measure the local, regional, or global environmental quality affected by the organization. - Examples: Ambient air quality index around the plant, Concentration of heavy metals in nearby water bodies, Local groundwater table depletion rate. **(b) Numerical Solution:** **Given:** $N_{units} = 1{,}000$; $V_{output} = ₹15{,}00{,}000$; $CF_{total} = 8{,}000 \; kg \; CO_2e$. **(i) Carbon Footprint per unit:** $$ CF_{per\;unit} = \frac{8{,}000}{1{,}000} = 8.0 \; kg \; CO_2e / unit $$ **(ii) Eco-Efficiency Index (Baseline):** $$ EEI_{baseline} = \frac{15{,}00{,}000}{8{,}000} = 187.5 \; ₹ / kg \; CO_2e $$ **After Solar Investment:** **(iii) New Carbon Footprint = 4,000 kg CO₂e.** $$ EEI_{new} = \frac{15{,}00{,}000}{4{,}000} = 375 \; ₹ / kg \; CO_2e $$ **Percentage Improvement in EEI:** $$ \% \; Improvement = \frac{EEI_{new} - EEI_{baseline}}{EEI_{baseline}} \times 100 $$ $$ \begin{aligned} \% \; Improvement &= \frac{375 - 187.5}{187.5} \times 100 \\ &= \frac{187.5}{187.5} \times 100 \\ &= 100 \; \% \end{aligned} $$ **Conclusion:** The solar investment **doubled** the eco-efficiency of the factory. The plant now creates twice the value per unit of carbon emitted, which is a textbook example of **decoupling economic growth from environmental impact** — the central thesis of green manufacturing. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes Where KTU Students Lose Marks:** > 1. **Unit Inconsistency:** Mixing kWh with MJ without conversion. KTU examiners specifically check unit consistency and deduct **1–2 marks** for mismatched units. > 2. **Forgetting to Convert Percentage:** When asked for "ratio," some students write a percentage. Always re-read the question's exact phrasing. > 3. **Missing Mass Balance:** In material utilization problems, students often forget that $M_{input} = M_{product} + M_{scrap}$. They compute MUR without verifying the mass balance, leading to logically inconsistent answers. > 4. **Ignoring "per unit" Normalization:** Always normalize by $N_{units}$ when comparing processes of different scales. Comparing absolute values (e.g., total CO₂ of a small plant vs. a large plant) is meaningless without normalization. > 5. **No Mention of ISO Standards:** Examiners reward students who cite **ISO 14001**, **ISO 50001**, or **GRI Standards** in their descriptive answers. It signals applied awareness beyond textbook theory. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Performance Evaluation (Module 3):** - **Definition:** Systematic quantitative + qualitative assessment of manufacturing systems against green benchmarks across the product life cycle. - **Three Pillars:** Environmental (Planet), Economic (Profit), Social (People) — the **Triple Bottom Line**. - **Four Hierarchical Levels:** Process → Plant → Corporate → Life Cycle (remember the order as **P-P-C-L**). - **Top 10 Metrics (must-know):** SEC, EP, MUR, SR, WI, WRI, CF, EEI, RC, HWR. Memorize formulas + units + direction (↑ or ↓). - **Eco-Efficiency:** Coined by **WBCSD**. Formula = Value ÷ Environmental Impact. It is the single most powerful integrated metric. - **Carbon Footprint:** Uses **IPCC Tier 1** emission factors. $CF = \sum (A_i \times EF_i)$. India grid EF ≈ **0.82 kg CO₂/kWh**. - **Standards to Mention:** **ISO 14001** (Environmental Management), **ISO 50001** (Energy Management), **ISO 14031** (EPIs), **GRI Standards** (Sustainability Reporting). - **PDCA Cycle:** Evaluation is continuous: **Plan-Do-Check-Act**. It is not a one-time audit. - **Mathematical Hygiene:** Always maintain **unit consistency**; always **normalize by production volume**; always check **mass balance**; always cite the **direction** (higher/lower is better) for each metric. - **Engineering Insight:** Green manufacturing is about **decoupling** — producing more economic value while generating less environmental impact. Performance Evaluation is the measurement framework that makes this decoupling **visible, auditable, and improvable**. - **Exam Strategy:** In 14-mark numerical questions, write down the **given data**, then **state the formula**, then show **step-by-step substitution**, then state the **final answer with units**, then add a brief **engineering comment**. This structure fetches full marks consistently under KTU valuation.

topics.title: Cause for Inefficient Operation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET753 - GREEN MANUFACTURING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Cause for Inefficient Operation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (KTU 2024 — PEIET753):**
> **Inefficient operation** in a manufacturing system refers to any operational state, configuration, or process condition in which the ratio of useful output (product, work, energy delivered) to total input (energy, material, labour, time) is **sub-optimal**, i.e., significantly below the thermodynamic, design, or industry-best benchmark. In green manufacturing, inefficiency is treated as a **waste stream** that simultaneously inflates production cost and the **ecological footprint** (carbon emissions, effluent, scrap).

The **First Law of Thermodynamics** (Conservation of Energy) sets the *theoretical* floor of inefficiency — losses are inevitable — while the **Second Law** quantifies the *minimum* unavoidable losses via **entropy generation**. Anything beyond this minimum constitutes **avoidable inefficiency** and is the primary target of green-manufacturing audits.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Terminology Watch:**
> - *Inefficiency* ≠ *Ineffective*. A process can be effective (delivers the desired product) yet highly inefficient (uses excess energy, material, or time).
> - *Green Manufacturing* audits inefficiency under a **triple-bottom-line** lens: **economic**, **environmental**, and **social** cost.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Leaking Bucket

Imagine your factory is a **bucket carrying water (useful production)** from a well to a field.

- **Holes in the bucket** = energy leak, scrap, idle time, coolant loss, compressed-air leak.
- The water still reaches the field, but you must **pump harder** (more input energy/cost).
- Some leaks are **natural** (thermodynamic minimum, friction) — you cannot eliminate them.
- Other leaks are **manufacturing-induced** (poor maintenance, wrong settings, oversized motors) — these are the **targets of green manufacturing**.

> [!TIP]
> **Intuition Rule (for KTU exams):** Every cause of inefficiency in a manufacturing system can be mapped to one of the **8 classic wastes of Lean** — **DOWNTIME**: *Defects, Overproduction, Waiting, Non-utilised talent, Transportation, Inventory, Motion, Extra-processing*. Pair this with energy-specific wastes (heat, sound, vibration) to give examiners a complete answer.

## 1.3 Standard Performance Metrics (KTU-Highlighted Constants)

| Metric | Symbol | Benchmark |
|---|---|---|
| Specific Energy Consumption | **SEC** | kWh / kg of product |
| Compressed Air Leakage Rate | **% Leakage** | < **10 %** acceptable |
| Power Factor | **cos φ** | ≥ **0.90** |
| Motor Loading Factor | **β** | **0.75 – 1.0** (sweet spot) |
| Coefficient of Performance | **COP** | 2 – 4 (HVAC) |
| Overall Equipment Effectiveness | **OEE** | **≥ 85 %** world-class |
| Waste-to-Product Ratio | **W/P** | Industry-specific |

> [!NOTE]
> Memorising the numeric benchmark thresholds (e.g., **10 % leak ceiling, 0.9 power factor, 85 % OEE**) is a high-frequency 3-mark question in KTU University Exams.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Six Major Domains of Inefficiency

Green-manufacturing literature (and KTU Module 3) classifies causes of inefficient operation into **six functional domains**. Each domain has its own dominant loss mechanism.

### 2.1.1 ⚡ Electrical Domain — Motors, Drives, Power Quality
- **Under-loaded / over-loaded motors:** Motors are most efficient between **75 % – 100 %** load. Below 50 % load, efficiency falls sharply due to fixed losses (iron + friction) being amortised over less useful output.
- **Rewound motors:** Each rewinding can drop efficiency by **2 – 5 %**.
- **Power-factor penalty:** A poor **cos φ** leads to utility **kVAh penalties** even when **kWh** is unchanged.
- **Harmonics:** Non-linear loads (VFDs, rectifiers) inject harmonics → I²R losses in cables, transformer heating, capacitor failures.

### 2.1.2 🌀 Fluid-Domain — Compressed Air, Pumping, HVAC
- **Compressed air** is the **most expensive utility** in a plant — ~ **6–8× the cost of electricity** per unit of useful energy delivered (DOE estimate).
- **Typical leakage rates** in Indian factories: **20 – 40 %** of compressor output is leaked through couplings, hoses, and quick-disconnects.
- **Pumps:** Throttling valves and bypass lines waste **15 – 30 %** of pump input power.
- **HVAC:** Oversized chillers, leaky ducts, set-point drift.

### 2.1.3 🔥 Thermal Domain — Furnaces, Boilers, Steam Traps
- **Furnace/boiler inefficiency** arises from: incomplete combustion, excess air, scale on heat-transfer surfaces, radiation/convection shell losses, and **flue-gas heat loss** (the largest single loss).
- **Steam traps** that are **failed-open** continuously vent live steam → massive fuel waste.
- **Insulation failure** on steam pipes/valves.

### 2.1.4 🔧 Mechanical Domain — Bearings, Lubrication, Alignment
- **Misalignment** between coupled shafts causes 1 – 5 % power loss and drastically shortens bearing life.
- **Over-greasing** of bearings = 30 % of premature bearing failures.
- **Belt slippage, worn V-belts, unbalanced rotating parts** = chronic losses.

### 2.1.5 📦 Material / Process Domain — Scrap, Rework, Coolant
- **Material utilisation ratio** in machining: often **10 – 30 %** of input bar-stock becomes chips.
- **Cutting fluid loss** through evaporation, drag-out, leaks.
- **Tool wear** → poor surface finish → re-work → energy + material double-spent.

### 2.1.6 🕒 Operational / Managerial Domain
- **Idling machinery** (machine ON, no work — "donut machine" running with no donut being made).
- **Poor scheduling** → start-up/shut-down losses (especially in furnace systems).
- **Lack of metering** → *“you cannot manage what you cannot measure”* (the **Deming–Drucker** principle).

## 2.2 Root-Cause Logic Chain

A KTU answer is marked higher when the student presents a **causal chain** rather than a flat list:

> **Root Cause → Mechanism → Symptom → Loss Mechanism → Environmental & Economic Impact**

**Example chain (compressed-air leak):**
> Worn coupling (root) → 6-bar air escapes to atmosphere (mechanism) → audible hissing + pressure drop (symptom) → compressor runs longer to maintain set-point (loss) → extra kWh + extra CO₂ (impact).

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cheat Sheet — Equations You Must Know for KTU 2024 University Exam**

| # | Formula | Meaning | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **η = P_out / P_in × 100 %** | Efficiency definition | Universal |
| 2 | **SEC = E_total / m_product** [kWh/kg] | Specific energy consumption | Benchmarking |
| 3 | **β = P_actual / P_rated** | Motor loading factor | Motor selection |
| 4 | **η_motor = f(β, design)** | Non-linear with β | Motor audit |
| 5 | **% Leakage = (V_leak / V_total) × 100** | Compressed-air audit | Utility bill reduction |
| 6 | **COP = Q_cooling / W_input** | HVAC performance | Refrigeration |
| 7 | **OEE = A × P × Q** | Availability × Performance × Quality | World-class mfg |
| 8 | **η_comb = 1 − (Σ losses / Q_input)** | Combustion efficiency | Furnaces/boilers |
| 9 | **Wasted kW × h × t × tariff** | Annual cost of waste | Payback calc. |
| 10 | **P_pump = ρ g Q H / η_pump** | Pump power equation | Pumping systems |
| 11 | **Flue-gas loss % = (T_g − T_a) / (CO₂ % × const.)** | Siegert formula | Boiler audit |
| 12 | **CO₂ = kWh × 0.82 kg/kWh** | Emission factor (India grid avg) | Carbon accounting |

> **Symbol Note:** Use `\vert` instead of `|` in any inline math to avoid LaTeX-typo issues. E.g., `\vert P_{actual} \vert`.

## 2.4 Real-World Utility of This Knowledge

| Industry Sector | Top Inefficiency Targeted |
|---|---|
| Automotive (OEM & Tier-1) | Compressed air, paint-shop HVAC, welding energy |
| Textile (Kerala scenario — Spinning) | Motor loading, steam in dyeing, water re-use |
| Foundry & Forging | Furnace excess air, sand-reclaim efficiency |
| Food & Dairy | Refrigeration COP, boiler efficiency |
| Cement | Pre-heater cyclone heat loss, kiln dust |
| Pharma | Cleanroom HVAC over-conditioning |
| Sheet-Metal / SMEs | Compressor leaks (single biggest loss) |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — Annual Energy & Cost Lost to a Compressed-Air Leak

A standard orifice-flow scenario is a **favourite KTU 14-mark derivation** because it combines fluid mechanics, thermodynamics, and economics.

### 3.1.1 Given Data (Standard KTU-Exam Style)
- Upstream pressure: **p₁ = 7 bar (g)** → absolute **P₁ = 8.01325 bar ≈ 8 × 10⁵ Pa**
- Atmospheric pressure: **P₂ = 1.01325 × 10⁵ Pa**
- Leak hole diameter: **d = 3 mm = 0.003 m**
- Discharge coefficient: **C_d = 0.65**
- Specific heat ratio of air: **γ = 1.4**
- Gas constant: **R = 287 J/(kg·K)**
- Inlet temperature: **T₁ = 300 K**
- Compressor specific power: **SPC = 6.5 kW per 100 cfm** (≈ **22 kW per 1 m³/min**)
- Annual operating hours: **t = 4800 h**
- Tariff: **₹ 8 / kWh**

### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Pressure ratio to check compressibility regime:**

$$\frac{P_{1}}{P_{2}} = \frac{8.01325}{1.01325} \approx 7.91$$

> Since **7.91 > 1.89** (the critical pressure ratio for air), the flow at the exit is **choked** (sonic) and we must use the **choked-mass-flow equation**.

**Step 2 — Apply the choked orifice formula:**

$$
\dot{m} = C_{d} \cdot A \cdot P_{1} \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{\gamma}{R\,T_{1}}} \cdot \left(\dfrac{2}{\gamma+1}\right)^{\!\tfrac{\gamma+1}{2(\gamma-1)}}
$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate the bracketed constant for γ = 1.4:**

$$
\left(\dfrac{2}{1.4+1}\right)^{\!\tfrac{2.4}{0.8}} = (0.8)^{3} = 0.512
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the mass-flow term:**

$$
\sqrt{\dfrac{1.4}{287 \times 300}} = \sqrt{1.626 \times 10^{-5}} = 4.033 \times 10^{-3}
$$

**Step 5 — Plug numerical values:**

$$
\dot{m} = 0.65 \times \dfrac{\pi (0.003)^{2}}{4} \times 8.01325 \times 10^{5} \times 4.033 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.512
$$

$$
\dot{m} = 0.65 \times 7.069 \times 10^{-6} \times 8.013 \times 10^{5} \times 4.033 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.512
$$

**Step 6 — Multiply term-by-term:**

$$
\dot{m} = 0.65 \times 7.069 \times 10^{-6} \times 8.013 \times 10^{5} \times 2.065 \times 10^{-3}
$$

$$
\dot{m} \approx 7.62 \times 10^{-3} \ \text{kg/s}
$$

**Step 7 — Convert mass flow to free-air volume flow:**

At STP, **1 kg of air ≈ 0.816 m³** → so

$$
\dot{V} = 7.62 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.816 \times 60 = 0.373 \ \text{m}^{3}\!/\text{min}
$$

**Step 8 — Convert to compressor-specific power demand:**

$$
P_{leak} = 0.373 \ \text{m}^{3}\!/\text{min} \times 22 \ \text{kW/(m}^{3}\!/\text{min)} \approx 8.20 \ \text{kW}
$$

**Step 9 — Annual energy lost:**

$$
E_{loss} = 8.20 \times 4800 = 39{,}360 \ \text{kWh/year}
$$

**Step 10 — Annual monetary loss:**

$$
C_{loss} = 39{,}360 \times 8 = ₹\ 3{,}14{,}880 \ \text{per year}
$$

**Step 11 — Annual CO₂ emissions (India grid factor ≈ 0.82 kg/kWh):**

$$
CO_{2} = 39{,}360 \times 0.82 \approx 32{,}275 \ \text{kg CO}_{2}/\text{year} \ (\approx 32.3 \ \text{tCO}_{2})
$$

### 3.1.3 Verdict
> [!TIP]
> A **single 3 mm hole** at 7-bar line costs ~ **₹ 3.15 lakh/year** and emits **32 t CO₂/year** — equivalent to the annual sequestration of **~ 800 trees**. This is *exactly* the kind of "wow-factor" the KTU examiner loves in a 14-mark answer.

---

## 3.2 Derivation — Motor Efficiency Loss at Off-Design Loading

A frequently-asked KTU question: *"A 15 kW motor is running at 4 kW load. Estimate the loss penalty."*

### 3.2.1 Use the standard motor-loss model

Total motor losses at any load = **Constant losses (P_c)** + **Variable losses (P_v · β²)**, where **β = P_actual / P_rated**.

For a typical **IE2 / IE3 4-pole motor**, the fixed-to-variable loss ratio is approximately **k = P_c / P_v ≈ 0.4** at rated load.

### 3.2.2 Step-by-Step

**Step 1 — Loading factor:**

$$
\beta = \dfrac{4}{15} = 0.2667
$$

**Step 2 — Assume rated input is 16.1 kW (rated efficiency ≈ 93 %), so rated losses:**

$$
P_{loss,r} = 16.1 - 15 = 1.1 \ \text{kW}
$$

**Step 3 — Split into fixed and variable:**

$$
P_{c} = \dfrac{k}{1+k} \cdot P_{loss,r} = \dfrac{0.4}{1.4} \times 1.1 = 0.314 \ \text{kW}
$$

$$
P_{v} = P_{loss,r} - P_{c} = 0.786 \ \text{kW}
$$

**Step 4 — Losses at 26.67 % load:**

$$
P_{loss,26} = P_{c} + P_{v}\,\beta^{2} = 0.314 + 0.786 \times (0.2667)^{2}
$$

$$
P_{loss,26} = 0.314 + 0.786 \times 0.0711 = 0.314 + 0.0559 = 0.370 \ \text{kW}
$$

**Step 5 — Input power at 26.67 % load:**

$$
P_{in,26} = P_{out} + P_{loss,26} = 4.0 + 0.370 = 4.370 \ \text{kW}
$$

**Step 6 — Efficiency at 26.67 % load:**

$$
\eta_{26} = \dfrac{4.0}{4.370} \times 100 = 91.5\ \%
$$

**Step 7 — Efficiency at 100 % load (rated):**

$$
\eta_{100} = \dfrac{15}{16.1} \times 100 = 93.2\ \%
$$

**Step 8 — Compare:**

> The motor is *still* ~ 91.5 % efficient, but it is **grossly oversized** — wasting **0.314 kW continuously as fixed losses** (magnetisation, friction, windage) that exist whether the motor does 4 kW of work or 15 kW. **The fix is right-sizing**, not rewinding.

### 3.2.3 Verdict
> [!NOTE]
> A common student mistake is to assume "efficiency rises with load" — the *efficiency* peaks at ~ 75 %–100 % load, but the *absolute loss* in kW is dominated by fixed losses when β < 0.5.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix — "Which Cause am I dealing with?"

| Symptom observed on shop floor | Most likely root cause | Diagnostic action |
|---|---|---|
| Frequent compressor cut-in | **Air leak OR undersized receiver** | Ultrasonic leak survey at night |
| Motor windings hot, low current | **Under-loading** | Measure β, replace with smaller motor or VFD |
| Boiler fuel bill rising, steam pressure OK | **Failed steam trap OR scale** | Trap-by-trap temperature survey |
| Chilled-water temp drift in cleanroom | **Condenser fouling** OR **refrigerant undercharge** | Log ΔT across condenser |
| Tool life < 100 pieces, surface finish poor | **Wrong cutting fluid, wrong speed/feed** | Recompute v·c, f_z; check fluid concentration |
| High reject rate on first shift | **Machine warm-up not completed** | Lock warm-up cycle in PLC |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Fish-Bone (Ishikawa) Diagram — Root-Cause Map of Inefficient Operation

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Flow — Energy Audit Cascade


## 4.3 Sankey-Style Loss Allocation (text representation)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    s1(["100 percent Input Energy"]) --> a1["Boiler 35 percent"]
    s1 --> a2["Compressor 25 percent"]
    s1 --> a3["Motors 20 percent"]
    s1 --> a4["HVAC 10 percent"]
    s1 --> a5["Lighting 5 percent"]
    s1 --> a6["Misc 5 percent"]
    a1 --> b1["Flue gas 18 percent"]
    a1 --> b2["Wall and pipe loss 4 percent"]
    a1 --> b3["Useful steam 13 percent"]

    a2 --> c3["Useful air 7 percent"]

```

> [!NOTE]
> In a **real KTU 14-mark answer**, the student may draw an equivalent Sankey diagram on paper, labelling widths proportional to kW lost. The visual earns **2–3 mark bonus** if drawn cleanly.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**List any six causes of inefficient operation in a manufacturing plant.**

**Model Answer (Point-wise, 6 points × 0.5 mark):**
1. Compressed-air leaks at couplings, hoses and quick-disconnects.
2. Under-loading of induction motors (β < 0.5).
3. Excess air in furnace/boiler combustion.
4. Failed steam traps (failed-open) venting live steam.
5. Lack of sub-metering of energy streams.
6. Idling of machinery during inter-shift gaps and breakdowns.

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`
**Define Specific Energy Consumption (SEC) and state its typical unit.**

**Model Answer:**
- **Definition:** SEC is the energy consumed per unit mass of finished product, used as a *normalised* benchmark to compare plants of different sizes.
- **Unit:** **kWh / kg of product** (also MJ/kg).
- **Significance:** Lower SEC ⇒ higher energy efficiency and lower carbon footprint; allows comparison across geographies and shifts.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks each) — Module-3 Internal Choice

> KTU 2024 Pattern: **Module-3 carries Q.5 or Q.6 with internal choice**. Either Option A or Option B is to be answered.

---

### 🔷 QUESTION A (14 Marks)

**`[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 3, Q.5(a)]`**
**(a)** Explain the **major causes of inefficient operation in a manufacturing plant**, classifying them into electrical, fluid, thermal, mechanical, material and managerial domains. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A factory operates a **compressor at 7 bar (g)** supplying a 3 mm equivalent leak hole. The compressor specific power is **22 kW per m³/min**, annual operating time is **5000 hours**, and electricity tariff is **₹ 9 / kWh**. Compute the **annual energy loss, monetary loss and CO₂ emission**. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) (7 marks)

| Domain | Cause | Loss mechanism |
|---|---|---|
| **Electrical** | Under-loaded motor, rewound motor, low PF | Fixed losses, kVA penalty, harmonics |
| **Fluid** | Air leaks, throttled pumps, oversized fans | Compressor over-run, by-pass energy |
| **Thermal** | Excess air, scale, failed traps, poor insulation | Flue-gas loss, blow-by, wall loss |
| **Mechanical** | Misalignment, poor lubrication, belt slip | Friction, premature failure |
| **Material** | High scrap, coolant drag-out, over-machining | Re-work energy, fluid replacement |
| **Managerial** | No metering, no SOP, idling | Chronic invisible waste |

*(Allocate 1 mark per row + 1 mark for the integrative closing statement — e.g., "Most causes are inter-linked; sub-metering is the master key.")*

#### Model Solution — Part (b) (7 marks)

Use choked-orifice flow as in **§3.1**.

**Step 1 — Mass-flow rate from §3.1, Step 6:**

$$
\dot{m} = 7.62 \times 10^{-3} \ \text{kg/s}
$$

**Step 2 — Free-air volumetric flow:**

$$
\dot{V} = 7.62 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.816 \times 60 = 0.373 \ \text{m}^{3}\!/\text{min}
$$

**Step 3 — Compressor shaft power required to feed the leak:**

$$
P_{leak} = 0.373 \times 22 = 8.21 \ \text{kW}
$$

**Step 4 — Annual energy lost:**

$$
E_{loss} = 8.21 \times 5000 = 41{,}050 \ \text{kWh}
$$

**Step 5 — Monetary loss:**

$$
C_{loss} = 41{,}050 \times 9 = ₹\ 3{,}69{,}450 \ (\approx ₹ 3.69 \ \text{lakh})
$$

**Step 6 — CO₂ emission (factor 0.82 kg/kWh):**

$$
CO_{2} = 41{,}050 \times 0.82 = 33{,}661 \ \text{kg} = 33.66 \ \text{tCO}_{2}
$$

**Step 7 — Conclusion:** A 3 mm leak costs ~ **₹ 3.7 lakh/year** and emits **~ 34 tCO₂/yr**. Justifies a **leak-survey + fix** project with payback typically < **2 months**.

---

### 🔷 QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**`[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 3, Q.6(b)]`**
**(a)** With the help of a **fish-bone diagram**, identify the **root causes of inefficient operation** in a manufacturing setup. Discuss in detail the **6-M framework** (Man, Machine, Material, Method, Measurement, Mother-Environment). **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A **15 kW, 3-phase induction motor** is operating at **30 % loading**. If rated efficiency is **93 %** and the fixed-to-variable loss ratio is **0.4**, calculate the **operating efficiency** and **annual energy lost** for **6000 hours** of operation per year. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) (7 marks)

- **Fish-bone diagram** — 1 mark for the sketch (see §4.1).
- **Man:** untrained operators, lack of empowerment — 1 mark.
- **Machine:** misalignment, bearing wear, rewound motors, VFD harmonics — 1 mark.
- **Material:** wrong grade, excess inventory, high scrap yield — 1 mark.
- **Method:** throttled valves, wrong tool path, excess air in furnace — 1 mark.
- **Measurement:** no sub-metering, infrequent calibration — 1 mark.
- **Mother-Environment:** leaky air, HVAC over-cooling, steam-trap failure — 1 mark.

*(Examiner's tip: 1 additional mark for closing — "The 6-M framework is a diagnostic lens, not a solution; corrective action follows a Pareto and root-cause analysis.")*

#### Model Solution — Part (b) (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Loading factor:**

$$
\beta = 0.30
$$

**Step 2 — Rated input & rated losses:**

$$
P_{in,r} = \dfrac{15}{0.93} = 16.13 \ \text{kW}
$$

$$
P_{loss,r} = 16.13 - 15 = 1.13 \ \text{kW}
$$

**Step 3 — Split losses (k = 0.4):**

$$
P_{c} = \dfrac{0.4}{1.4} \times 1.13 = 0.323 \ \text{kW}
$$

$$
P_{v} = 1.13 - 0.323 = 0.807 \ \text{kW}
$$

**Step 4 — Losses at β = 0.30:**

$$
P_{loss,0.3} = 0.323 + 0.807 \times (0.30)^{2} = 0.323 + 0.0726 = 0.396 \ \text{kW}
$$

**Step 5 — Input at 30 % load:**

$$
P_{in,0.3} = 4.5 + 0.396 = 4.896 \ \text{kW}
$$

**Step 6 — Operating efficiency:**

$$
\eta_{0.3} = \dfrac{4.5}{4.896} \times 100 = 91.9\ \%
$$

**Step 7 — Annual wasted energy (over a fully-loaded 4.5 kW load that *should* need only 4.83 kW of input):**

$$
E_{waste} = (P_{in,0.3} - P_{out,0.3}) \times h = 0.396 \times 6000 = 2{,}376 \ \text{kWh/yr}
$$

**Step 8 — Equivalent monetary cost @ ₹ 8/kWh:**

$$
C_{waste} = 2{,}376 \times 8 = ₹\ 19{,}008 \ \text{per year}
$$

**Step 9 — Conclusion:** The motor is **massively oversized**; replacement with a **5.5 kW** (or use of a **VFD** to maintain β ≥ 0.7) would save **~₹ 19,000/yr** and ~ **1.95 tCO₂/yr**.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 5 Ways Students Lose Marks in This Topic (as flagged by KTU Board Examiners):**
> 1. **Listing without classifying** — a flat 10-item bullet list with no domain-categorisation. → *Always use the 6-M or 8-waste (DOWNTIME) framework.*
> 2. **Forgetting units** in derived answers (e.g., writing "3.7 lakh" without stating "₹/year").
> 3. **Not stating the discharge coefficient C_d** in compressed-air derivations — a single line, but worth 0.5 mark.
> 4. **Mixing η and COP** — they are *not* the same. η is dimensionless; COP can exceed 1 for heat pumps.
> 5. **Skipping the closing "engineering recommendation" sentence** in 14-mark answers — examiners look for closure; without it, you lose **1–2 marks** even with correct maths.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Revision Bullets — Print This Page!**

- ☐ Inefficiency = ratio of useful output to total input **below benchmark**.
- ☐ Three categories of energy loss: **Conversion, Distribution, End-Use**.
- ☐ **8 Wastes of Lean** = **DOWNTIME** (Defects, Overproduction, Waiting, Non-utilised talent, Transport, Inventory, Motion, Extra-processing).
- ☐ **6-M** = Man, Machine, Material, Method, Measurement, Mother-Environment.
- ☐ **Compressed air** = most expensive utility (~ 6–8× electricity on a per-useful-energy basis).
- ☐ **3 mm leak at 7 bar** ≈ **₹ 3–4 lakh/year** loss in a typical tariff.
- ☐ **Motor sweet spot** β ∈ [0.75, 1.0]; below 0.5 → fixed losses dominate.
- ☐ **Rewound motor** = −2 to −5 % efficiency each time.
- ☐ **Power factor** must be ≥ 0.90; penalty below 0.85.
- ☐ **Steam trap failure** = 5–10 % fuel waste per failed trap.
- ☐ **COP** of chiller > 1 is normal (it's a *heat pump* definition).
- ☐ **OEE** = A × P × Q; world-class = 85 %.
- ☐ **India grid CO₂ factor** ≈ **0.82 kg CO₂ / kWh**.
- ☐ **Right-sizing > Rewinding** for inefficient motors.
- ☐ Always end 14-mark answers with a **Payback period** or **Recommendation sentence**.
- ☐ Use **choked-orifice formula** when P₁/P₂ > 1.89 for air leaks.
- ☐ Indian grid CO₂ emission factor of 0.82 kg/kWh is KTU-expected benchmark value.
- ☐ **Sankey diagram** earns bonus marks — show **kW lost** as widths.

---

### 🔖 End of Topic Notes — "Cause for Inefficient Operation" (Module 3)
