- ✅ **Six Components (V-M-G-I-R-A):** Vision, Mission, Goals, Intent, Resources, Action.
- ✅ **Three Hierarchical Levels:** Corporate → Business Unit → Operational/Shop-Floor.
- ✅ **Four Driver Categories:** Regulatory, Market, Competitive, Internal.
- ✅ **Five Common Barriers:** High CAPEX, Cultural Resistance, Tech Gaps, Policy Inconsistency, Greenwashing.
- ✅ **The Green Execution Chasm:** 80% declare intent, only 20% achieve it on the shop floor.
- ✅ **SMART Objectives:** Specific, Measurable, Achievable, Relevant, Time-bound.
- ✅ **Intent Cascade:** 5 stages — Formulation → Communication → Internalization → Operationalization → Iteration.
- ✅ **Key Formula — IPG (Intent-Performance Gap):**
$$IPG = k \cdot I_{strength} \cdot R_c \cdot C_e \cdot \beta$$
- ✅ **Carbon Trajectory Equations (Linear and Exponential):**
$$C(t) = C_0 - k_r \cdot t \quad ; \quad C(t) = C_0 \cdot e^{-\lambda t}$$
- ✅ **Key Metrics to Memorize:** EPI, tCO₂e, kWh/unit, m³/unit, % Recycling, % Green Revenue.
- ✅ **Industry Examples to Quote:** Toyota, 3M, Apple, Tata Steel, Reliance, Walmart, Unilever.
- ✅ **Closing Mantra for KTU Answers:**
*"Intent provides the soul; SMART objectives provide the skeleton; Execution provides the muscle — all three are required for a living green manufacturing body."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: System Approach
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET753 - GREEN MANUFACTURING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** System Approach

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **System Approach (KTU 2024 Definition):**
> The *System Approach* in Green Manufacturing is a holistic, integrated methodology that conceptualizes a manufacturing enterprise — and its interactions with the environment, society, and economy — as an **interconnected system** of material flows, energy flows, information flows, and capital flows. Rather than optimizing any single process in isolation, it seeks to analyze, model, and optimize the **entire production–consumption chain** from raw material extraction to end-of-life recovery, ensuring that environmental, economic, and social objectives (the **Triple Bottom Line**) are pursued simultaneously.

A "system" in this context is formally defined as a set of interrelated elements working together toward a common goal, bounded by an explicit **system boundary** through which **inputs** (materials, energy, information) enter and **outputs** (products, by-products, emissions, waste) leave.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Terminology Board Must Know:**
> - **System Boundary:** The imaginary perimeter separating the system from its environment.
> - **Throughput:** The rate at which inputs are transformed into outputs inside the system.
> - **Feedback Loop:** Information or material returned from output stages back to input stages to regulate performance.
> - **Stock & Flow:** *Stocks* are accumulations; *flows* are rates of change of stocks.
> - **Emergent Property:** A characteristic of the whole system that none of its parts possess individually.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Think of a **car engine**. A mechanic can fix a single spark plug (a "reductionist" view), but a *systems-thinking* mechanic would ask: *How does this plug affect combustion? Combustion affects torque. Torque affects fuel use. Fuel use affects emissions. Emissions affect the catalytic converter. The converter's heat affects the next plug…* 

The **System Approach** in Green Manufacturing is exactly this. We stop asking *"How do I make this one process cleaner?"* and start asking *"How do I redesign the whole factory — or the whole supply chain — so that the waste of one process becomes the input of another, just like nutrients cycle in a forest?"*

Another perfect analogy: **a forest ecosystem**. Trees drop leaves → leaves become soil → soil feeds new trees → animals eat plants → animals fertilize soil. **Nothing is "waste."** The System Approach aims to make a manufacturing network behave like a forest — a vision called **Industrial Ecology**.

---

## 1.3 The Core Engineering Metrics Used

> [!NOTE]
> **Three Core Sustainability Metrics (Board-Favorite):**
> - **Material Intensity (MI)** = $\dfrac{\text{Mass of Material Input (kg)}}{\text{Service Output (unit)}}$ — measured in **kg/unit**
> - **Energy Intensity (EI)** = $\dfrac{\text{Energy Consumed (MJ)}}{\text{Service Output (unit)}}$ — measured in **MJ/unit**
> - **Pollution Index (PI)** = $\dfrac{\text{Mass of Pollutant Emitted (kg)}}{\text{Service Output (unit)}}$ — measured in **kg/unit**

The **System Approach** uses these three ratios as the *yardsticks* for measuring how green any system is at any chosen boundary (process, factory, company, or national).

---

## 1.4 Why System Approach is Critical for Green Manufacturing

Traditional manufacturing optimizes **local efficiency** (e.g., 95% yield in one machine) but creates **global inefficiency** (the rejected 5% becomes hazardous waste). The System Approach exposes these trade-offs by tracking every flow across boundaries — leading to truly *sustainable* solutions like **Industrial Symbiosis, Cradle-to-Cradle design, and Circular Economy models**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Five Foundational Principles of Systems Thinking Applied to Green Manufacturing

The System Approach rests on five principles that the KTU examiner loves to test:

1. **Holism (Wholeness):** The system exhibits properties that no individual component has alone. *Example:* A factory's total carbon footprint ≠ sum of machine-level footprints because of shared utilities, logistics, and feedback reuse.

2. **Interconnectedness & Interdependence:** Every element influences and is influenced by every other element. *Example:* Switching to a renewable energy source changes the economics of waste-heat recovery.

3. **Hierarchical Structure (Multi-Level):** Systems exist within larger systems. The hierarchy in green manufacturing is:
   - **Level 1:** Product (micro)
   - **Level 2:** Process
   - **Level 3:** Factory / Plant
   - **Level 4:** Eco-Industrial Park (EIP)
   - **Level 5:** Regional / National Economy
   - **Level 6:** Global / Planetary (Biosphere)

4. **Inputs → Throughputs → Outputs (I-T-O Model):** Every system transforms inputs into outputs. *Goal of Green Manufacturing:* Minimize undesired outputs (emissions, waste) and close the loop so outputs become inputs.

5. **Dynamic Equilibrium & Feedback:** Systems self-regulate via negative feedback (corrective) and amplify via positive feedback (growth). *Example:* Recycling rates increasing → raw material prices dropping → less incentive to recycle (negative feedback) — a classic sustainability trap.

## 2.2 The Input–Process–Output (I-P-O) Model of a Green Manufacturing System

A green manufacturing system is modeled as:

$$\text{Inputs (I)} \xrightarrow{\text{Process (P)}} \text{Outputs (O)} + \text{Environmental Burden (E)}$$

Where:
- **I** = Raw materials, energy, water, information, capital
- **P** = Conversion activities (machining, forming, assembly, etc.)
- **O** = Useful products, by-products
- **E** = Emissions to air, water, soil; solid waste; heat rejected

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Statement:** "The goal of a green manufacturing system is to **maximize O (useful product)**, **minimize E (environmental burden)**, and **close the loop** by feeding O-by-products back as I to upstream processes."

## 2.3 The Five Methodological Tools of the System Approach

| # | Tool | Purpose | Boundary |
|---|------|---------|----------|
| 1 | **Material Flow Analysis (MFA)** | Tracks all material inputs and outputs in mass terms | Cradle-to-grave |
| 2 | **Energy Flow Analysis (EFA)** | Tracks all energy inputs, conversions, and losses | Process / plant / nation |
| 3 | **Substance Flow Analysis (SFA)** | Tracks a *specific* hazardous substance (e.g., lead, mercury) | Industrial sector / region |
| 4 | **Life Cycle Assessment (LCA)** | Quantifies environmental impact of a product across all stages | Cradle-to-grave or cradle-to-cradle |
| 5 | **Industrial Symbiosis (IS)** | Networks firms so one's waste is another's input | Eco-Industrial Park |

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield, Board-Critical)

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units | When to Use |
|---|---------|---------|-------|-------------|
| 1 | $\sum m_{in} = \sum m_{out} + \dfrac{dM_{system}}{dt} + \sum m_{reacted}$ | **Conservation of Mass** (general balance) | kg | Any process / factory / national economy |
| 2 | $\sum E_{in} = \sum E_{out} + \dfrac{dE_{system}}{dt}$ | **First Law of Thermodynamics** | MJ or kWh | Energy analysis of any system |
| 3 | $\eta_{process} = \dfrac{E_{useful}}{E_{input}} \times 100\%$ | **Process Efficiency** | % | Efficiency of machine or system |
| 4 | $MI = \dfrac{m_{material\,in}}{N_{units}}$ | **Material Intensity** | kg/unit | Sustainability KPI |
| 5 | $EI = \dfrac{E_{energy}}{N_{units}}$ | **Energy Intensity** | MJ/unit | Sustainability KPI |
| 6 | $PI = \dfrac{m_{pollutant}}{N_{units}}$ | **Pollution Intensity** | kg/unit | Sustainability KPI |
| 7 | $R = \dfrac{m_{recycled}}{m_{total\,input}} \times 100\%$ | **Recycling / Closing-the-Loop Ratio** | % | Circular economy metric |
| 8 | $E_{CO_2} = \sum_i (m_i \times EF_i)$ | **Cumulative CO₂ Equivalent Emissions** | kg CO₂-eq | Carbon footprint of a system |
| 9 | $S_{symbiosis} = \dfrac{\text{Number of symbiotic links}}{\text{Number of firms}}$ | **Industrial Symbiosis Index** | dimensionless | Eco-industrial park analysis |
| 10 | $T_{recovery} = \dfrac{m_{recovered}}{m_{waste\,generated}} \times 100\%$ | **Waste Recovery Rate** | % | Closed-loop manufacturing |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL LaTeX Rule for KTU Answer Sheets:** When writing any formula on paper, always define each variable immediately below the equation. Examiners award a full 2–3 marks just for clear variable definitions.

## 2.5 System Boundary Choices — The Strategic Decision

| Boundary Name | Scope | When Used |
|---------------|-------|-----------|
| **Cradle-to-Gate** | Raw material extraction → factory exit gate | B2B (Business-to-Business) product comparisons |
| **Gate-to-Gate** | Factory-in to factory-out (single facility) | Internal process improvement |
| **Cradle-to-Grave** | Raw material → use → disposal | Full product LCA |
| **Cradle-to-Cradle** | Raw material → use → **remanufacture/recycle** (loop closed) | Circular Economy / Green Manufacturing **— the ideal for KTU** |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application Domain | How System Approach is Used |
|--------------------|----------------------------|
| **Eco-Industrial Parks (EIPs)** | Kalundborg (Denmark) is the world's most famous model — 30+ firms share steam, fly-ash, gypsum, sludge, and cooling water |
| **Automotive Industry** | Tesla vertically integrates batteries, motors, and energy — closing material and energy loops at company scale |
| **Steel Industry** | By-product gases (coke oven gas, blast furnace gas) are used internally for power and heat — internal symbiosis |
| **Cement Industry** | Uses fly-ash from coal power plants and slag from steel plants — cross-industry symbiosis |
| **National Policy** | EU's *Circular Economy Action Plan*, India's *National Resource Efficiency Policy* are system-approach policies |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: The Universal Mass Balance Equation of a Green Manufacturing System

The single most important equation in the System Approach is the **Conservation of Mass** applied to a bounded system.

**Statement:** For any system boundary, the rate of mass accumulation inside the system equals the difference between mass entering and mass leaving, plus any mass generated or consumed by chemical reaction.

### Step 1 — Define the system

Let a green manufacturing plant be bounded by an imaginary line. Inside the boundary, mass $M_{system}$ may accumulate (in raw-material stock, work-in-progress, finished goods inventory, or waste piles).

### Step 2 — Identify all flows crossing the boundary

Three categories of mass flow exist:

- **Input flows** $\dot{m}_{in,k}$ — where $k = 1, 2, 3, \dots, n$ indexes each input stream
- **Output flows** $\dot{m}_{out,j}$ — where $j = 1, 2, 3, \dots, m$ indexes each output stream
- **Generation / consumption by reaction** $\dot{r}_{reaction}$ — net mass produced or consumed by chemical reaction inside the boundary

### Step 3 — Write the fundamental balance

For a system, the rate of change of mass stored inside must equal the net rate of mass entering *plus* any net rate of generation by reaction. The "net rate entering" is (in − out):

$$\frac{dM_{system}}{dt} = \sum_{k=1}^{n} \dot{m}_{in,k} - \sum_{j=1}^{m} \dot{m}_{out,j} + \sum \dot{r}_{reaction}$$

### Step 4 — Rearrange to the KTU "exam-friendly" form

Rearranging, we obtain the universal mass balance:

$$\boxed{\;\sum_{k=1}^{n} \dot{m}_{in,k} = \sum_{j=1}^{m} \dot{m}_{out,j} + \frac{dM_{system}}{dt} - \sum \dot{r}_{reaction}\;}$$

This is the form examiners expect in green-manufacturing answer scripts.

### Step 5 — Three special-case simplifications (very important for KTU)

**Case A — Steady-state, no reaction (typical continuous process):**
When the system operates at steady state, the mass stored inside is constant, so $\dfrac{dM_{system}}{dt} = 0$, and if no chemical reaction occurs, $\sum \dot{r}_{reaction} = 0$. The balance reduces to:

$$\sum_{k=1}^{n} \dot{m}_{in,k} = \sum_{j=1}^{m} \dot{m}_{out,j}$$

**Interpretation:** "What comes in must go out." This is the simplest and most-tested form.

**Case B — Steady-state, with reaction (e.g., combustion):**
Here $\dfrac{dM_{system}}{dt} = 0$ but $\sum \dot{r}_{reaction} \neq 0$. The balance becomes:

$$\sum_{k=1}^{n} \dot{m}_{in,k} = \sum_{j=1}^{m} \dot{m}_{out,j} - \sum \dot{r}_{reaction}$$

**Case C — Batch process (unsteady):**
The full equation must be used because mass is accumulating or depleting inside the boundary (e.g., a tank being filled or emptied).

### Step 6 — Worked Example for Board Practice

> **Problem (KTU-Style 14-Mark Type):** A surface-treatment plant receives $120 \text{ kg/h}$ of metal parts, $8 \text{ kg/h}$ of fresh process chemicals, and $200 \text{ L/h}$ of rinse water (density $1 \text{ kg/L}$). Outputs are $118 \text{ kg/h}$ of treated parts, $6 \text{ kg/h}$ of recoverable chemical drag-out, and a sludge stream of $12 \text{ kg/h}$. The system is at steady state. No chemical reaction occurs. Calculate the mass of wastewater emitted to the environment.

**Solution — step by step:**

Total mass input = mass of parts + mass of chemicals + mass of water

$$\sum \dot{m}_{in} = 120 + 8 + (200 \times 1) = 328 \text{ kg/h}$$

Total known mass output = mass of treated parts + recoverable drag-out + sludge

$$\sum \dot{m}_{out,\,known} = 118 + 6 + 12 = 136 \text{ kg/h}$$

Apply the steady-state, no-reaction mass balance:

$$\sum \dot{m}_{in} = \sum \dot{m}_{out} \implies 328 = 136 + \dot{m}_{wastewater}$$

Solving for the unknown wastewater:

$$\dot{m}_{wastewater} = 328 - 136 = 192 \text{ kg/h}$$

**Green interpretation:** Out of $328 \text{ kg/h}$ entering, **only $192 \text{ kg/h}$ (≈ 58.5%)** becomes environmental burden. If the system is redesigned to recover the $192 \text{ kg/h}$ of water and dissolved chemicals (e.g., by reverse osmosis), the burden can approach zero — a *closed-loop* system, the ultimate goal of the System Approach.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Closing-the-Loop Recycling Ratio (R)

**Given:** A manufacturing system receives $m_{total}$ mass of input per unit time, of which $m_{recycled}$ is secondary (recycled) material.

**Step 1 — Define the ratio**

The closing-the-loop ratio expresses the fraction of input that comes from recycled sources:

$$R = \frac{m_{recycled}}{m_{total}}$$

**Step 2 — Express as percentage (board convention)**

$$R\,(\%) = \frac{m_{recycled}}{m_{total}} \times 100\%$$

**Step 3 — Bound the value**

By definition, $0 \leq R \leq 1$ (or 0 % to 100 %). A higher $R$ indicates a more closed-loop, more sustainable system.

**Step 4 — Worked example**

A plant uses $500 \text{ kg}$ of aluminium input per day, of which $180 \text{ kg}$ is recycled scrap. Then:

$$R = \frac{180}{500} \times 100\% = 36\%$$

This means the system is partially closed-loop; the System Approach would push $R$ toward 100 %.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparing the System Approach with the Traditional (Reductionist) Approach

| Dimension | Traditional Approach | **System Approach (Green Manufacturing)** |
|-----------|---------------------|------------------------------------------|
| **Philosophy** | Reductionist — analyze parts in isolation | Holistic — analyze interactions and whole |
| **Objective** | Maximize local profit & local yield | Maximize Triple Bottom Line (Planet, People, Profit) |
| **Boundary** | Single machine or process | Cradle-to-cradle (full lifecycle) |
| **Waste View** | Waste is unavoidable | Waste = design flaw = resource in wrong place |
| **Tool Used** | Cost-benefit analysis, PERT, CPM | MFA, SFA, LCA, Industrial Symbiosis, System Dynamics |
| **Time Horizon** | Short-term / quarterly | Multi-generational / 50–100 years |
| **Decision Variable** | Cost per unit | Material intensity, Energy intensity, CO₂-eq per service unit |
| **Failure Mode** | Sub-optimization (good part, bad whole) | Emergent global efficiency |
| **Exemplar Industry** | Conventional mass production | Circular economy, EIPs, remanufacturing |
| **KTU Conceptual Label** | "End-of-pipe" pollution control | "Design for Environment (DfE)" |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diagram 1 — The Generic I-P-O System Model of Green Manufacturing

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    rawMat["RAW MATERIALS"] -->|input| procA
    energy["ENERGY POWER WATER"] -->|input| procA
    info["INFORMATION CAPITAL"] -->|input| procA
    procA["MANUFACTURING SYSTEM"] -->|useful output| product["USEFUL PRODUCT"]
    procA -->|by product| byprod["BY PRODUCT"]
    procA -->|emission to air| air["AIR EMISSIONS"]
    procA -->|liquid waste| water["WATER EMISSIONS"]
    procA -->|solid waste| solid["SOLID WASTE"]
    procA -->|heat rejected| heat["REJECTED HEAT"]
    byprod -. feedback reuse .-> procA
    heat -. feedback energy .-> procA
    air --> envbox["ENVIRONMENT"]
    water --> envbox
    solid --> envbox
    product --> consumer["CONSUMER USE PHASE"]
    consumer -. return for recycling .-> procA
```

**Reading the diagram:** Inputs enter on the left, the system transforms them in the middle, and outputs branch into useful products (top) and environmental burdens (bottom). Dashed feedback lines show where the *System Approach* seeks to close the loop.

## 4.2 Diagram 2 — Hierarchical Levels of the System Approach

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L6["LEVEL 6 GLOBAL BIOSPHERE"] --> L5
    L5["LEVEL 5 NATIONAL REGIONAL ECONOMY"] --> L4
    L4["LEVEL 4 ECO INDUSTRIAL PARK"] --> L3
    L3["LEVEL 3 FACTORY PLANT"] --> L2
    L2["LEVEL 2 PROCESS MACHINE"] --> L1
    L1["LEVEL 1 PRODUCT COMPONENT"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Board Tip:** When the KTU question says *"Apply the system approach at the factory level"*, you must restrict your mass/energy balance to the factory boundary, NOT the national or biosphere boundary. Picking the wrong boundary is the **#1 mark-deduction reason** in this module.

## 4.3 Diagram 3 — Industrial Symbiosis (By-Product Synergy) at an Eco-Industrial Park

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    power["COAL POWER PLANT"] -->|fly ash steam| cement
    power -->|CO2 heat| algae["ALGAE BIOREFINERY"]
    steel["STEEL PLANT"] -->|slag BF gas| cement
    steel -->|sludge| brick["BRICK MANUFACTURER"]
    cement["CEMENT PLANT"] -->|clinker dust| brick
    algae -->|biomass| ferm["FERMENTATION PLANT"]
    ferm -->|spent mash| farm["AQUACULTURE FARM"]
    farm -->|waste water organic| biogas["BIOGAS PLANT"]
    biogas -->|methane CO2| power
    brick -->|waste heat| district["DISTRICT HEATING NETWORK"]
    district -->|hot water steam| power
    district -->|hot water| ferm
```

**Reading the diagram:** Every firm's waste becomes another firm's input. **No firm is an island.** This is the *System Approach* in its physical, industrial form — modeled on a natural ecosystem.

## 4.4 Diagram 4 — Feedback Loops in a Green Manufacturing System

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    feedback["PERFORMANCE METRICS ENERGY CO2 WASTE"] -->|data| monitor["MONITORING SYSTEM"]
    monitor -->|corrective signal| control["CONTROL SYSTEM"]
    control -->|adjustment| process["MANUFACTURING PROCESS"]
    process -->|emissions waste products| outputbox["OUTPUT STREAMS"]
    outputbox -->|measured value| feedback
```

This is a **closed-loop control system** — the engineering heart of the System Approach. Without the feedback loop, the system cannot self-correct toward greener performance.

## 4.5 Diagram 5 — Cradle-to-Cradle vs Cradle-to-Grave (Boundary Comparison)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph grave["CRADLE TO GRAVE LINEAR"]
        a1["RAW MAT"] --> a2["MFG"] --> a3["USE"] --> a4["LANDFILL"]
    end
    subgraph cradle["CRADLE TO CRADLE CIRCULAR"]
        b1["RAW MAT"] --> b2["MFG"] --> b3["USE"] --> b4["RECYCLE REMANUFACTURE"]
        b4 -. re enters as input .-> b2
    end
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The KTU "Ideal Boundary"** for any green-manufacturing answer is **Cradle-to-Cradle**. Always mention the boundary you are assuming at the start of any LCA or system-analysis answer. Examiners explicitly test this.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
**Define the "System Approach" in the context of green manufacturing. Mention any two core principles.**

**Model Answer (Board Key Pattern):**
The *System Approach* in green manufacturing is a holistic methodology that views a manufacturing enterprise and its environmental interactions as an interconnected system of material, energy, and information flows, bounded by an explicit boundary, with the goal of simultaneously optimizing environmental, economic, and social performance.

**Two core principles (any two for 3 marks):**
1. **Holism** — the whole system has properties not found in its parts.
2. **Interconnectedness** — every component influences every other through material, energy, and information flows.
3. **Feedback and self-regulation** — systems use feedback loops to maintain dynamic equilibrium and improve performance.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Paper]**
**Differentiate between Material Flow Analysis (MFA) and Substance Flow Analysis (SFA).**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | **Material Flow Analysis (MFA)** | **Substance Flow Analysis (SFA)** |
|--------|----------------------------------|-----------------------------------|
| **Scope** | Tracks *all* material inputs and outputs of a system | Tracks a *specific* substance (e.g., lead, mercury, cadmium) |
| **Granularity** | Broad — usually tons of total material per year | Narrow — kilograms of a single hazardous substance |
| **Purpose** | Overall resource efficiency | Environmental risk assessment of a specific pollutant |
| **Boundary** | National, regional, or sectoral | Often regional or industrial sector |
| **Example** | Tracking total steel flow in India | Tracking mercury flow in the chlor-alkali industry |

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 Rule:** Part B of ESE carries 14 marks. Within a module, you must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B. Each part is 7 marks. The system is the System Approach, so you must draw a flow diagram, define the boundary, and apply a balance equation.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1**

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Adapted]**

> **(a)** Explain the System Approach to green manufacturing. With a neat flowchart, illustrate the Input–Process–Output (I-P-O) model and discuss how the boundary choice affects the analysis. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A manufacturing unit receives $200 \text{ kg/h}$ of mild steel sheets, $15 \text{ kg/h}$ of lubricants, and $50 \text{ kg/h}$ of process water. Outputs are $195 \text{ kg/h}$ of finished panels, $4 \text{ kg/h}$ of reusable scrap, and $7 \text{ kg/h}$ of oily sludge. The plant is at steady state with no chemical reaction. Calculate the **mass of wastewater emitted per hour** and the **material intensity (kg/panel)**, assuming each panel has a finished mass of $2.5 \text{ kg}$. Comment on the greenness of the process. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]**

**Definition (2 marks):** The System Approach in green manufacturing is a holistic methodology that models a manufacturing enterprise as an interconnected system of material, energy, and information flows bounded by an explicit boundary, with the objective of optimizing environmental, economic, and social performance simultaneously.

**I-P-O Flowchart (3 marks):**

```
[Raw Material]   [Energy]
[Water]    ---> [PROCESS] ---> [Product]
                         \---> [By-product]
                         \---> [Emissions: Air, Water, Soil]
                         \---> [Waste Heat]
         (Feedback: by-product reused as input)
```

**Boundary choice discussion (2 marks):** The four standard boundaries are *gate-to-gate* (factory-only), *cradle-to-gate* (raw material to factory), *cradle-to-grave* (raw material to disposal), and *cradle-to-cradle* (raw material back to raw material, fully closed loop). The wider the boundary, the more complete the environmental picture. For *green manufacturing*, **cradle-to-cradle** is the ideal. Choosing a narrow boundary (gate-to-gate) hides upstream extraction impacts and downstream end-of-life impacts — a common greenwashing trap.

#### **Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]**

**Step 1 — Total mass input (1 mark)**

$$\sum \dot{m}_{in} = 200 + 15 + 50 = 265 \text{ kg/h}$$

**Step 2 — Known mass outputs (1 mark)**

$$\sum \dot{m}_{out,\,known} = 195 + 4 + 7 = 206 \text{ kg/h}$$

**Step 3 — Apply steady-state, no-reaction mass balance (1 mark)**

$$\sum \dot{m}_{in} = \sum \dot{m}_{out} \implies 265 = 206 + \dot{m}_{wastewater}$$

**Step 4 — Solve for wastewater (1 mark)**

$$\dot{m}_{wastewater} = 265 - 206 = 59 \text{ kg/h}$$

**Step 5 — Production rate (1 mark)**

$$\dot{N}_{panels} = \frac{195 \text{ kg/h}}{2.5 \text{ kg/panel}} = 78 \text{ panels/h}$$

**Step 6 — Material intensity (1 mark)**

$$MI = \frac{265 \text{ kg/h}}{78 \text{ panels/h}} = 3.40 \text{ kg/panel}$$

(Theoretical minimum = 2.5 kg/panel; excess = 0.90 kg/panel = ≈ 26.5 % over-input.)

**Step 7 — Greenness comment (1 mark):** Approximately $59 \text{ kg/h}$ of wastewater and $0.90 \text{ kg/panel}$ of unaccounted mass (lubricants, water, sludge) represent environmental burden. The plant is **not** fully closed-loop; recommendation is to install an oil–water separator and recycle the water, plus recover the $4 \text{ kg/h}$ of scrap — a classic System-Approach intervention.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2 (Alternative Choice)**

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Adapted]**

> **(a)** Discuss the concept of *Industrial Symbiosis* as an application of the System Approach. With a suitable example (e.g., Kalundborg, Denmark), explain how by-product synergy is achieved. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A foundry uses $1000 \text{ kg}$ of pig iron and $200 \text{ kg}$ of coke per batch. The outputs are $920 \text{ kg}$ of castings, $80 \text{ kg}$ of slag, $90 \text{ kg}$ of coke oven gas, and the balance is flue gas. Calculate the **mass of flue gas emitted per batch** and the **closing-the-loop recycling ratio**, assuming $80 \text{ kg}$ of slag is internally reused as road material within the same industrial estate. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]**

**Definition of Industrial Symbiosis (2 marks):** Industrial Symbiosis (IS) is a sub-domain of the System Approach and Industrial Ecology where geographically co-located firms exchange materials, energy, water, and by-products so that one firm's waste becomes another's resource, mimicking natural ecosystem cycles.

**Principles (1 mark):** (i) Geographic proximity, (ii) complementary waste-resource matching, (iii) multi-firm networked relationships, (iv) long-term cooperation.

**Kalundborg Example (4 marks):** Kalundborg, Denmark is the world's prototypical Eco-Industrial Park. Key exchanges include:
- **Asnæs Power Plant** → supplies steam to *Novozymes* and *Novo Nordisk*, and fly-ash to cement manufacturers.
- **Statoil Refinery** → supplies desulphurisation gas (used to make sulphuric acid) to *Gyproc* (gypsum board manufacturer).
- **Novo Nordisk** → supplies sludge and yeast residue to farmers as fertilizer.
- **Fish farms** → use cooling water from the power plant.
- **Inter-firm water pipeline** → treated wastewater is reused across the park.

Result: **CO₂ emissions reduced by ~25 %, fresh water use cut by ~60 %, and significant cost savings** — proof that the System Approach delivers both environmental and economic gains.

#### **Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]**

**Step 1 — Total input mass (1 mark)**

$$\sum m_{in} = 1000 + 200 = 1200 \text{ kg/batch}$$

**Step 2 — Known output mass (1 mark)**

$$\sum m_{out,\,known} = 920 + 80 + 90 = 1090 \text{ kg/batch}$$

**Step 3 — Mass balance (1 mark)**

$$1200 = 1090 + m_{flue\,gas} \implies m_{flue\,gas} = 110 \text{ kg/batch}$$

**Step 4 — Closing-the-loop ratio (2 marks)**

$$R = \frac{m_{recycled}}{m_{total\,input}} \times 100\% = \frac{80}{1200} \times 100\% = 6.67\%$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation and improvement (2 marks):** $R = 6.67\%$ is very low. Recommendation: route the $110 \text{ kg}$ of flue gas to a heat-recovery boiler (energy symbiosis) and investigate re-melting of the $80 \text{ kg}$ slag as a flux in the next batch (material symbiosis). The System Approach would push $R$ toward 50–80 % by internal recycling plus by-product exchange with neighbouring firms in an Eco-Industrial Park.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in System-Approach Questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting to state the system boundary** explicitly at the start of the answer. Examiners deduct 1–2 marks for this.
> 2. **Confusing Material Flow Analysis (MFA) with Substance Flow Analysis (SFA).** MFA = total mass; SFA = one specific substance.
> 3. **Mixing up cradle-to-grave with cradle-to-cradle.** The latter is *closed loop*; the former is *linear with disposal*.
> 4. **Ignoring the steady-state assumption.** For continuous processes you must say "at steady state, $dM/dt = 0$" before writing the mass balance.
> 5. **Reporting a material intensity without units.** Always write **kg/panel**, **kg/unit**, or **kg/kWh** — examiners check units.
> 6. **Treating the System Approach as a single tool.** It is a *family* of methods: MFA, SFA, EFA, LCA, Industrial Symbiosis, and System Dynamics.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **System Approach = holistic view** of manufacturing as a flow system of materials, energy, and information.
- **I-P-O Model:** Inputs → Process → Outputs + Environmental Burden; goal is to *close the loop* and minimize burden.
- **System boundary** must be declared: *gate-to-gate*, *cradle-to-gate*, *cradle-to-grave*, or *cradle-to-cradle* (ideal).
- **Universal mass balance:** $\sum m_{in} = \sum m_{out} + \dfrac{dM}{dt} - \sum r_{reaction}$.
- **Universal energy balance:** $\sum E_{in} = \sum E_{out} + \dfrac{dE}{dt}$.
- **Three core KPIs:** Material Intensity (kg/unit), Energy Intensity (MJ/unit), Pollution Index (kg/unit).
- **Key tools:** MFA, SFA, EFA, LCA, Industrial Symbiosis, System Dynamics.
- **Industrial Symbiosis** = by-product synergy between firms; **Kalundborg (Denmark)** is the textbook example.
- **Closing-the-loop ratio** $R = m_{recycled} / m_{total\,input} \times 100\%$; aim for $R \to 100\%$.
- **Hierarchy of systems:** Product → Process → Factory → Eco-Industrial Park → Region → Nation → Biosphere.
- **Triple Bottom Line:** Planet (environment), People (social), Profit (economic) — all three optimized together.
- **Traditional vs System Approach:** Reductionist vs Holistic; Local profit vs Global sustainability; End-of-pipe vs Design-for-Environment.
- **Five principles:** Holism, Interconnectedness, Hierarchy, I-T-O, Dynamic Equilibrium with Feedback.
- **For continuous processes:** assume **steady state** ($dM/dt = 0$) unless the question says otherwise.
- **Always quote units** (kg/h, kg/panel, MJ/unit, kg CO₂-eq) — marks are deducted for missing units.
- **The "ideal" boundary in any KTU answer is Cradle-to-Cradle** — say it, justify it, and proceed.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Weightage
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET753 - GREEN MANUFACTURING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Weightage

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Weightage** in the context of Green Manufacturing refers to a **quantitative prioritization technique** used to assign relative importance (priority coefficients) to multiple environmental impact categories, sustainability indicators, or evaluation criteria within a multi-criteria decision-making (MCDM) framework. It transforms qualitative judgments about environmental significance into numerical coefficients that aggregate heterogeneous metrics into a single composite score.

Mathematically, a weight $w_i$ is a non-negative number assigned to criterion $i$ such that:

$$0 \le w_i \le 1, \quad \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i = 1$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** Weightage is the systematic assignment of numerical importance values to environmental, economic, and social indicators so that their combined effect can be computed for evaluating green manufacturing processes, products, or systems.

The general **Weighted Sum Model (WSM)** score for an alternative $A_j$ across $n$ criteria is given by:

$$S(A_j) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i \cdot a_{ij}$$

where $a_{ij}$ is the performance score of alternative $j$ on criterion $i$.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The School Report Card":**
> Imagine a school uses six subjects (Mathematics, Science, English, Hindi, Social Studies, Computer) to grade a student. But not all subjects carry equal importance for an engineering aspirant. The school may assign:
> - Mathematics → **0.30** (most important)
> - Science → **0.25**
> - English → **0.15**
> - Computer → **0.15**
> - Social Studies → **0.10**
> - Hindi → **0.05**

> The student's final "merit score" is calculated by multiplying each subject's grade by its **weightage** and summing them up. This is *exactly* how green manufacturing works: when a factory is evaluated on 6 environmental criteria (CO₂, water use, waste, energy, toxicity, recyclability), each criterion is given a *weightage* based on its regional/global importance, and the final "green score" is the weighted sum.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics in Green Manufacturing Weightage

| Metric Category | Typical Indicator | Range |
|---|---|---|
| **GWP** (Global Warming Potential) | kg CO₂-eq | **0 – 1** (normalized) |
| **AP** (Acidification Potential) | kg SO₂-eq | **0 – 1** |
| **EP** (Eutrophication Potential) | kg PO₄-eq | **0 – 1** |
| **HTP** (Human Toxicity Potential) | kg 1,4-DCB-eq | **0 – 1** |
| **POCP** (Photochemical Ozone Creation) | kg C₂H₄-eq | **0 – 1** |
| **ADP** (Abiotic Depletion Potential) | kg Sb-eq | **0 – 1** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All impact category scores are **normalized** to a [0,1] scale before applying weightage, ensuring no single criterion mathematically dominates due to unit differences.

## 1.4 Why Weightage Matters in Green Manufacturing

1. **Decision Aggregation** — Combines conflicting metrics (e.g., cost vs. emissions) into one comparable value.
2. **Stakeholder Reflection** — Captures regional, regulatory, and corporate priorities.
3. **LCA Endpoint Modeling** — Required for converting mid-point indicators to damage categories.
4. **Certification Compatibility** — ISO 14040, ISO 14044, and Eco-Indicator 95/99 methodologies all require weightage assignment.
5. **Product Comparison** — Enables side-by-side green product scoring (e.g., Type I Ecolabels).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation — The Three Pillars of Weightage

### Pillar 1: Normalization (Pre-requisite)
Before weightage can be applied, raw impact scores must be **normalized** to a common dimensionless scale. The two dominant methods are:

**Method A — Reference Value Normalization:**

$$a_{ij}^{\text{norm}} = \frac{a_{ij}}{a_{i,\text{ref}}}$$

where $a_{i,\text{ref}}$ is the European or global reference inventory value for category $i$.

**Method B — Distance-to-Target Normalization:**

$$a_{ij}^{\text{norm}} = \frac{a_{ij}}{a_{i,\text{target}}}$$

### Pillar 2: Weight Determination
Three principal approaches are accepted under KTU/Green Manufacturing syllabus:

1. **Equal Weightage (Panel/Default):** $w_i = \frac{1}{n}$
2. **Expert-Based Weightage (AHP — Analytic Hierarchy Process):** Pairwise comparison matrix.
3. **Distance-Based Weightage (Entropy Method):** Objective, data-driven.

### Pillar 3: Aggregation
The final composite green score uses either:
- **Weighted Sum Model (WSM)** — additive
- **Weighted Product Model (WPM)** — multiplicative

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Logical Breakdown — Analytic Hierarchy Process (AHP)

AHP, developed by **Thomas L. Saaty (1980)**, is the most examiner-preferred weightage method in KTU papers. The procedure is:

1. **Define the goal** (e.g., "Rank manufacturing processes by greenness").
2. **Identify criteria** (energy, emissions, water, waste, cost).
3. **Construct pairwise comparison matrix** $\mathbf{A} = [a_{ij}]$ where $a_{ij}$ is Saaty's 1–9 scale rating.
4. **Reciprocity property enforced:** $a_{ji} = 1/a_{ij}$.
5. **Compute principal eigenvector** of $\mathbf{A}$ — this gives the weights.
6. **Verify consistency** using the **Consistency Ratio (CR)**; require $CR < 0.10$.

## 2.3 Entropy-Based Objective Weightage

When expert opinion is unavailable, the **Shannon Entropy** method derives weights purely from data variance.

For a normalized decision matrix $\mathbf{R} = [r_{ij}]_{m \times n}$:

**Step 1 — Proportion calculation:**

$$p_{ij} = \frac{r_{ij}}{\sum_{j=1}^{m} r_{ij}}$$

**Step 2 — Entropy of criterion $i$:**

$$E_i = -k \sum_{j=1}^{m} p_{ij} \ln(p_{ij}), \quad k = \frac{1}{\ln(m)}$$

**Step 3 — Degree of diversification (divergence):**

$$d_i = 1 - E_i$$

**Step 4 — Entropy weight:**

$$w_i = \frac{d_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} d_i}$$

> [!NOTE]
> A criterion with **higher variance** across alternatives receives a **higher weight** because it provides more discriminating information — a cornerstone of information theory applied to green evaluation.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Boundary / Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\sum w_i = 1$ | Normalization of weights | Dimensionless |
| 2 | $S_j = \sum w_i \cdot a_{ij}$ | Weighted Sum (WSM) score | Dimensionless |
| 3 | $P_j = \prod a_{ij}^{w_i}$ | Weighted Product (WPM) score | Dimensionless |
| 4 | $CR = \frac{CI}{RI}$ | Consistency Ratio (AHP) | $CR < 0.10$ |
| 5 | $CI = \frac{\lambda_{\max} - n}{n - 1}$ | Consistency Index | $\lambda_{\max} \ge n$ |
| 6 | $E_i = -\frac{1}{\ln m} \sum p_{ij} \ln p_{ij}$ | Shannon Entropy | $0 \le E_i \le 1$ |
| 7 | $w_i = \frac{1 - E_i}{\sum (1 - E_i)}$ | Entropy weight | $0 \le w_i \le 1$ |
| 8 | $a_{ij}^{\text{norm}} = \frac{a_{ij}}{a_{i,\text{ref}}}$ | Reference normalization | $0 \le a^{\text{norm}} \le 1$ |
| 9 | $\text{Eco-Indicator} = \sum w_i \cdot \text{Impact}_i$ | Eco-Indicator 95/99 | Eco-points |
| 10 | $RCR = \frac{\text{Renewable Resource Use}}{\text{Total Resource Use}}$ | Renewable Content Ratio | $0 \le RCR \le 1$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Random Index (RI) values for AHP** — examiners may provide or expect you to know:
> $n=1: 0.00$, $n=2: 0.00$, $n=3: 0.58$, $n=4: 0.90$, $n=5: 1.12$, $n=6: 1.24$, $n=7: 1.32$, $n=8: 1.41$, $n=9: 1.45$, $n=10: 1.49$.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Eco-Design Decisions** — Philips, Toyota, and Unilever use AHP-derived weightage to compare competing product designs.
- **Policy Setting** — EU PEF (Product Environmental Footprint) mandates equal weightage for 16 impact categories.
- **Green Supplier Selection** — Weightage integrates environmental + cost + quality criteria.
- **Carbon Footprint Labeling** — India's Bharat Stage norms implicitly assign weightage to GWP, NOx, PM.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Derivation 1 — Entropy Weightage (Complete)

**Problem Statement:** A factory evaluates 4 manufacturing processes ($P_1, P_2, P_3, P_4$) across 3 environmental criteria: Energy Use (E), CO₂ Emissions (C), Water Consumption (W). The raw scores are:

| Process | Energy (MJ) | CO₂ (kg) | Water (L) |
|---|---|---|---|
| $P_1$ | 120 | 8.0 | 50 |
| $P_2$ | 80 | 6.5 | 70 |
| $P_3$ | 150 | 9.5 | 40 |
| $P_4$ | 100 | 7.0 | 60 |

Find the entropy-based weightage and rank the processes.

### Step 1 — Construct the decision matrix
$$\mathbf{R} = \begin{bmatrix} 120 & 8.0 & 50 \\ 80 & 6.5 & 70 \\ 150 & 9.5 & 40 \\ 100 & 7.0 & 60 \end{bmatrix}$$

### Step 2 — Compute column sums (denominator for proportions)
For Energy: $120 + 80 + 150 + 100 = 450$
For CO₂: $8.0 + 6.5 + 9.5 + 7.0 = 31.0$
For Water: $50 + 70 + 40 + 60 = 220$

### Step 3 — Compute proportion matrix $p_{ij}$

$$p_{ij} = \frac{r_{ij}}{\sum_j r_{ij}}$$

For Energy column:
$$p_{11} = \frac{120}{450} = 0.2667, \quad p_{21} = \frac{80}{450} = 0.1778$$
$$p_{31} = \frac{150}{450} = 0.3333, \quad p_{41} = \frac{100}{450} = 0.2222$$

For CO₂ column:
$$p_{12} = \frac{8.0}{31.0} = 0.2581, \quad p_{22} = \frac{6.5}{31.0} = 0.2097$$
$$p_{32} = \frac{9.5}{31.0} = 0.3065, \quad p_{42} = \frac{7.0}{31.0} = 0.2258$$

For Water column:
$$p_{13} = \frac{50}{220} = 0.2273, \quad p_{23} = \frac{70}{220} = 0.3182$$
$$p_{33} = \frac{40}{220} = 0.1818, \quad p_{43} = \frac{60}{220} = 0.2727$$

### Step 4 — Compute entropy for each criterion

Use $k = \frac{1}{\ln(4)} = \frac{1}{1.3863} = 0.7213$.

**Entropy for Energy ($E_1$):**

$$E_1 = -0.7213 \Big[ 0.2667 \ln(0.2667) + 0.1778 \ln(0.1778) + 0.3333 \ln(0.3333) + 0.2222 \ln(0.2222) \Big]$$

Computing term by term:
- $0.2667 \times (-1.3218) = -0.3525$
- $0.1778 \times (-1.7272) = -0.3071$
- $0.3333 \times (-1.0986) = -0.3662$
- $0.2222 \times (-1.5041) = -0.3342$

Sum $= -1.3600$. Therefore:

$$E_1 = -0.7213 \times (-1.3600) = 0.9810$$

**Entropy for CO₂ ($E_2$):**
- $0.2581 \times (-1.3552) = -0.3498$
- $0.2097 \times (-1.5610) = -0.3273$
- $0.3065 \times (-1.1827) = -0.3625$
- $0.2258 \times (-1.4884) = -0.3361$

Sum $= -1.3757$. Therefore:

$$E_2 = -0.7213 \times (-1.3757) = 0.9924$$

**Entropy for Water ($E_3$):**
- $0.2273 \times (-1.4816) = -0.3367$
- $0.3182 \times (-1.1451) = -0.3644$
- $0.1818 \times (-1.7047) = -0.3099$
- $0.2727 \times (-1.2993) = -0.3543$

Sum $= -1.3653$. Therefore:

$$E_3 = -0.7213 \times (-1.3653) = 0.9848$$

### Step 5 — Compute divergence

$$d_1 = 1 - 0.9810 = 0.0190$$
$$d_2 = 1 - 0.9924 = 0.0076$$
$$d_3 = 1 - 0.9848 = 0.0152$$

### Step 6 — Compute entropy weights

Sum of divergences: $0.0190 + 0.0076 + 0.0152 = 0.0418$

$$w_1 = \frac{0.0190}{0.0418} = 0.4545$$
$$w_2 = \frac{0.0076}{0.0418} = 0.1818$$
$$w_3 = \frac{0.0152}{0.0418} = 0.3636$$

**Verification:** $0.4545 + 0.1818 + 0.3636 = 0.9999 \approx 1.00$ ✓

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering interpretation:** Energy receives the highest weight (45.45%) because its values vary most across the four processes (80–150 MJ) — this gives maximum discriminating power. CO₂ has the lowest weight (18.18%) because all processes have similar CO₂ footprints.

## 3.2 Worked Derivation 2 — AHP Pairwise Consistency Check

**Problem:** A pairwise comparison matrix for 3 criteria (C₁, C₂, C₃) is given. Verify consistency.

$$\mathbf{A} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 3 & 5 \\ 1/3 & 1 & 2 \\ 1/5 & 1/2 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

### Step 1 — Column normalization
Col 1 sum = $1 + 0.333 + 0.200 = 1.533$
Col 2 sum = $3 + 1 + 0.5 = 4.500$
Col 3 sum = $5 + 2 + 1 = 8.000$

Normalized matrix:
$$\mathbf{A}_{\text{norm}} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.652 & 0.667 & 0.625 \\ 0.217 & 0.222 & 0.250 \\ 0.130 & 0.111 & 0.125 \end{bmatrix}$$

### Step 2 — Row averages = priority vector (weights)
$$w_1 = (0.652 + 0.667 + 0.625)/3 = 0.648$$
$$w_2 = (0.217 + 0.222 + 0.250)/3 = 0.230$$
$$w_3 = (0.130 + 0.111 + 0.125)/3 = 0.122$$

**Verification:** $0.648 + 0.230 + 0.122 = 1.000$ ✓

### Step 3 — Compute $\lambda_{\max}$

$$\mathbf{A} \cdot \mathbf{w} = \begin{bmatrix} 1(0.648) + 3(0.230) + 5(0.122) \\ (1/3)(0.648) + 1(0.230) + 2(0.122) \\ (1/5)(0.648) + (1/2)(0.230) + 1(0.122) \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 2.074 \\ 0.690 \\ 0.367 \end{bmatrix}$$

$$\lambda_i = \frac{(\mathbf{Aw})_i}{w_i}$$
$$\lambda_1 = 2.074/0.648 = 3.200, \quad \lambda_2 = 0.690/0.230 = 3.000, \quad \lambda_3 = 0.367/0.122 = 3.008$$

$$\lambda_{\max} = \frac{3.200 + 3.000 + 3.008}{3} = 3.069$$

### Step 4 — Consistency Index and Ratio

$$CI = \frac{\lambda_{\max} - n}{n - 1} = \frac{3.069 - 3}{3 - 1} = \frac{0.069}{2} = 0.0345$$

For $n=3$, $RI = 0.58$:

$$CR = \frac{CI}{RI} = \frac{0.0345}{0.58} = 0.0595$$

**Conclusion:** $CR = 0.0595 < 0.10$ ✓ — **Consistency is acceptable.** The pairwise judgments are reliable.

## 3.3 Composite Green Score (WSM Application)

Using the entropy weights from §3.1 ($w_1 = 0.4545, w_2 = 0.1818, w_3 = 0.3636$), we compute a composite green score for each process. Since *lower* raw scores are better, we use the reciprocal or normalized form:

$$S_j = \sum_{i=1}^{3} w_i \cdot \left( \frac{a_{i,\min}}{a_{ij}} \right)$$

where $a_{i,\min}$ is the minimum (best) value in column $i$.

- $E_{\min} = 80$, $C_{\min} = 6.5$, $W_{\min} = 40$

| Process | $w_1(E_{\min}/E)$ | $w_2(C_{\min}/C)$ | $w_3(W_{\min}/W)$ | $S_j$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| $P_1$ | $0.4545 \times 80/120 = 0.3030$ | $0.1818 \times 6.5/8.0 = 0.1477$ | $0.3636 \times 40/50 = 0.2909$ | **0.7416** |
| $P_2$ | $0.4545 \times 80/80 = 0.4545$ | $0.1818 \times 6.5/6.5 = 0.1818$ | $0.3636 \times 40/70 = 0.2078$ | **0.8441** |
| $P_3$ | $0.4545 \times 80/150 = 0.2424$ | $0.1818 \times 6.5/9.5 = 0.1244$ | $0.3636 \times 40/40 = 0.3636$ | **0.7304** |
| $P_4$ | $0.4545 \times 80/100 = 0.3636$ | $0.1818 \times 6.5/7.0 = 0.1688$ | $0.3636 \times 40/60 = 0.2424$ | **0.7748** |

### Final Ranking
$$\boxed{P_2 (0.8441) \succ P_4 (0.7748) \succ P_1 (0.7416) \succ P_3 (0.7304)}$$

> **Process $P_2$ is the greenest** — highest composite score, indicating the best balance of energy, emissions, and water performance under objective entropy-based weightage.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Weightage Methodology

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Goal Definition: Rank Green Manufacturing Alternatives] --> B[Identify n Environmental Criteria]
    B --> C[Collect Raw Impact Scores aij]
    C --> D[Normalization Stage]
    D --> D1[Reference Value Method]
    D --> D2[Distance to Target Method]
    D --> D3[Min Max Scaling]
    D1 --> E[Weight Determination Stage]
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
    E --> E1[Equal Weightage]
    E --> E2[AHP Expert Based]
    E --> E3[Entropy Objective]
    E --> E4[Delphi Iterative]
    E1 --> F[Aggregation Stage]
    E2 --> F
    E3 --> F
    E4 --> F
    F --> F1[Weighted Sum Model WSM]
    F --> F2[Weighted Product Model WPM]
    F --> F3[TOPSIS Ranking]
    F1 --> G[Consistency Validation]
    F2 --> G
    F3 --> G
    G --> G1[Check CR less than 0.10]
    G --> G2[Sensitivity Analysis]
    G1 --> H[Final Green Score Sj]
    G2 --> H
    H --> I[Rank Alternatives and Recommend]
```

## 4.2 Saaty 1–9 Scale Reference (For AHP Pairwise)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[1 Equal Importance] --> S2[3 Moderate]
    S2 --> S3[5 Strong]
    S3 --> S4[7 Very Strong]
    S4 --> S5[9 Extreme]
    S5 --> S6[2 4 6 8 Intermediate]
    S6 --> S7[Reciprocals 1/n for inverse]
```

## 4.3 Entropy Weightage Computation Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    M[Start: Decision Matrix R m x n] --> N[Step 1: Normalize each column]
    N --> O[Step 2: Compute proportions pij = rij / sum rj]
    O --> P[Step 3: Compute entropy Ei using k = 1/ln m]
    P --> Q[Step 4: Compute divergence di = 1 minus Ei]
    Q --> R[Step 5: Compute weight wi = di / sum di]
    R --> S[Step 6: Verify sum wi = 1]
    S --> T[Step 7: Apply WSM to get Sj]
    T --> U[Step 8: Rank alternatives]
```

## 4.4 Data Flow: From Raw Impact to Green Decision

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Raw[Raw Impact Data] --> Norm[Normalization]
    Norm --> Score[Normalized Score Matrix]
    Score --> Weight[Weight Vector]
    Weight --> Dot[Dot Product]
    Score --> Dot
    Dot --> Result[Composite Green Score]
    Result --> Rank[Final Ranking]
    Rank --> Dec[Manufacturing Decision]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**
**Define "weightage" in the context of green manufacturing evaluation. State any two methods used to assign weightage to environmental criteria.**

**Model Answer:**
Weightage in green manufacturing is the assignment of numerical importance values (weights $w_i$) to multiple environmental criteria such that $\sum w_i = 1$, enabling aggregation of heterogeneous impact scores into a single composite green index.

**Two methods:**
1. **Analytic Hierarchy Process (AHP)** — Saaty's pairwise comparison method using a 1–9 scale.
2. **Entropy Method** — Objective, data-driven approach based on Shannon's information theory where higher-variance criteria receive higher weights.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
**Differentiate between subjective and objective weightage methods. Give one example of each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Subjective Weightage | Objective Weightage |
|---|---|---|
| Source | Expert judgment / stakeholder opinion | Mathematical/computational derivation from data |
| Bias | Prone to expert bias | Bias-free, data-driven |
| Example | AHP, Delphi Method | Entropy Method, CRITIC, Standard Deviation Method |
| Data requirement | Pairwise comparison matrix | Decision matrix with performance values |
| Consistency check | Required (CR < 0.10) | Not required |

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks) — CHOICE 1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model]**

**(a)** Explain the Analytic Hierarchy Process (AHP) for weightage determination in green manufacturing. Discuss the Saaty 1–9 scale and the procedure to compute the priority vector. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A factory compares 3 manufacturing routes using 4 environmental criteria: Energy (E), Emissions (C), Water (W), and Waste (S). The normalized score matrix and expert-aggregated weights are:

| Route | E | C | W | S |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| R₁ | 0.7 | 0.6 | 0.8 | 0.5 |
| R₂ | 0.8 | 0.9 | 0.6 | 0.7 |
| R₃ | 0.6 | 0.7 | 0.9 | 0.8 |

Weights: $w_E = 0.40, w_C = 0.30, w_W = 0.20, w_S = 0.10$.

Using the Weighted Sum Model, rank the routes. Which is the most green route? **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**AHP Procedure:**
1. **Define the decision goal** and decompose it into hierarchical levels: Goal → Criteria → Sub-criteria → Alternatives.
2. **Construct pairwise comparison matrix** $\mathbf{A}_{n \times n}$ using Saaty's 1–9 scale:
   - 1 = Equal importance
   - 3 = Moderate importance
   - 5 = Strong importance
   - 7 = Very strong importance
   - 9 = Extreme importance
   - Reciprocals (1/2, 1/3, ..., 1/9) for inverse preferences
3. **Compute the priority vector** (weights) as the normalized principal eigenvector of $\mathbf{A}$. Approximation methods:
   - Column normalization + row averaging (used in this question)
   - Geometric mean method
4. **Compute $\lambda_{\max}$** = average of $(\mathbf{Aw})_i / w_i$.
5. **Compute Consistency Index:** $CI = (\lambda_{\max} - n)/(n-1)$.
6. **Compute Consistency Ratio:** $CR = CI/RI$. Accept if $CR < 0.10$.
7. **Aggregate weights** through hierarchy if multiple levels exist.

The Saaty 1–9 scale captures relative judgment in a structured, mathematically tractable form, transforming qualitative expert opinion into quantitative weights with built-in consistency verification — making it ideal for green manufacturing decisions where criteria like emissions vs. cost must be balanced.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

Apply the WSM formula:

$$S_j = \sum_{i=1}^{4} w_i \cdot a_{ij}$$

**For Route R₁:**
$$S_1 = (0.40 \times 0.7) + (0.30 \times 0.6) + (0.20 \times 0.8) + (0.10 \times 0.5)$$
$$S_1 = 0.28 + 0.18 + 0.16 + 0.05 = \mathbf{0.67}$$

**For Route R₂:**
$$S_2 = (0.40 \times 0.8) + (0.30 \times 0.9) + (0.20 \times 0.6) + (0.10 \times 0.7)$$
$$S_2 = 0.32 + 0.27 + 0.12 + 0.07 = \mathbf{0.78}$$

**For Route R₃:**
$$S_3 = (0.40 \times 0.6) + (0.30 \times 0.7) + (0.20 \times 0.9) + (0.10 \times 0.8)$$
$$S_3 = 0.24 + 0.21 + 0.18 + 0.08 = \mathbf{0.71}$$

**Final Ranking:**
$$\boxed{R_2 (0.78) \succ R_3 (0.71) \succ R_1 (0.67)}$$

**Route R₂ is the most green manufacturing route** under the given weightage and normalized scores.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — CHOICE 2 (Alternative to Question A)
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**

**(a)** With a suitable mathematical derivation, explain the Entropy method of objective weightage. Show that criteria with higher variance receive higher weights. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Four alternative materials (M₁, M₂, M₃, M₄) are evaluated on three environmental criteria. The raw data matrix is:

| Material | Energy (MJ/kg) | CO₂ (kg/kg) | Water (L/kg) |
|---|---|---|---|
| M₁ | 200 | 4.0 | 80 |
| M₂ | 150 | 3.0 | 100 |
| M₃ | 250 | 5.0 | 60 |
| M₄ | 180 | 3.5 | 90 |

Compute the entropy-based weights and rank the materials using WSM (treating lower impact as greener). **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Entropy Method Derivation:**

Consider a normalized decision matrix $\mathbf{R} = [r_{ij}]_{m \times n}$ where $m$ = alternatives and $n$ = criteria.

**Step 1 — Proportion Matrix:**
The information content of value $r_{ij}$ relative to column $i$ is captured by:

$$p_{ij} = \frac{r_{ij}}{\sum_{j=1}^{m} r_{ij}}, \quad \sum_{j=1}^{m} p_{ij} = 1$$

**Step 2 — Shannon Entropy of Criterion $i$:**

$$E_i = -k \sum_{j=1}^{m} p_{ij} \ln(p_{ij})$$

where $k = \frac{1}{\ln m}$ ensures $0 \le E_i \le 1$.

**Mathematical justification for variance–weight relationship:**

For a fixed column, consider the entropy inequality. When all $p_{ij} = 1/m$ (uniform distribution), entropy attains its **maximum** $E_i^{\max} = 1$. When data is concentrated (low variance), entropy is small. The divergence:

$$d_i = 1 - E_i$$

is therefore a **measure of variability** — high $d_i$ ⇒ high data spread ⇒ high discriminative power. Since:

$$w_i = \frac{d_i}{\sum_{i} d_i}$$

**Q.E.D.: Criteria with higher variance receive higher weightage.**

This makes engineering sense: a criterion that varies significantly across alternatives is more useful for *distinguishing* them, so it deserves greater weight in the final ranking.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step 1 — Column sums:**
- Energy: $200 + 150 + 250 + 180 = 780$
- CO₂: $4.0 + 3.0 + 5.0 + 3.5 = 15.5$
- Water: $80 + 100 + 60 + 90 = 330$

**Step 2 — Proportion matrix:**

| | E | C | W |
|---|---|---|---|
| M₁ | 0.2564 | 0.2581 | 0.2424 |
| M₂ | 0.1923 | 0.1935 | 0.3030 |
| M₃ | 0.3205 | 0.3226 | 0.1818 |
| M₄ | 0.2308 | 0.2258 | 0.2727 |

**Step 3 — Compute $k = 1/\ln(4) = 0.7213$**

**Entropy for Energy ($E_E$):**
- $0.2564 \ln(0.2564) = 0.2564 \times (-1.3601) = -0.3487$
- $0.1923 \ln(0.1923) = 0.1923 \times (-1.6485) = -0.3170$
- $0.3205 \ln(0.3205) = 0.3205 \times (-1.1378) = -0.3647$
- $0.2308 \ln(0.2308) = 0.2308 \times (-1.4663) = -0.3384$

Sum $= -1.3688$

$$E_E = -0.7213 \times (-1.3688) = 0.9873$$

**Entropy for CO₂ ($E_C$):**
- $0.2581 \times (-1.3542) = -0.3495$
- $0.1935 \times (-1.6424) = -0.3178$
- $0.3226 \times (-1.1314) = -0.3650$
- $0.2258 \times (-1.4877) = -0.3359$

Sum $= -1.3682$

$$E_C = -0.7213 \times (-1.3682) = 0.9868$$

**Entropy for Water ($E_W$):**
- $0.2424 \times (-1.4170) = -0.3435$
- $0.3030 \times (-1.1939) = -0.3618$
- $0.1818 \times (-1.7047) = -0.3099$
- $0.2727 \times (-1.2993) = -0.3543$

Sum $= -1.3695$

$$E_W = -0.7213 \times (-1.3695) = 0.9877$$

**Step 4 — Divergences:**
- $d_E = 1 - 0.9873 = 0.0127$
- $d_C = 1 - 0.9868 = 0.0132$
- $d_W = 1 - 0.9877 = 0.0123$

Sum $= 0.0382$

**Step 5 — Entropy weights:**
$$w_E = 0.0127/0.0382 = 0.3325$$
$$w_C = 0.0132/0.0382 = 0.3455$$
$$w_W = 0.0123/0.0382 = 0.3220$$

**Verification:** $0.3325 + 0.3455 + 0.3220 = 1.000$ ✓

**Step 6 — WSM Ranking (lower impact = greener, so use $a_{\min}/a_{ij}$):**

- $E_{\min} = 150$, $C_{\min} = 3.0$, $W_{\min} = 60$

| Material | $w_E \cdot 150/E$ | $w_C \cdot 3.0/C$ | $w_W \cdot 60/W$ | $S_j$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| M₁ | $0.3325 \times 0.75 = 0.2494$ | $0.3455 \times 0.75 = 0.2591$ | $0.3220 \times 0.75 = 0.2415$ | **0.7500** |
| M₂ | $0.3325 \times 1.00 = 0.3325$ | $0.3455 \times 1.00 = 0.3455$ | $0.3220 \times 0.60 = 0.1932$ | **0.8712** |
| M₃ | $0.3325 \times 0.60 = 0.1995$ | $0.3455 \times 0.60 = 0.2073$ | $0.3220 \times 1.00 = 0.3220$ | **0.7288** |
| M₄ | $0.3325 \times 0.833 = 0.2771$ | $0.3455 \times 0.857 = 0.2962$ | $0.3220 \times 0.667 = 0.2147$ | **0.7880** |

**Final Ranking:**
$$\boxed{M_2 (0.8712) \succ M_4 (0.7880) \succ M_1 (0.7500) \succ M_3 (0.7288)}$$

**Material M₂ is the greenest choice** under entropy-based weightage.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting to verify $\sum w_i = 1$** — students often lose 1 mark for not validating weight normalization.
> 2. **Confusing "lower = better" vs. "higher = better"** — when applying WSM for green metrics, always use $a_{\min}/a_{ij}$ or normalize to *higher = greener*.
> 3. **Skipping the consistency check** in AHP — $CR$ must be reported; $CR > 0.10$ means the matrix must be revised.
> 4. **Unit mismatch** — never apply weightage to non-normalized values with different units (kg, MJ, L). Always normalize first.
> 5. **Forgetting the $k$ constant** in entropy — the formula $E_i = -k \sum p_{ij} \ln p_{ij}$ with $k = 1/\ln(m)$ is required; writing $-\sum p \ln p$ alone is incomplete.
> 6. **Miscounting RI** — for $n = 4$, $RI = 0.90$, not $0.58$. Memorize the table.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Weightage in Green Manufacturing**

- [x] **Definition:** Weightage is the assignment of numerical importance to environmental criteria, satisfying $0 \le w_i \le 1$ and $\sum w_i = 1$.
- [x] **Two broad families:** Subjective (AHP, Delphi) and Objective (Entropy, CRITIC, Std. Deviation).
- [x] **AHP Steps:** Goal → Pairwise matrix → Normalize → Average rows → Get weights → Compute $\lambda_{\max}$ → Calculate $CR$ → Accept if $CR < 0.10$.
- [x] **Entropy Steps:** Build decision matrix → Proportions → Entropy $E_i$ → Divergence $d_i$ → Weight $w_i = d_i / \sum d_i$.
- [x] **WSM Formula:** $S_j = \sum w_i \cdot a_{ij}$ — additive aggregation.
- [x] **WPM Formula:** $P_j = \prod a_{ij}^{w_i}$ — multiplicative aggregation.
- [x] **Consistency Index:** $CI = (\lambda_{\max} - n)/(n - 1)$.
- [x] **Consistency Ratio:** $CR = CI/RI$; threshold = 0.10.
- [x] **RI Table (key values):** n=3 → 0.58; n=4 → 0.90; n=5 → 1.12; n=6 → 1.24.
- [x] **Normalization Pre-requisite:** All criteria must be on the same dimensionless scale before applying weights.
- [x] **Direction matters:** For green metrics, "lower impact = better" — invert with $a_{\min}/a_{ij}$ before WSM.
- [x] **Real-world use:** Eco-design (Philips), supplier selection (Toyota), EU PEF, ISO 14040/44 LCA.
- [x] **High-variance criterion → high entropy weight** (information-theoretic principle).
- [x] **Eco-Indicator 95/99** uses equal weightage; **ReCiPe** uses hierarchist/individualist/egalitarian perspectives.
- [x] **AHP advantages:** Built-in consistency check, expert knowledge integration.
- [x] **Entropy advantages:** Bias-free, fully reproducible, no expert required.
- [x] **Composite Green Score** = $\sum w_i \cdot (\text{normalized green performance}_i)$.
- [x] **KTU favourite question pattern:** "Compute entropy-based weights and rank using WSM" — practice this template thoroughly.
- [x] **Always** show: (1) proportion table, (2) entropy table, (3) divergence, (4) weights, (5) verification $\sum w_i = 1$, (6) final ranking.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Assessment Process
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET753 - GREEN MANUFACTURING
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Assessment Process

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Assessment Process** in Green Manufacturing refers to the systematic, standardized methodology used to quantitatively evaluate the environmental burdens and potential impacts associated with a product, process, or service throughout its entire life cycle. The most globally recognized framework for this process is the **Life Cycle Assessment (LCA)**, formally codified under the **ISO 14040:2006** and **ISO 14044:2006** standards.

According to KTU 2024 syllabus nomenclature, the Assessment Process comprises four interrelated phases:

1. **Goal and Scope Definition**
2. **Life Cycle Inventory (LCI) Analysis**
3. **Life Cycle Impact Assessment (LCIA)**
4. **Interpretation**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The Assessment Process is the quantitative backbone of Green Manufacturing. Without a standardized assessment, claims of "eco-friendly" or "green" products lack scientific credibility and are rejected under international trade regulations (e.g., EU Carbon Border Adjustment Mechanism).

> [!NOTE]
> **Standards Anchor:** ISO 14040 (Principles and Framework) and ISO 14044 (Requirements and Guidelines) jointly govern all four phases of LCA. They are mandatory reference documents for any KTU question on the Assessment Process.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are a **doctor examining a patient**. Before prescribing medicine, you must:
- **Define the goal** of the check-up (Goal & Scope).
- **Measure vital signs** — blood pressure, sugar level, heart rate (Inventory).
- **Diagnose potential diseases** based on those readings (Impact Assessment).
- **Recommend lifestyle changes** and explain what the results mean (Interpretation).

The **Assessment Process in Green Manufacturing works identically**. Instead of a human patient, the "patient" is a **product** (say, a 1-liter PET water bottle). Instead of blood pressure, we measure:
- CO₂ emissions (kg CO₂-eq)
- Water consumption (liters)
- Energy used (MJ)

Instead of diagnosing diseases, we diagnose **environmental impacts** like global warming potential, eutrophication, and ozone depletion.

| Medical Check-up Step | Green Manufacturing Assessment Step |
|---|---|
| Patient history & symptoms | Goal and Scope Definition |
| Blood test, X-ray, MRI | Life Cycle Inventory (LCI) |
| Disease diagnosis | Life Cycle Impact Assessment (LCIA) |
| Doctor's advice & prescription | Interpretation & Improvement |

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

The following standard metrics are universally applied in the Assessment Process:

- **Global Warming Potential (GWP):** measured in **kg CO₂-equivalent (kg CO₂-eq)**, normalized to a 100-year time horizon.
- **Functional Unit (FU):** the quantified performance of a product system used as a reference unit.
- **Characterization Factor:** unitless multiplier (e.g., CH₄ has a **GWP₁₀₀ = 28** kg CO₂-eq per kg CH₄).
- **System Boundary:** the interface between the product system and the environment/other product systems.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition Check:** A *Functional Unit* answers the question — "What does the product DO?" For 1 liter of bottled water, the FU is *"deliver 1 liter of drinking water to the consumer."* Without an FU, comparing two products is meaningless — you would be comparing apples to oranges.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Four Phases of the Assessment Process (ISO 14040/14044)

### Phase 1: Goal and Scope Definition
- **Goal:** States the intended application, reasons for study, target audience, and whether results will be used in comparative assertions disclosed publicly.
- **Scope:** Defines the **product system**, **functional unit**, **system boundary**, **allocation procedures**, **impact categories**, and **methodology**.

### Phase 2: Life Cycle Inventory (LCI) Analysis
- **Data Collection:** Quantitative compilation of inputs (raw materials, energy) and outputs (emissions to air/water/soil, co-products, waste) for the product system.
- **Allocation:** Partitioning flows when a process yields multiple co-products (e.g., a petroleum refinery producing gasoline, diesel, and LPG). Methods:
  * **Mass-based allocation**
  * **Economic (value-based) allocation**
  * **Energy-based allocation**
- **Cut-off criteria:** Specifies the minimum mass, energy, or environmental significance for including a flow.

### Phase 3: Life Cycle Impact Assessment (LCIA)
This phase translates LCI data into environmental impact scores. It follows the **mandatory and optional elements** defined in ISO 14044:

**Mandatory Elements:**
- **Classification:** Assigning LCI data to relevant impact categories (e.g., CO₂ → Climate Change; SO₂ → Acidification).
- **Characterization:** Converting classified data into a common metric using characterization factors.

**Optional Elements:**
- **Normalization:** Expressing impact results relative to a reference value (e.g., per capita global emissions).
- **Grouping:** Sorting impact categories into hierarchical sets (e.g., ranking).
- **Weighting:** Aggregating impact categories using value-based weights (subjective; not allowed in comparative assertions disclosed to public).

### Phase 4: Interpretation
- **Identification of Significant Issues:** Finding the hot-spot processes driving impacts.
- **Evaluation:** Checking completeness, sensitivity, and consistency.
- **Conclusions & Recommendations:** Documenting limitations and improvement strategies.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Examiner Note:** A common mistake students make is mixing the optional LCIA elements (Normalization, Grouping, Weighting) with the mandatory ones. Only **Classification** and **Characterization** are mandatory under ISO 14044. Weighting is **prohibited** in public comparative claims.

## 2.2 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Industry Sector | Application of Assessment Process |
|---|---|
| Automotive | Comparing EVs vs. ICE vehicles (cradle-to-grave CO₂) |
| Packaging | Selecting biodegradable vs. plastic containers |
| Construction | LEED/BREEAM certification of green buildings |
| Electronics | E-waste quantification, RoHS compliance |
| Textile | Water footprint of cotton vs. synthetic fibers |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Expression | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Characterization | $I_{c} = \sum_{i} m_{i} \times CF_{i}$ | varies by category | $m_i$ = mass of substance $i$; $CF_i$ = characterization factor |
| 2 | Mass Allocation | $A_{j} = F_{tot} \times \dfrac{m_{j}}{m_{co\text{-}products}}$ | kg or MJ | Allocated flow to co-product $j$ |
| 3 | Economic Allocation | $A_{j} = F_{tot} \times \dfrac{V_{j}}{\sum V_{k}}$ | currency | $V_j$ = market value of product $j$ |
| 4 | GWP Total | $GWP = \sum_{i} m_{i} \times GWP_{100,i}$ | kg CO₂-eq | Aggregated climate change impact |
| 5 | Cumulative Energy Demand | $CED = \sum_{i} E_{i} \times f_{i}$ | MJ-eq | $f_i$ = energy carrier factor |
| 6 | Functional Unit Reference Flow | $RF = \dfrac{Total\_Output}{Functional\_Unit\_Size}$ | kg/FU | Used to scale inventory data |
| 7 | Eco-indicator 95/99 Score | $EI = \sum_{c} I_{c} \times w_{c}$ | Pt (points) | $w_c$ = weighting factor (subjective) |

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical Notational Rule:** In the formulas above, the vertical bar symbol `\vert` is intentionally NOT used to avoid breaking the markdown table. KTU board answers must show the full derivation using $\mid$ in LaTeX math mode.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Total Global Warming Potential (GWP) from Multi-Source Emissions

**Problem Statement (KTU-style):**
A manufacturing facility emits the following gases in one operational year:
- 1,200 kg of CO₂
- 25 kg of CH₄
- 0.8 kg of N₂O

Using the **IPCC AR5 100-year GWP characterization factors** (CO₂ = 1, CH₄ = 28, N₂O = 265), compute the total Global Warming Potential in kg CO₂-equivalent.

### Step 1: Identify the Governing Equation
The aggregated GWP is the mass-weighted sum of each substance's GWP contribution:

$$
GWP_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} m_{i} \times GWP_{100,i}
$$

where:
- $m_i$ = mass of greenhouse gas $i$ in **kg**
- $GWP_{100,i}$ = 100-year characterization factor of gas $i$
- $n$ = number of distinct greenhouse gases (here, 3)

### Step 2: Expand the Summation
Substituting $n = 3$ for CO₂, CH₄, and N₂O:

$$
GWP_{total} = m_{CO_2} \cdot GWP_{100,CO_2} + m_{CH_4} \cdot GWP_{100,CH_4} + m_{N_2O} \cdot GWP_{100,N_2O}
$$

### Step 3: Substitute the Numerical Values

$$
GWP_{total} = (1200 \times 1) + (25 \times 28) + (0.8 \times 265)
$$

### Step 4: Compute Each Term Individually

$$
\text{Term 1 (CO}_2\text{)} = 1200 \times 1 = 1200 \text{ kg CO}_2\text{-eq}
$$

$$
\text{Term 2 (CH}_4\text{)} = 25 \times 28 = 700 \text{ kg CO}_2\text{-eq}
$$

$$
\text{Term 3 (N}_2\text{O)} = 0.8 \times 265 = 212 \text{ kg CO}_2\text{-eq}
$$

### Step 5: Aggregate the Contributions

$$
GWP_{total} = 1200 + 700 + 212
$$

$$
GWP_{total} = 2112 \text{ kg CO}_2\text{-eq}
$$

### Step 6: Result and Interpretation
The facility's annual climate change burden is **2,112 kg CO₂-equivalent**. Although N₂O is emitted in the smallest mass (0.8 kg), its high characterization factor (265) makes it responsible for **~10%** of the total GWP. This justifies why industrial abatement strategies must prioritize substances by GWP, not by mass.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** The N₂O contribution (212 kg) exceeds 10% of the total despite being < 0.07% of the total mass. This is the essence of characterization — converting heterogeneous pollutants into a single comparable unit.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Mass-Based Allocation in a Multi-Output Process

**Problem Statement:**
A petroleum refinery co-produces 3 products from a single distillation unit:
- Gasoline: 500 kg, market value ₹80/kg
- Diesel: 350 kg, market value ₹90/kg
- LPG: 150 kg, market value ₹60/kg

The total upstream crude oil extraction burden is **10,000 MJ of energy** and **2,000 kg of CO₂ emissions**. Compute the mass-allocated share of energy and emissions to each co-product.

### Step 1: Total Mass of Co-products
$$
M_{tot} = 500 + 350 + 150 = 1000 \text{ kg}
$$

### Step 2: Mass Fraction of Each Co-product

$$
f_{gas} = \frac{500}{1000} = 0.50
$$

$$
f_{die} = \frac{350}{1000} = 0.35
$$

$$
f_{LPG} = \frac{150}{1000} = 0.15
$$

### Step 3: Apply Mass-Based Allocation Formula

$$
A_{j} = F_{tot} \times \frac{m_{j}}{M_{tot}}
$$

### Step 4: Allocate Energy (10,000 MJ)

$$
E_{gas} = 10000 \times 0.50 = 5000 \text{ MJ}
$$

$$
E_{die} = 10000 \times 0.35 = 3500 \text{ MJ}
$$

$$
E_{LPG} = 10000 \times 0.15 = 1500 \text{ MJ}
$$

### Step 5: Allocate CO₂ Emissions (2,000 kg)

$$
C_{gas} = 2000 \times 0.50 = 1000 \text{ kg}
$$

$$
C_{die} = 2000 \times 0.35 = 700 \text{ kg}
$$

$$
C_{LPG} = 2000 \times 0.15 = 300 \text{ kg}
$$

### Step 6: Verification
The sum of allocated flows must equal the total input:

$$
5000 + 3500 + 1500 = 10000 \text{ MJ} \;\checkmark
$$

$$
1000 + 700 + 300 = 2000 \text{ kg} \;\checkmark
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Mass-Based Allocation?** It is the **default** method in ISO 14044 §4.3.4.1, applied unless physical causality or other relationships justify an alternative. Economic allocation is preferred when co-products have significantly different market values (e.g., pharma-grade vs. industrial-grade chemicals).

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Allocation Methods

| Criterion | Mass Allocation | Economic Allocation | Energy Allocation |
|---|---|---|---|
| Data Requirement | Low | High (price data) | Medium (calorific values) |
| Reflects Physical Causality | Moderate | Low | High |
| Reflects Economic Value | Low | High | Low |
| Sensitivity to Market Volatility | None | High | None |
| Preferred By ISO 14044 | Default fallback | When physical link unclear | When energy is dominant |
| Typical Use | Mining, bulk materials | Petrochemicals, metals | Fuels, electricity generation |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Assessment Process

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the four-phase Assessment Process as defined by ISO 14040/14044, with explicit data flow between modules.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Phase 0: Trigger and Problem Definition]
    A1[Phase 1: Goal and Scope Definition]
    A2[Phase 2: Life Cycle Inventory Analysis]
    A3[Phase 3: Life Cycle Impact Assessment]
    A4[Phase 4: Interpretation]

    B1[Functional Unit Selection]
    B2[System Boundary Setting]
    B3[Data Collection Inputs and Outputs]
    B4[Allocation Procedure]
    B5[Classification into Impact Categories]
    B6[Characterization with CF]
    B7[Normalization Optional]
    B8[Weighting Optional Subjective]
    B9[Significant Issue Identification]
    B10[Sensitivity and Uncertainty Check]
    B11[Conclusions and Recommendations]

    C1[Product System Model]
    C2[Inventory Table kg MJ m3]
    C3[Impact Category Indicators]
    C4[LCIA Profile and Hot Spots]
    C5[LCA Report Public Disclosure]

    A0 --> A1
    A1 --> B1
    A1 --> B2
    A1 --> A2
    A2 --> B3
    A2 --> B4
    A2 --> C2
    A2 --> A3
    A3 --> B5
    A3 --> B6
    A3 --> B7
    A3 --> B8
    A3 --> C3
    A3 --> C4
    A3 --> A4
    A4 --> B9
    A4 --> B10
    A4 --> B11
    A4 --> C5

    style A1 fill:#ffe599,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style A2 fill:#b6d7a8,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style A3 fill:#a4c2f4,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style A4 fill:#ea9999,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style C5 fill:#d5a6bd,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

**Reading the Diagram:**
- The yellow node represents **Scope** (where the study is bounded).
- The green node is the **Inventory** (raw data quantification).
- The blue node is **Impact Assessment** (translation into environmental scores).
- The red node is **Interpretation** (actionable conclusions).
- The pink node at the bottom-right represents the **final output**: a public LCA report.

## 4.2 Schematic: Cradle-to-Grave System Boundary for a Product

The following Mermaid diagram depicts a typical product life cycle with a **cradle-to-grave** system boundary, used during Phase 1 (Scope Definition).

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    R1[Raw Material Extraction]
    R2[Manufacturing and Assembly]
    R3[Distribution and Logistics]
    R4[Use Phase by Consumer]
    R5[End of Life Disposal or Recycling]
    R6[Final Emissions to Air Water Soil]

    R1 --> R2
    R2 --> R3
    R3 --> R4
    R4 --> R5
    R5 --> R6

    S1[System Boundary] -. encompasses .-> R1
    S1 -. encompasses .-> R2
    S1 -. encompasses .-> R3
    S1 -. encompasses .-> R4
    S1 -. encompasses .-> R5

    style S1 fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#cc0000,stroke-width:3px,stroke-dasharray:5 5
    style R6 fill:#f4cccc,stroke:#660000,stroke-width:2px
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Visual Convention:** The **dashed red boundary** marks the *system boundary*. Anything inside is included in the assessment; outside flows are excluded. Different system boundaries yield vastly different GWP results — a critical KTU board discussion point.

## 4.3 Impact Pathway Diagram (LCIA Mechanism)

This Mermaid block explains how an LCI flow translates to an impact category through the **environmental mechanism** of LCIA.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    L1[LCI Flow kg of CO2]
    L2[LCI Flow kg of SO2]
    L3[LCI Flow kg of PO4]
    L4[LCI Flow kg of CFC11]

    M1[Climate Change via IR Absorption]
    M2[Acidification via H plus Formation]
    M3[Eutrophication via Nutrient Loading]
    M4[Ozone Depletion via ODS Reaction]

    E1[Damage to Human Health]
    E2[Damage to Ecosystems]
    E3[Damage to Resource Availability]

    L1 --> M1
    L2 --> M2
    L3 --> M3
    L4 --> M4

    M1 --> E1
    M2 --> E2
    M3 --> E2
    M4 --> E1
    M1 --> E3
    M2 --> E3

    style M1 fill:#fce5cd
    style M2 fill:#fce5cd
    style M3 fill:#fce5cd
    style M4 fill:#fce5cd
    style E1 fill:#d9ead3
    style E2 fill:#d9ead3
    style E3 fill:#d9ead3
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** The relationship between LCI → Environmental Mechanism → Impact Category → Damage is the *midpoint-to-endpoint* LCIA framework. CML 2001 uses midpoint; ReCiPe uses endpoint. Examiners love asking which is which.
