## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper 2]**
Define the term **"Servitization"** in the context of the service sector. How does it contribute to green manufacturing?

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> **Servitization** is the strategic transformation wherein manufacturing firms augment or replace their traditional product-based revenue model with **integrated product-service systems (PSS)** that deliver functional outcomes (e.g., "comfort hours" instead of "air conditioners").
>
> **Contribution to Green Manufacturing:**
> 1. **Dematerialization** — fewer physical units serve more users, reducing embodied emissions.
> 2. **Manufacturer incentive for durability** — the firm now retains ownership, so it designs for longevity and easy maintenance.
> 3. **Closed-loop material flows** — return, refurbish, remanufacture become economically attractive.
> 4. **Decoupling of profit from throughput** — revenue from service continuity, not unit volume, breaking the Jevons Paradox link.
>
> *(Mark allocation: 1 mark definition + 2 marks for green-manufacturing linkage)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Sample Paper]**
List any **three green service sector initiatives** commonly adopted in the Indian banking sector.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The **Indian banking sector** has adopted the following green initiatives:
1. **Green Banking** — financing only ESG-compliant projects and renewable energy ventures.
2. **Paperless Banking** — e-statements, digital KYC, NEFT/UPI adoption drastically reducing paper consumption.
3. **Green Branches** — solar-powered ATMs, energy-efficient buildings, water recycling.
4. **Carbon-Neutral Operations** — carbon accounting of loan portfolios under RBI guidelines (2023).

*(Any three: 1 mark each)*

---

### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

---

#### QUESTION A (14 Marks) — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]* *(Attempt any one from A or B)*

**(a)** Explain the **five pillars of green service sector design** with suitable industrial examples. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A logistics company delivers **50,000 parcels/year** using diesel vans. Each parcel delivery emits **0.5 kg CO₂e**. The company switches to **electric vehicles** with renewable-charged electricity. Emissions per delivery drop to **0.15 kg CO₂e**, but the reverse-logistics (return pickups) adds **3,000 kg CO₂e/year**. Calculate the **annual CO₂e reduction** and comment on whether the transition is environmentally justified. **(7 Marks)**

---

### MODEL SOLUTION — Question A

#### Part (a) — Five Pillars of Green Service Sector Design (7 Marks)

The five pillars constitute the operational framework for designing environmentally responsible services:

1. **Dematerialization (2 Marks)**
   - Replacing physical artefacts with digital or intangible equivalents.
   - *Example:* Cloud storage replacing physical filing systems in BPO services; telemedicine replacing patient travel.
   - Reduces embodied emissions per service unit.

2. **Functional Economy / Servitization (2 Marks)**
   - Selling *use* and *outcome* instead of *ownership*.
   - *Example:* Rolls-Royce "Power-by-the-Hour" sells thrust-hours; Xerox pay-per-page.
   - Aligns manufacturer incentive with product longevity.

3. **Eco-Efficiency (1 Mark)**
   - Maximizing service value per unit of environmental impact.
   - *Example:* Energy-Star-rated hotels achieving ₹5000 revenue per kWh consumed.

4. **Life-Cycle Extension (1 Mark)**
   - Services (maintenance, repair, upgrade) that prolong product usefulness.
   - *Example:* Apple's iPhone upgrade / trade-in programme.

5. **Closing the Loop / Circularity (1 Mark)**
   - Reverse logistics, take-back, refurbishment.
   - *Example:* Amazon's reverse-logistics returns hub for e-waste refurbishment.

---

#### Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 Marks)

**Given:**
- Annual parcels delivered $N = 50{,}000$
- Old emissions per delivery $e_{\text{old}} = 0.5$ kg CO₂e
- New emissions per delivery $e_{\text{new}} = 0.15$ kg CO₂e
- Reverse-logistics emissions $I_{\text{RL}} = 3{,}000$ kg CO₂e/year

**Step 1 — Annual emissions under diesel vans:**

$$I_{\text{diesel}} = 50{,}000 \times 0.5 = 25{,}000 \text{ kg CO}_2\text{e/year}$$

**Step 2 — Annual emissions under electric vans (including reverse logistics):**

$$I_{\text{electric}} = 50{,}000 \times 0.15 + 3{,}000 = 7{,}500 + 3{,}000 = 10{,}500 \text{ kg CO}_2\text{e/year}$$

**Step 3 — Net annual reduction:**

$$\Delta I = I_{\text{diesel}} - I_{\text{electric}} = 25{,}000 - 10{,}500 = 14{,}500 \text{ kg CO}_2\text{e/year}$$

**Step 4 — Percentage reduction:**

$$\% \text{ Reduction} = \frac{14{,}500}{25{,}000} \times 100 = 58\%$$

**Step 5 — Justification comment:**
The transition reduces emissions by **58%** — well above any logistics overhead. The **3,000 kg CO₂e from reverse logistics is more than offset** by the operational savings. The transition is **environmentally justified** and aligns with India's **Net-Zero 2070** commitment and FAME-II EV policy.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Alert:** Common mistakes students make in this type of question:
> 1. **Forgetting to add reverse-logistics emissions** to the new scenario (loses 2 marks).
> 2. **Computing "per parcel" reduction only** and not multiplying by $N$ (loses 1 mark).
> 3. **Not stating the % reduction** explicitly (loses 1 mark).
> 4. **Not commenting on policy alignment** — examiners expect a one-line link to NEP 2020 sustainability goals or Net-Zero commitments (loses 1 mark).

---

#### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — *[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model Paper]*

**(a)** Discuss the role of **Green IT** in transforming the service sector. List at least **four** specific green-IT practices. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A hospital uses **2,000 kWh of electricity per day** at ₹8/kWh. The hospital implements **energy-efficient measures** that reduce consumption by **20%**. It also installs a **50 kW rooftop solar PV** system generating **4.5 kWh/kW/day**. Calculate the **annual cost savings (₹)** and **annual CO₂e reduction (kg)** assuming grid emission factor **0.82 kg CO₂e/kWh**. **(7 Marks)**

---

### MODEL SOLUTION — Question B

#### Part (a) — Green IT in Service Sector (7 Marks)

**Definition (2 Marks):**
**Green IT (Green Information Technology)** refers to the practice of designing, manufacturing, using, and disposing of computing resources (servers, data centres, networks, end-user devices) in an environmentally sustainable manner. It encompasses both the **green use of IT** (efficiency of operations) and the **green design of IT** (energy-efficient hardware).

**Four Specific Green-IT Practices (5 Marks — 1.25 each):**

1. **Virtualization & Cloud Computing** — Consolidating servers using virtualization reduces physical hardware count, lowering both embodied energy and cooling loads.

2. **Energy-Efficient Data Centres** — Use of free cooling, hot/cold aisle containment, AI-based workload scheduling, PUE (Power Usage Effectiveness) optimization toward **PUE < 1.5**.

3. **E-Waste Management & Circular Procurement** — Refurbishment of laptops, certified e-waste recycling, take-back programmes aligned with India's **E-Waste Management Rules 2022**.

4. **Green Software Engineering** — Writing energy-aware code (e.g., dark-mode UIs reduce OLED energy, efficient algorithms reduce CPU cycles, sleep-mode APIs).

5. *(Bonus)* **Renewable-Powered Operations** — Solar/wind-powered data centres (e.g., Google's carbon-neutral data centres).

---

#### Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 Marks)

**Given:**
- Daily electricity consumption $E_0 = 2{,}000$ kWh/day
- Tariff $r = ₹8$/kWh
- Efficiency reduction = 20%
- Solar PV capacity = 50 kW
- Solar yield = 4.5 kWh/kW/day
- Grid emission factor = 0.82 kg CO₂e/kWh
- Operating days/year = 365 (assumed)

**Step 1 — Energy saved by efficiency measures:**

$$E_{\text{saved}} = 2{,}000 \times 0.20 = 400 \text{ kWh/day}$$

**Step 2 — Daily electricity bill saved:**

$$\text{Bill saved per day} = 400 \times 8 = ₹3{,}200$$

**Step 3 — Annual bill savings from efficiency:**

$$\text{Annual saving}_\text{eff} = 3{,}200 \times 365 = ₹1{,}168{,}000$$

**Step 4 — Daily solar generation:**

$$E_{\text{solar}} = 50 \times 4.5 = 225 \text{ kWh/day}$$

**Step 5 — Annual solar generation:**

$$\text{Annual solar} = 225 \times 365 = 82{,}125 \text{ kWh/year}$$

**Step 6 — Annual bill savings from solar:**

$$\text{Annual saving}_\text{solar} = 82{,}125 \times 8 = ₹6{,}57{,}000$$

**Step 7 — Total annual cost savings:**

$$S_{\text{total}} = ₹1{,}168{,}000 + ₹6{,}57{,}000 = ₹1{,}825{,}000$$

**Step 8 — Annual CO₂e reduction (combined):**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{CO}_2\text{e saved} &= (400 + 225) \times 365 \times 0.82 \\
&= 625 \times 365 \times 0.82 \\
&= 228{,}125 \times 0.82 \\
&= 1{,}87{,}062.5 \text{ kg CO}_2\text{e/year}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:**
The hospital achieves **₹18.25 lakh/year** in cost savings and **~187 tonnes of CO₂e avoided annually** — a textbook green-service-sector transformation.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Alert — Question B Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Mixing up daily and annual figures** — students often multiply a daily number by 12 instead of 365, losing 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Ignoring the solar capacity-factor conversion** — solar gives 4.5 kWh **per kW installed per day**; some students forget the "per kW" and write 4.5 kWh directly.
> 3. **Not converting to tonnes** for CO₂e — examiners prefer "tonnes/year" notation; missing unit conversion loses 0.5 mark.
> 4. **Skipping the policy linkage** — mention **PUE < 1.5** and India's **E-Waste Rules 2022** for full marks.

---

## 📌 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

- ✅ **Service Sector = Tertiary sector** delivering intangible value; ~50% of world GDP, ~40% energy use.
- ✅ **Five Pillars**: Dematerialization, Functional Economy, Eco-Efficiency, Life-Cycle Extension, Closing the Loop.
- ✅ **Servitization** = shift from product-sale to product-service-system (PSS); increases durability, reduces material throughput.
- ✅ **Carbon Productivity** = Value / CO₂e; **must rise** for decoupling.
- ✅ **Energy Intensity** = Energy / GDP; **must fall** for decoupling.
- ✅ **MIPS** = Material per service unit; lower = greener.
- ✅ **Green IT** practices: Virtualization, PUE-optimized data centres, e-waste management, green software.
- ✅ **Green Banking** = ESG financing, paperless operations, RBI carbon-disclosure mandates.
- ✅ **Reverse Logistics** adds emissions — must be checked against manufacturing-side savings using the **breakeven $k^*$ formula**.
- ✅ **Breakeven reuse cycles** $k^* = \frac{I_{\text{logistics}}}{I_{\text{logistics}} - I_{\text{per-unit}}}$ defines service viability.
- ✅ **Case studies to memorize for KTU**: Rolls-Royce Power-by-the-Hour, Xerox pay-per-page, Amazon reverse logistics, Indian banks' green initiatives, hospital energy retrofits.
- ✅ **Statistical fact**: Service sector uses **~40% of world energy** — so it is the *largest* lever for decarbonization.
- ✅ **Frameworks to cite**: UNEP Eco-Efficiency, Circular Economy (Ellen MacArthur), GRI Standards, TCFD, India's Net-Zero 2070.
- ✅ **Always mention NEP 2020 alignment** when writing long answers — KTU explicitly values NEP 2020 awareness.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET753 - GREEN MANUFACTURING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Logistics

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Logistics (in Green Manufacturing Context):** Logistics refers to the comprehensive planning, implementation, and control of the efficient flow and storage of goods, services, and related information from the point of origin to the point of consumption, designed in such a way as to **minimize negative environmental impact**, reduce energy consumption, and optimize resource utilization across the entire supply chain.

**Sustainable / Green Logistics** extends this definition by integrating the triple-bottom-line principle (People, Planet, Profit) into every logistical decision—from procurement of raw materials, through production, warehousing, transportation, distribution, to end-of-life recovery of products.

The two principal branches within Module 4 are:

1. **Forward Logistics** — The traditional flow of materials from supplier → manufacturer → distributor → customer.
2. **Reverse Logistics** — The backward flow of used products, packaging, or materials from the end user back through the supply chain for recycling, remanufacturing, refurbishment, or disposal.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Green Bloodstream of Industry"

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: Logistics as the Circulatory System of a Factory**
> Think of a manufacturing company as a human body. The heart pumps blood (raw materials) through arteries (forward logistics) to organs (production lines, warehouses, retailers). Just as a healthy body needs clean, efficient circulation, a sustainable industry needs logistics that **carries resources with minimal waste, lowest emissions, and full circular recovery**. When blood returns to the heart carrying used oxygen (waste), the body recycles it. Similarly, **reverse logistics** brings back used products so the industry can recapture value—a closed-loop "industrial bloodstream."

## 1.3 Core Logistics Components (Syllabus Pillars)

- **Transportation:** Movement of goods via road, rail, sea, air, or pipelines.
- **Warehousing:** Storage and inventory management at hubs.
- **Inventory Management:** Just-in-Time (JIT), Economic Order Quantity (EOQ).
- **Packaging:** Material choice, weight reduction, recyclability.
- **Order Processing & Information Flow:** Digital systems (ERP, WMS, TMS).
- **Reverse Flows:** Returns, recalls, refurbishment, recycling.

## 1.4 Standard Metrics in Green Logistics (KTU High-Yield)

The following metrics are universally tested in KTU examinations:

- **Carbon Footprint (kg CO₂e / ton-km)**
- **Empty Running Percentage (%)**
- **Load Factor (%)**
- **Fuel Efficiency (km / litre or MJ / ton-km)**
- **Reverse Recovery Rate (%)**
- **Greenhouse Gas (GHG) Emissions per unit distance**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Framework of Green Logistics

The discipline rests on five foundational pillars, each a high-frequency KTU topic:

### Pillar 1 — Modal Shift Strategy
Shifting freight from high-emission modes (road, air) to lower-emission modes (rail, inland waterway, sea).

### Pillar 2 — Vehicle Routing & Load Optimization
Mathematically solving the **Vehicle Routing Problem (VRP)** to minimize total distance, fuel, and emissions while maximizing load factor.

### Pillar 3 — Network Design Optimization
Locating warehouses and distribution centres (DCs) to balance **service level** against **transport emissions**.

### Pillar 4 — Reverse Logistics & Closed-Loop Systems
Capturing end-of-life products for **remanufacturing, refurbishing, cannibalization, or material recycling**.

### Pillar 5 — Green Packaging & Eco-Warehousing
Using biodegradable, returnable, or reusable packaging and energy-efficient warehouse design.

## 2.2 The 7R Framework of Green Reverse Logistics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The 7R Principle (Frequently asked in KTU 2-mark questions):**
> 1. **Return** – Bring products back from end user.
> 2. **Recycle** – Reprocess materials for new use.
> 3. **Reuse** – Use a product again for the same function.
> 4. **Repair** – Restore a defective product to working condition.
> 5. **Refurbish** – Upgrade cosmetic/functional aspects.
> 6. **Remanufacture** – Disassemble and rebuild to "as-new" specification.
> 7. **Redistribute** – Channel recovered products to secondary markets.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Variables / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | CO₂ Emission per ton-km | $E_{CO_2} = \dfrac{M_f \times EF_f \times 1000}{Q \times d}$ | $M_f$ = fuel mass [kg], $EF_f$ = emission factor [kg CO₂/kg fuel], $Q$ = cargo [tonnes], $d$ = distance [km] |
| 2 | Load Factor | $LF = \dfrac{W_{actual}}{W_{capacity}} \times 100\%$ | $W_{actual}$ = loaded weight, $W_{capacity}$ = max capacity |
| 3 | Empty Running % | $ER = \dfrac{D_{empty}}{D_{total}} \times 100\%$ | $D_{empty}$ = km run without load, $D_{total}$ = total km |
| 4 | Vehicle Fuel Consumption | $FC = a + b \cdot v^2$ | $a$ = idling loss [L/h], $b$ = drag coefficient, $v$ = speed [km/h] |
| 5 | Basic EOQ | $EOQ = \sqrt{\dfrac{2DS}{H}}$ | $D$ = annual demand [units], $S$ = ordering cost [₹], $H$ = holding cost [₹/unit/yr] |
| 6 | Transport Cost per Unit | $C_{unit} = \dfrac{C_{fixed} + C_{variable} \cdot d}{Q}$ | $C_{fixed}$ = fixed cost, $C_{variable}$ = per-km cost, $Q$ = load quantity |
| 7 | Warehouse Carbon Index | $WCI = \dfrac{E_{warehouse} + E_{lighting} + E_{HVAC}}{A_{floor}}$ | Energy terms in [kWh], $A_{floor}$ in [m²] |
| 8 | Reverse Recovery Rate | $RRR = \dfrac{N_{recovered}}{N_{sold}} \times 100\%$ | $N_{recovered}$ = units returned, $N_{sold}$ = units originally sold |
| 9 | Green Packaging Ratio | $GPR = \dfrac{M_{recyclable}}{M_{total}} \times 100\%$ | $M_{recyclable}$ = recyclable material mass, $M_{total}$ = total packaging mass |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

In modern Indian industries (Tata Motors, Mahindra, Infosys, Flipkart, ITC), green logistics directly contributes to:
- **ESG Compliance** (mandated by SEBI for top 1000 listed companies).
- **Reduction of Scope 3 Emissions** (often 70–90% of a company's carbon footprint).
- **Cost Savings** through fuel efficiency, route optimization, and reverse recovery.
- **Regulatory Compliance** with India's Plastic Waste Management Rules, E-Waste Rules, and Extended Producer Responsibility (EPR) policy.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Fuel-Based CO₂ Emission per ton-km

This is a **classic KTU 14-mark derivation**.

**Given:**
- A truck consumes $M_f$ kg of diesel over a distance $d$ km.
- Diesel emission factor $EF_f = 3.169$ **kg CO₂ per kg diesel** (standard IPCC value).
- Cargo carried $Q$ tonnes.

**Step 1 — Total emissions from the trip**
Total CO₂ emitted by the vehicle during the trip is directly proportional to the fuel burned.

$$
E_{trip} = M_f \times EF_f
$$

**Step 2 — Normalize by cargo weight**
To make the metric comparable across trips carrying different loads, divide total emissions by cargo mass carried (in tonnes).

$$
E_{per\,ton} = \dfrac{M_f \times EF_f}{Q}
$$

**Step 3 — Normalize by distance**
Finally, to obtain emissions per unit transport work (ton-km), divide by distance $d$.

$$
E_{CO_2} = \dfrac{M_f \times EF_f}{Q \cdot d}
$$

**Unit verification:**
Numerator: kg $\times$ (kg CO₂ / kg) = kg CO₂
Denominator: tonne × km = tonne-km
Result: **kg CO₂ per tonne-km** ✓

**Step 4 — Conversion to gram per ton-km (common KTU form)**
Multiply by 1000 to express in grams, since typical values are very small (e.g., 60 g CO₂/ton-km for rail vs. 110 g for road).

$$
E_{CO_2}^{(g)} = \dfrac{M_f \times EF_f \times 1000}{Q \cdot d}
$$

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Load Factor Improvement & Emission Reduction

Let initial load factor be $LF_1$ and improved load factor be $LF_2 = LF_1 + \Delta LF$.

**Step 1 — Define baseline emission per trip**
With load factor $LF_1$, the cargo carried is $Q_1 = LF_1 \cdot C$, where $C$ is vehicle capacity.

$$
E_1 = \dfrac{M_f \times EF_f}{LF_1 \cdot C \cdot d}
$$

**Step 2 — Define improved emission per trip**
With load factor $LF_2$, the cargo carried increases to $Q_2 = LF_2 \cdot C$.

$$
E_2 = \dfrac{M_f \times EF_f}{LF_2 \cdot C \cdot d}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute percentage reduction in emission intensity**

$$
\%\,\text{Reduction} = \dfrac{E_1 - E_2}{E_1} \times 100\%
$$

$$
\%\,\text{Reduction} = \left(1 - \dfrac{LF_1}{LF_2}\right) \times 100\%
$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation**
If $LF_1 = 50\%$ and $LF_2 = 75\%$, then:

$$
\%\,\text{Reduction} = \left(1 - \dfrac{50}{75}\right) \times 100\% = 33.33\%
$$

Thus, a **50% increase in load factor** yields a **33.3% reduction in emission intensity per ton-km** — a powerful insight often tested in KTU problems.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Mode-wise Emission Comparison

| Mode | Typical EF (g CO₂ / ton-km) | Energy Use (MJ / ton-km) | Suitability for Green Logistics |
|---|---|---|---|
| Road (truck) | 80 – 120 | 2.5 – 3.5 | Short-haul, last-mile |
| Rail (electric) | 20 – 40 | 0.6 – 1.0 | Bulk, long-haul |
| Inland waterway | 30 – 50 | 0.8 – 1.2 | Heavy bulk, low-cost |
| Sea (container ship) | 8 – 20 | 0.2 – 0.6 | International, bulk |
| Air (cargo plane) | 500 – 800 | 15 – 25 | High-value, urgent only |

## 3.4 Numerical Worked Example (KTU Typical Style)

**Problem:** A logistics company operates a 10-tonne capacity truck over 400 km, consuming 80 L of diesel (density 0.85 kg/L). The truck returns **empty**. Calculate:
(i) One-way emission intensity.
(ii) Round-trip emission intensity.

**Step (i) — One-way**
Fuel mass: $M_f = 80 \times 0.85 = 68$ kg.
Load $Q = 10$ tonnes, distance $d = 400$ km, $EF = 3.169$ kg CO₂/kg diesel.

$$
E_{CO_2} = \dfrac{68 \times 3.169 \times 1000}{10 \times 400} = \dfrac{215,492}{4000} = 53.87 \;\text{g CO}_2/\text{ton-km}
$$

**Step (ii) — Round-trip (with empty return)**
Total cargo work = $10 \times 400 = 4000$ ton-km (return trip contributes 0 cargo work but burns ~half fuel).

Total fuel = $68 \times 2 = 136$ kg. Total emissions $= 136 \times 3.169 = 431.0$ kg CO₂.

$$
E_{round} = \dfrac{431.0 \times 1000}{10 \times 400} = 107.75 \;\text{g CO}_2/\text{ton-km}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students often forget that **empty-return trips double the effective emission intensity** even though cargo work is the same. Always compute total ton-km as (cargo × loaded distance only), not total distance.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Green Logistics Block Architecture — Closed-Loop Supply Chain

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[Supplier Raw Material] --> nodeB[Manufacturer Plant]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Distribution Center]
    nodeC --> nodeD[Retailer]
    nodeD --> nodeE[End Consumer]
    nodeE --> nodeF[Collection Point]
    nodeF --> nodeG[Sorting Inspection Hub]
    nodeG --> nodeH[Recycling Stream]
    nodeG --> nodeI[Remanufacturing Stream]
    nodeG --> nodeJ[Disposal Stream]
    nodeH --> nodeB
    nodeI --> nodeB
```

## 4.2 Logistics Decision Framework — Hierarchy of Green Actions

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeStart[Logistics Decision Trigger] --> nodeP1{Is Transport Necessary}
    nodeP1 -- No --> nodeP1A[Eliminate Move Avoid Emissions]
    nodeP1 -- Yes --> nodeP2{Can Mode Shift to Rail or Sea}
    nodeP2 -- Yes --> nodeP2A[Shift to Low Emission Mode]
    nodeP2 -- No --> nodeP3{Can Load Factor Improve}
    nodeP3 -- Yes --> nodeP3A[Consolidate Shipments]
    nodeP3 -- No --> nodeP4{Can Route Optimize}
    nodeP4 -- Yes --> nodeP4A[Apply VRP Algorithm]
    nodeP4 -- No --> nodeP5[Use Clean Fuel Vehicle]
```

## 4.3 7R Reverse-Logistics Hierarchy Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeR1[Returned Product Entry] --> nodeR2[Inspection Triage]
    nodeR2 --> nodeR3[Reuse As Is]
    nodeR2 --> nodeR4[Repair Restore]
    nodeR2 --> nodeR5[Refurbish Upgrade]
    nodeR2 --> nodeR6[Remanufacture As New]
    nodeR2 --> nodeR7[Recycle Materials]
    nodeR2 --> nodeR8[Safe Disposal]
```

## 4.4 Warehouse Sustainability Layered Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeW0[Warehouse Eco Design] --> nodeW1[Roof Solar PV Array]
    nodeW0 --> nodeW2[LED Lighting with Daylight Sensors]
    nodeW0 --> nodeW3[HVAC Heat Recovery System]
    nodeW0 --> nodeW4[Rainwater Harvesting Unit]
    nodeW0 --> nodeW5[EV Forklifts and AGVs]
    nodeW0 --> nodeW6[WMS Software for Slotting]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model]`

**Q: Define Green Logistics and list any four of its operational objectives.**

**Model Answer:**
Green Logistics is the systematic practice of planning, executing, and controlling the flow and storage of goods, services, and information from origin to consumption, with the **explicit objective of minimizing environmental degradation**, reducing carbon emissions, and conserving natural resources while meeting customer service levels.

**Four operational objectives:**
1. Reduction of greenhouse gas emissions across all transport modes.
2. Optimization of load factor to minimize empty running.
3. Promotion of modal shift from road/air to rail/sea/inland water.
4. Adoption of reverse logistics for closed-loop material recovery.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Jun 2024 Model]`

**Q: Differentiate between Forward Logistics and Reverse Logistics with one example each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Forward Logistics | Reverse Logistics |
|---|---|---|
| Direction of flow | Supplier → Customer | Customer → Supplier / Recycler |
| Trigger | Forecasted demand | Returns, recalls, end-of-life |
| Speed priority | High (customer expectation) | Moderate (recovery window) |
| Cost recovery | Revenue generating | Often cost-incurring unless recovered |
| Example | Shipping a finished car from plant to showroom | Collecting an end-of-life car battery for cobalt recovery |

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]`

**Q (a) [7 Marks]:** With the aid of a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of a **closed-loop green logistics network**, identifying all forward and reverse flows.

**Model Answer:**

A closed-loop green logistics network integrates the traditional forward chain with a structured reverse channel to recover value from end-of-life products. The architecture comprises six interconnected blocks:

**Block 1 — Suppliers:** Source raw materials (preferably recycled-content).
**Block 2 — Manufacturer:** Transforms inputs into finished goods; powered by renewable energy where feasible.
**Block 3 — Distribution Centre (DC):** Warehouses using eco-design principles, consolidating shipments.
**Block 4 — Retailer / Channel Partner:** Final-mile dispatch using EVs or cycle-logistics in dense urban zones.
**Block 5 — End Consumer:** Uses the product; returns it at end-of-life.
**Block 6 — Collection / Recovery Hub:** Receives returns, sorts them, and routes to three sub-streams:
- **Recycling Stream** — material extraction.
- **Remanufacturing Stream** — disassembly, repair, reassembly.
- **Disposal Stream** — only for non-recoverable residual waste.

```
Supplier → Manufacturer → DC → Retailer → Consumer
                                              ↓
       Remanufacturing ← Recovery Hub ← Collection
            ↓
       Manufacturer (closed loop)
```

The closed loop **re-introduces recovered value** into manufacturing, completing the cycle.

---

**Q (b) [7 Marks]:** A truck of 12-tonne capacity travels 500 km carrying 8 tonnes of cargo and consumes 90 L diesel (density 0.84 kg/L). Diesel emission factor = 3.17 kg CO₂/kg. Compute:
(i) Load factor.
(ii) Specific emission intensity in g CO₂/ton-km.
(iii) The percentage reduction in intensity if load factor is raised to 100%.

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Load factor**

$$
LF = \dfrac{8}{12} \times 100\% = 66.67\%
$$

**(ii) Emission intensity**
Fuel mass $M_f = 90 \times 0.84 = 75.6$ kg.

$$
E_{CO_2} = \dfrac{75.6 \times 3.17 \times 1000}{8 \times 500} = \dfrac{239,652}{4000}
$$

$$
E_{CO_2} = 59.91 \;\text{g CO}_2/\text{ton-km}
$$

**(iii) Reduction if LF → 100%**

$$
\%\,\text{Reduction} = \left(1 - \dfrac{66.67}{100}\right) \times 100\% = 33.33\%
$$

**Conclusion:** Increasing the load factor from 66.67% to 100% cuts emission intensity by one-third — a critical insight for green logistics planning.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Jun 2023 Style]`

**Q (a) [7 Marks]:** Explain the **7R framework** of green reverse logistics with a real-world industrial example.

**Model Answer:**

The 7R framework (Rogers & Tibben-Lembke) is the **canonical classification** for reverse-logistics value recovery actions. Each R represents a progressively deeper intervention:

1. **Return:** Customer-initiated return of a product (e.g., Amazon customer returning a defective mobile phone).
2. **Recycle:** Shredding of the device to recover gold, copper, rare-earth elements.
3. **Reuse:** The same phone is resold after minor cleaning as a refurbished unit.
4. **Repair:** Replace a faulty battery; phone is restored to working condition.
5. **Refurbish:** Replace screen and case; phone is upgraded cosmetically.
6. **Remanufacture:** Full disassembly; PCB testing; new shell; reassembled as "as-new."
7. **Redistribute:** The remanufactured phones are sold in secondary markets or emerging economies at lower price points.

**Industrial Example:** Caterpillar's Reman program — diesel engines returned from mining sites are remanufactured and re-sold with the same warranty as new units, recovering up to **85% of the original material value**.

---

**Q (b) [7 Marks]:** Compare the **emission intensities of road, rail, and sea transport** for the same freight task of 1000 tonnes over 500 km, and recommend the most green-friendly mode.

**Model Answer:**

Assumed emission factors (typical averages):
- Road (diesel truck): 100 g CO₂ / ton-km
- Rail (electric, India grid): 30 g CO₂ / ton-km
- Sea (coastal vessel): 15 g CO₂ / ton-km

**Step 1 — Total freight work**

$$
W = 1000 \times 500 = 500{,}000 \;\text{ton-km}
$$

**Step 2 — Total emissions per mode**

$$
E_{road} = 100 \times 500{,}000 = 5.0 \times 10^{7} \;\text{g} = 50{,}000 \;\text{kg CO}_2
$$

$$
E_{rail} = 30 \times 500{,}000 = 1.5 \times 10^{7} \;\text{g} = 15{,}000 \;\text{kg CO}_2
$$

$$
E_{sea} = 15 \times 500{,}000 = 7.5 \times 10^{6} \;\text{g} = 7{,}500 \;\text{kg CO}_2
$$

**Step 3 — Comparative reduction vs. road baseline**

$$
\%\,\text{Reduction (rail)} = \dfrac{50{,}000 - 15{,}000}{50{,}000} \times 100\% = 70\%
$$

$$
\%\,\text{Reduction (sea)} = \dfrac{50{,}000 - 7{,}500}{50{,}000} \times 100\% = 85\%
$$

**Recommendation:** **Sea transport** is the greenest option (85% lower emissions than road), but for time-sensitive cargo, **rail** offers a balanced 70% reduction while remaining faster than maritime. **Road should be reserved** for last-mile, short-haul, and high-value deliveries where flexibility outweighs emission cost.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Do NOT confuse ton-km with kg-km** — always normalize cargo mass to tonnes before applying emission factors; this is the #1 mark-losing mistake.
> 2. **Always quote numerical units** explicitly (g CO₂/ton-km, NOT just "emissions"). Vague answers get zero in numerical parts.
> 3. **In reverse-logistics diagrams**, you must include the **feedback arrow** returning into manufacturing. Diagrams without the closed loop are marked incomplete.
> 4. **Formula substitution steps** must be shown — writing the final answer without derivation costs up to 50% of the marks.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST

- **Definition:** Green Logistics = low-emission, resource-efficient flow of goods + closed-loop recovery.
- **Two Branches:** Forward Logistics (origin → consumer) and Reverse Logistics (consumer → recovery).
- **7R Principle:** Return → Recycle → Reuse → Repair → Refurbish → Remanufacture → Redistribute.
- **Key Formula (High-Yield):** $E_{CO_2} = \dfrac{M_f \times EF_f \times 1000}{Q \cdot d}$.
- **Load Factor Improvement:** A linear increase in LF causes a proportional decrease in emission intensity.
- **Empty Running Penalty:** Doubles effective emission intensity per ton-km.
- **Modal Hierarchy (Clean → Dirty):** Sea < Rail < Inland Water < Road < Air.
- **Standard EF (Diesel):** **3.169 kg CO₂ / kg fuel** (IPCC default).
- **KPIs:** Load Factor %, Empty Running %, RRR (Reverse Recovery Rate), GPR (Green Packaging Ratio), WCI (Warehouse Carbon Index).
- **Green Warehouse Features:** Solar PV, LED + daylight sensors, HVAC heat recovery, rainwater harvesting, EV forklifts, WMS slotting.
- **Real-World Programs:** Caterpillar Reman, Amazon Renewed, Flipkart Reverse Pickup, ITC e-Choupal logistics, Mahindra's CERO vehicle-recycling.
- **Indian Policy Hooks:** EPR (Extended Producer Responsibility), Plastic Waste Management Rules, E-Waste Rules, Green Highways Policy.
- **Closed-Loop Rule:** Always show the feedback arrow from Collection Hub back to Manufacturer in any diagram — partial-loop diagrams are marked down.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Airports and Hotels
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET753 - GREEN MANUFACTURING
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Airports and Hotels

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Airports and Hotels in the Context of Green Manufacturing

### Formal Academic Definition
**Green Manufacturing in Airports and Hotels** refers to the holistic application of sustainable design principles, energy-efficient operations, waste minimization strategies, and eco-friendly material usage in the construction and operation of **aviation infrastructure (airports)** and **hospitality infrastructure (hotels)**. It extends the traditional manufacturing-based "Reduce–Reuse–Recycle" framework to the **service sector built environment**, integrating life-cycle assessment (LCA), renewable energy integration, and green building certification systems such as **LEED (Leadership in Energy and Environmental Design)** and **GRIHA (Green Rating for Integrated Habitat Assessment)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Context):**
> Green Manufacturing in the service sector emphasizes sustainable infrastructure planning where the "product" is the building itself, and the "manufacturing process" is its design, construction, and operational lifecycle.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Think of an airport or hotel like a **giant machine that is always running**:
- It **consumes energy 24×7** (lighting, HVAC, elevators, escalators, kitchens).
- It **uses enormous water** (guest rooms, laundry, landscaping, terminal washrooms).
- It **generates massive waste** (food waste, packaging, paper, e-waste, construction debris).

Just like a factory aims to reduce defects and waste in producing a car, a **green airport/hotel** aims to **reduce its "operational defects"** — wasteful energy, wasted water, and dumped waste. The runway is the *production line*, the terminal/hotel is the *factory floor*, and passengers/guests are the *customers*. Green manufacturing simply asks: *"Can we run this factory with cleaner energy, less water, and zero landfill?"*

### Standard Sustainability Metrics Used

| Metric | Symbol | Standard Value |
|---|---|---|
| Energy Use Intensity | **EUI** | 100–250 kWh/m²/year |
| Water Use Intensity | **WUI** | 50–150 litres/guest-night |
| Carbon Dioxide Emissions | **CO₂e** | <30 kg CO₂e/m²/year (Green Star) |
| Indoor Air Quality Index | **IAQ** | CO₂ < 800 ppm |
| Daylight Factor | **DF** | ≥ 2% (LEED requirement) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters for KTU exams:** Airports and hotels are **energy-intensive service hubs**. They account for nearly **8% of global commercial energy use** and represent one of the **fastest-growing segments** of sustainable infrastructure development globally.

### Scope of Green Practices in Airports vs Hotels

| Feature | Airports | Hotels |
|---|---|---|
| Primary Energy Load | HVAC, ground support equipment (GSE), runway lighting | HVAC, laundry, kitchens, guest room electricity |
| Water Consumption | Terminal facilities, runway wash-down, cooling towers | Linen laundry, swimming pools, landscaping |
| Waste Stream | Aircraft cabin waste, de-icing fluid, e-waste | Food waste, toiletries, packaging |
| Green Focus | Apron electrification, solar farms, rainwater harvesting | Smart rooms, key-card power cut-off, organic waste composting |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Framework: The 5 Pillars of Green Airport/Hotel Design

### Pillar 1 — Sustainable Site Selection & Planning
- Proximity to public transport to reduce private-vehicle emissions.
- Preservation of natural ecosystems (no construction on wetlands/farmland).
- **Brownfield development** preferred over greenfield (reuses contaminated/abandoned land).

### Pillar 2 — Energy Efficiency & Renewable Integration
- **Passive design**: orientation, insulation, double-glazed façades, cool roofs.
- **Active systems**: LED lighting (CFL → LED reduces load by **75%**), Variable Refrigerant Flow (VRF) HVAC, solar PV panels on rooftops.
- **Cogeneration (CHP)**: simultaneous production of electricity + heat (90% efficiency vs 35% grid).
- **Geothermal heat pumps**: ground-source energy for terminal/room HVAC.

### Pillar 3 — Water Conservation
- **Low-flow fixtures**: faucets (6 L/min), dual-flush toilets, aerated showerheads.
- **Rainwater harvesting** for landscape irrigation and toilet flushing.
- **Greywater recycling** — reusing shower/laundry water for gardening.
- **Condensate recovery** from HVAC for cooling tower makeup water.

### Pillar 4 — Material Selection & Indoor Environmental Quality
- **Locally sourced materials** (within 500 km radius — LEED credit).
- **Rapidly renewable materials**: bamboo, cork, recycled steel, low-VOC paints.
- **Daylight harvesting**: occupancy + lux sensors reduce artificial lighting by 40%.

### Pillar 5 — Waste Management Hierarchy
Adopted in the order: **Prevent → Minimize → Reuse → Recycle → Recover → Dispose**.
- Source segregation at guest rooms, kitchens, and terminal bins.
- Organic waste converted via **biogas digesters** or **composting units**.
- Construction & Demolition (C&D) waste target: divert **≥75%** from landfill.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Principle:**
> The **waste management hierarchy** is a 6-step pyramid. Examiners love testing whether students can place examples (e.g., composting = Recycling, incineration with energy recovery = Recovery, landfill = Disposal) in the correct layer.

## 2.2 Green Certification Systems (KTU Mandatory Knowledge)

| Certification | Origin | Focus | Rating Scale |
|---|---|---|---|
| **LEED** (Leadership in Energy and Environmental Design) | USA (USGBC) | New construction + existing buildings | Certified → Silver → Gold → Platinum |
| **GRIHA** | India (TERI) | Indian climate-specific | 1 star (≥50 pts) → 5 stars (≥90 pts) |
| **BREEAM** | UK | European holistic sustainability | Pass → Good → Very Good → Excellent → Outstanding |
| **Green Star** | Australia (GBCA) | Operational + design | 4 Star → 5 Star → 6 Star |
| **EDGE** (Excellence in Design for Greater Efficiencies) | IFC/World Bank | Emerging markets | Certified (≥20% energy, water, embodied energy savings) |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula / Equation | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|
| $EUI = \dfrac{E_{total}}{A_{floor}}$ | Energy Use Intensity — total annual energy ÷ floor area | kWh/m²/year |
| $WUI = \dfrac{V_{water}}{N_{guests} \times D_{nights}}$ | Water Use Intensity per guest-night | L/guest-night |
| $CO_{2e} = E_{grid} \times EF_{grid}$ | Grid emission factor (India avg ≈ **0.716 kg CO₂e/kWh**) | kg CO₂e |
| $\eta_{CHP} = \dfrac{Q_{useful} + W_{electrical}}{Q_{fuel}}$ | Combined Heat & Power efficiency | dimensionless (%) |
| $\eta_{PV} = \dfrac{P_{out}}{G \times A} \times 100\%$ | Solar PV conversion efficiency (mono-Si ≈ **18–22%**) | dimensionless (%) |
| $S_{rainwater} = R \times A_{roof} \times C_{runoff}$ | Annual rainwater harvestable volume | m³/year |
| $\Delta T_{cool} = \dfrac{Q_{load}}{\dot{m} \times c_p}$ | Cooling tower temperature drop | °C |
| $C_{divert} = \dfrac{W_{recycled} + W_{reused}}{W_{total}} \times 100\%$ | Waste diversion rate from landfill | % |

> [!NOTE]
> **Important constants to memorize:**
> - Grid emission factor (India) ≈ **0.716 kg CO₂e/kWh** (CEA 2023 data).
> - 1 kWh = **3.6 MJ**.
> - Solar constant ≈ **1367 W/m²** (AM 1.5 standard test condition).

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Cochin International Airport (CIAL), Kerala** — world's first fully solar-powered airport (12 MWp PV plant; saves 300,000 tonnes of CO₂ over 25 years).
- **ITC Grand Chola, Chennai** — India's largest LEED Platinum certified hotel.
- **Hyatt Regency** chain globally uses key-card energy management (cuts room energy by **25–40%**).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Annual Energy & Carbon Audit of a Mid-Size Hotel

### Given Problem Statement
A 4-star hotel has the following annual operational data:
- Total floor area $A_{floor}$ = **25,000 m²**
- Annual electricity consumption $E_{grid}$ = **5,000,000 kWh**
- Annual LPG consumption for kitchen/boiler = **50,000 kg**
- Calorific value of LPG ≈ **46 MJ/kg**
- Grid emission factor $EF_{grid}$ = **0.716 kg CO₂e/kWh**
- Emission factor of LPG = **3.0 kg CO₂e/kg** (combustion factor)

**Required:**
(a) Compute the Energy Use Intensity (EUI).
(b) Compute the annual CO₂ equivalent emissions.
(c) Compare with benchmark and suggest 2 green retrofits.

---

### Part (a): Energy Use Intensity Calculation

**Step 1 — Identify the formula from the KTU formula sheet:**

$$EUI = \dfrac{E_{total}}{A_{floor}}$$

**Step 2 — Convert LPG energy to kWh equivalent (since EUI must be in common units):**

Given: LPG mass = 50,000 kg, Calorific value $CV_{LPG}$ = 46 MJ/kg.

$$E_{LPG} = m_{LPG} \times CV_{LPG}$$

$$E_{LPG} = 50{,}000 \text{ kg} \times 46 \text{ MJ/kg}$$

$$E_{LPG} = 2{,}300{,}000 \text{ MJ}$$

Convert MJ → kWh using the constant **1 kWh = 3.6 MJ**:

$$E_{LPG (kWh)} = \dfrac{2{,}300{,}000 \text{ MJ}}{3.6 \text{ MJ/kWh}}$$

$$E_{LPG (kWh)} \approx 638{,}889 \text{ kWh}$$

**Step 3 — Sum total energy in kWh:**

$$E_{total} = E_{grid} + E_{LPG(kWh)}$$

$$E_{total} = 5{,}000{,}000 + 638{,}889$$

$$E_{total} = 5{,}638{,}889 \text{ kWh}$$

**Step 4 — Compute EUI:**

$$EUI = \dfrac{5{,}638{,}889 \text{ kWh}}{25{,}000 \text{ m}^2}$$

$$\boxed{EUI \approx 225.6 \text{ kWh/m}^2/\text{year}}$$

---

### Part (b): Annual CO₂ Equivalent Emissions

**Step 1 — Grid-related emissions:**

$$CO_{2e,grid} = E_{grid} \times EF_{grid}$$

$$CO_{2e,grid} = 5{,}000{,}000 \times 0.716$$

$$CO_{2e,grid} = 3{,}580{,}000 \text{ kg CO}_2\text{e} \approx 3{,}580 \text{ tonnes}$$

**Step 2 — LPG-related emissions:**

$$CO_{2e,LPG} = m_{LPG} \times EF_{LPG}$$

$$CO_{2e,LPG} = 50{,}000 \times 3.0 = 150{,}000 \text{ kg} = 150 \text{ tonnes}$$

**Step 3 — Total annual emissions:**

$$CO_{2e,total} = 3{,}580 + 150$$

$$\boxed{CO_{2e,total} = 3{,}730 \text{ tonnes/year}}$$

---

### Part (c): Benchmarking & Retrofit Recommendations

**Benchmark Comparison:**

| Benchmark | EUI (kWh/m²/yr) | Status |
|---|---|---|
| Best-in-class Green Hotel | < 100 | 🟢 Excellent |
| LEED Gold Target | 100–150 | 🟡 Good |
| Industry Average | 200–250 | 🟠 Acceptable |
| **Our Hotel (Calculated)** | **225.6** | 🟠 Average |

**Retrofit Recommendation 1 — LED + Occupancy Sensor Retrofit:**
Replace all incandescent/CFL bulbs with LEDs (75% reduction in lighting load). Assume lighting = 20% of grid electricity:

$$\text{Savings} = 0.20 \times 5{,}000{,}000 \times 0.75 = 750{,}000 \text{ kWh/year}$$

$$\text{CO}_2 \text{ avoided} = 750{,}000 \times 0.716 = 537 \text{ tonnes/year}$$

**Retrofit Recommendation 2 — Solar PV Rooftop Installation:**
Assume 2000 m² of usable rooftop, with $\eta_{PV} = 18\%$ and average daily solar insolation in Kerala = **5 kWh/m²/day**:

$$E_{PV} = \eta_{PV} \times G \times A \times 365$$

$$E_{PV} = 0.18 \times 5 \times 2000 \times 365$$

$$E_{PV} = 657{,}000 \text{ kWh/year}$$

This offsets **~13%** of grid electricity → **471 tonnes CO₂/year** avoided.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Rainwater Harvesting Sizing for an Airport Terminal

### Given Problem Statement
A regional airport terminal has:
- Roof catchment area $A_{roof}$ = **15,000 m²**
- Annual rainfall in the region $R$ = **2400 mm/year**
- Runoff coefficient $C_{runoff}$ = **0.85** (concrete/metal roof)

**Required:**
(a) Compute annual harvestable rainwater volume.
(b) Determine if this can offset toilet flushing demand of **50,000 litres/day** for the terminal.

---

### Part (a): Annual Harvested Volume

**Step 1 — Apply the rainfall-runoff formula:**

$$S_{rainwater} = R \times A_{roof} \times C_{runoff}$$

**Step 2 — Convert rainfall from mm to m** (since area is in m²):

$$R = 2400 \text{ mm} = 2.4 \text{ m}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute:**

$$S_{rainwater} = 2.4 \times 15{,}000 \times 0.85$$

$$\boxed{S_{rainwater} = 30{,}600 \text{ m}^3/\text{year} = 30.6 \text{ million litres/year}}$$

---

### Part (b): Daily Demand vs Daily Supply

**Step 1 — Daily demand:**

$$D_{daily} = 50{,}000 \text{ L/day} = 50 \text{ m}^3/\text{day}$$

**Step 2 — Average daily supply (assuming uniform rainfall over 365 days):**

$$S_{daily} = \dfrac{30{,}600}{365}$$

$$S_{daily} \approx 83.8 \text{ m}^3/\text{day}$$

**Step 3 — Compare:**

Since $S_{daily} = 83.8 \text{ m}^3/\text{day} > D_{daily} = 50 \text{ m}^3/\text{day}$, **the harvested rainwater can fully meet the toilet flushing demand**, with a surplus of **~33.8 m³/day**.

**Excess calculation:**

$$\text{Excess} = 83.8 - 50.0 = 33.8 \text{ m}^3/\text{day} \approx 12{,}337 \text{ m}^3/\text{year}$$

This surplus can be redirected to **landscape irrigation** or **cooling tower makeup water**.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Common Exam Traps

| Mistake Students Make | Correct Approach |
|---|---|
| Mixing units (MJ with kWh) | Always convert to a **common unit** (kWh) before arithmetic |
| Forgetting to multiply by 365 for annual solar yield | Use $E_{PV} = \eta \times G \times A \times 365$ |
| Using 1.0 instead of runoff coefficient | Apply $C_{runoff} < 1$ for realistic losses |
| Confusing CHP efficiency with electrical efficiency | CHP = electrical + thermal output combined |
| Writing "EUI = total energy" without dividing by area | EUI is **always normalized per m²** |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Green Hotel Ecosystem

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Guest Arrival Check In] --> B[Smart Key Card Activates Room Power]
    B --> C[Occupancy Sensor Detects Presence]
    C --> D[Lighting HVAC Adjusted]
    D --> E[Real Time Energy Dashboard Display]
    E --> F[Central Building Management System BMS]
    F --> G[Data Logged for LEED Reporting]

    subgraph Energy Flow
        H[Roof Top Solar PV 250 kWp] --> I[DC to AC Inverter]
        I --> J[Hotel Internal Grid]
        K[Grid Electricity Backup] --> J
        J --> L[Rooms Kitchens Laundry HVAC]
    end

    subgraph Water Flow
        M[Rainwater Harvest Tank] --> N[Toilet Flush Irrigation]
        O[Greywater from Showers] --> P[Reed Bed Filtration]
        P --> Q[Recycled Water for Cooling Tower]
    end

    subgraph Waste Flow
```

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1
**Q: Define the term "Energy Use Intensity (EUI)" and state its typical benchmark value for a green hotel.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]`

**Model Answer:**
EUI is the ratio of total annual energy consumption of a building to its total floor area, expressed in **kWh/m²/year**. It is a key performance indicator used to benchmark building energy performance. The benchmark value for a **green hotel (LEED Gold)** is typically **< 150 kWh/m²/year**, while best-in-class sustainable hotels achieve **< 100 kWh/m²/year**.

---

### Question A2
**Q: List any three sustainable practices adopted by airports to reduce their carbon footprint.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]`

**Model Answer:**
1. **Rooftop solar PV installation** (e.g., Cochin International Airport — 12 MWp).
2. **Apron electrification** to allow aircraft to switch off Auxiliary Power Units (APUs).
3. **LED runway and taxiway lighting** replacing halogen fixtures.
4. *(Any three of the above, or alternatives like rainwater harvesting, electric GSE, biodiesel blends.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Question — Internal Choice Format (14 Marks)

### Question A (14 Marks)
**Q: A 200-room resort hotel located in Kerala has the following operational data:**
- Floor area: 40,000 m²
- Annual electricity: 8,500,000 kWh
- Annual diesel for boiler: 80,000 litres (density = 0.85 kg/L, calorific value = 43 MJ/kg)
- Grid emission factor: 0.716 kg CO₂e/kWh
- Diesel emission factor: 2.68 kg CO₂e/kg

**Compute:**
**(a)** Energy Use Intensity (EUI) of the hotel in kWh/m²/year.
**(b)** Total annual CO₂ equivalent emissions in tonnes/year.
**(c)** Suggest three green retrofits to bring EUI below 150 kWh/m²/year.

`[KTU University Exam – Model Paper Pattern, 2024 Scheme]`

---

### Model Solution — Question A

#### Part (a): EUI Calculation — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — Convert diesel energy to kWh:**

Mass of diesel:
$$m_{diesel} = 80{,}000 \text{ L} \times 0.85 \text{ kg/L} = 68{,}000 \text{ kg}$$

Energy from diesel in MJ:
$$E_{diesel} = 68{,}000 \times 43 = 2{,}924{,}000 \text{ MJ}$$

Convert to kWh:
$$E_{diesel(kWh)} = \dfrac{2{,}924{,}000}{3.6} \approx 812{,}222 \text{ kWh}$$

**Step 2 — Total annual energy:**

$$E_{total} = 8{,}500{,}000 + 812{,}222 = 9{,}312{,}222 \text{ kWh}$$

**Step 3 — EUI:**

$$EUI = \dfrac{9{,}312{,}222}{40{,}000}$$

$$\boxed{EUI \approx 232.8 \text{ kWh/m}^2/\text{year}}$$

**Comparison:** EUI > 150 → Hotel is **not green-certified**.

---

#### Part (b): Annual CO₂ Emissions — 7 Marks

**Grid CO₂:**
$$CO_{2e,grid} = 8{,}500{,}000 \times 0.716 = 6{,}086{,}000 \text{ kg} = 6{,}086 \text{ tonnes}$$

**Diesel CO₂:**
$$CO_{2e,diesel} = 68{,}000 \times 2.68 = 182{,}240 \text{ kg} \approx 182 \text{ tonnes}$$

**Total:**
$$\boxed{CO_{2e,total} = 6{,}268 \text{ tonnes/year}}$$

---

#### Part (c): Three Green Retrofits (counted in part b) — 7 Marks Total

1. **Solar PV Rooftop (500 kWp)** — saves ~750,000 kWh/year.
2. **LED + Occupancy Sensors** — saves ~1,000,000 kWh/year (12% of grid).
3. **Heat Recovery from Boiler Flue Gas** — saves ~10% diesel.
4. **Variable Frequency Drives on HVAC motors.**
5. **Biodiesel B20 blend** replacing diesel.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**Q: With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the integrated green practices adopted in a sustainable airport. Discuss the role of:**
**(a)** Renewable energy systems and rainwater harvesting in airport sustainability.
**(b)** Waste management and green certification systems (LEED/GRIHA) in airport infrastructure.

`[KTU University Exam – Model Paper Pattern, 2024 Scheme]`

---

### Model Solution — Question B

#### Part (a): Renewable Energy & Rainwater Harvesting — 7 Marks

**Renewable Energy Systems:**
- **Rooftop and canopy-mounted solar PV plants** (e.g., CIAL — 12 MWp).
- **Solar thermal collectors** for hot water generation in terminal washrooms.
- **Wind-solar hybrid systems** at coastal airports (e.g., Trivandrum).
- **Biogas plants** for organic waste from in-flight catering and terminal restaurants.
- **Geothermal heat pumps** for terminal HVAC.

**Rainwater Harvesting:**
- Roof catchment from terminal building + parking sheds.
- Recharge pits to replenish groundwater table.
- Storage tanks for non-potable uses: toilet flush, landscaping, runway wash-down, cooling tower make-up water.
- Formula: $S_{rainwater} = R \times A_{roof} \times C_{runoff}$.

#### Part (b): Waste Management & Green Certification — 7 Marks

**Waste Management Hierarchy (Prevent → Reduce → Reuse → Recycle → Recover → Dispose):**
- **Source segregation** at terminal gates, lounges, food courts.
- **Composting / biogas digesters** for organic waste (in-flight meal leftovers).
- **Hazardous waste handling** for de-icing fluid, aviation fuel spills, e-waste from X-ray machines.
- **Construction & Demolition (C&D) waste** — target ≥75% diversion from landfill.

**Green Certification Systems:**
- **LEED v4.1 BD+C** (Building Design + Construction) for terminals.
- **GRIHA** for Indian airports — climate-specific credits for monsoon performance.
- Certification drives: water savings, energy efficiency, indoor air quality, sustainable materials.
- Recognition benefits: brand value, lower operating costs, passenger goodwill.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Unit inconsistency** — Mixing MJ with kWh without conversion → lose 2 marks.
> 2. **Skipping unit normalization** — Writing "EUI = 9,312,222" without dividing by 40,000 → lose 3 marks.
> 3. **Forgetting the runoff coefficient** — In rainwater problems, using C = 1 instead of 0.7–0.9 → lose 1–2 marks.
> 4. **Confusing emission factors** — Grid EF (0.716) ≠ Diesel EF (2.68) ≠ LPG EF (3.0) — using the wrong one → lose 2 marks.
> 5. **Listing waste management without hierarchy order** — Examiners expect **Prevent → Reduce → Reuse → Recycle → Recover → Dispose** in that exact order → lose 1 mark.
> 6. **Generic answers in 14-mark questions** — Always include **numerical computation + diagram + comparison + recommendation** for full marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

### 🎯 Core Definitions to Memorize
- ✅ **Green Manufacturing** = sustainable production across lifecycle.
- ✅ **EUI** = total annual energy ÷ floor area (kWh/m²/yr).
- ✅ **WUI** = water used per guest-night.
- ✅ **LEED** = US-based green building rating.
- ✅ **GRIHA** = India's indigenous green rating (1★ to 5★).
- ✅ **CHP / Cogeneration** = simultaneous electricity + heat (90% efficient).

### 🎯 Formulas to Memorize
- ✅ $EUI = E_{total} / A_{floor}$
- ✅ $CO_{2e} = E_{grid} \times 0.716$ kg CO₂e/kWh
- ✅ $E_{LPG(kWh)} = (m \times CV) / 3.6$
- ✅ $S_{rainwater} = R \times A_{roof} \times C_{runoff}$
- ✅ $C_{divert} = (W_{recycled} + W_{reused}) / W_{total} \times 100\%$

### 🎯 The Six Pillars of Green Airport/Hotel (Quick Recall)
1. Sustainable site planning
2. Energy efficiency + renewables
3. Water conservation (rainwater + greywater)
4. Sustainable materials + IEQ
5. Waste management hierarchy (6 levels)
6. Green certification (LEED / GRIHA / BREEAM)

### 🎯 Numerical Constants (Don't Forget!)
- ✅ Grid EF (India) = **0.716 kg CO₂e/kWh**
- ✅ 1 kWh = **3.6 MJ**
- ✅ Solar PV efficiency = **18–22%**
- ✅ Kerala avg solar insolation ≈ **5 kWh/m²/day**
- ✅ Typical run-off coefficient (concrete roof) = **0.80–0.90**
- ✅ CHP efficiency = **75–90%**
- ✅ LED energy savings over CFL = **75%**

### 🎯 Real-World Case Studies (Mention in Exams for Bonus Marks)
- ✅ **CIAL (Cochin)** — World's first fully solar-powered airport.
- ✅ **ITC Grand Chola** — India's largest LEED Platinum hotel.
- ✅ **Hyatt** — Key-card energy management system.
- ✅ **Changi Airport, Singapore** — Skytrain + indoor gardens + LEED Gold.

1. **Introduction** (2 marks) — definition + scope.
2. **Block diagram** (2 marks) — show flow/process.
3. **Numerical/schematic details** (6 marks) — formulas + calculations.
4. **Real-world case study or example** (2 marks).
5. **Conclusion + recommendations** (2 marks).
